cases.title: An Elderly Man With Days of Vomiting and Dizziness
cases.chiefComplaint: Lightheadedness and decreased urination after several days of vomiting and diarrhea.
cases.history: A 72-year-old man presents to the ED with 4 days of profuse watery diarrhea and vomiting that began after a family barbecue where others also fell ill. He has been unable to keep down liquids and reports feeling dizzy when standing. He notes his urine has become dark and scant over the past day. He denies abdominal pain, fever, blood in the stool, dysuria, or flank pain. Past medical history includes hypertension and osteoarthritis. Medications are lisinopril 20 mg daily, hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily, and ibuprofen 400 mg as needed for knee pain, which he has taken more frequently this week. He has no known drug allergies. He lives independently with his wife, drinks alcohol socially, and quit smoking 20 years ago. Family history is notable for hypertension and type 2 diabetes. He has no history of kidney disease, and a basic metabolic panel from a clinic visit 2 months ago was reportedly normal.
cases.exam: The patient appears tired and mildly ill. Mucous membranes are dry and skin turgor is decreased. Blood pressure drops to 84/54 mmHg with a heart rate of 124 bpm on standing, reproducing his lightheadedness. The neck veins are flat. The lungs are clear and the heart is tachycardic with a regular rhythm and no murmurs. The abdomen is soft and nontender with hyperactive bowel sounds and no costovertebral angle tenderness. There is no peripheral edema. He is alert and oriented with no asterixis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on the history and exam, what is the most appropriate immediate intervention?
options: Start a loop diuretic to treat oliguria as the first step today
Obtain a renal biopsy before any fluid resuscitation as the first step today
Begin empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics for presumed sepsis
Administer intravenous isotonic crystalloid
stages.explanation: This patient has classic clinical volume depletion (orthostasis, dry mucous membranes, flat neck veins, tachycardia) from GI losses, compounded by nephrotoxic and hemodynamically active medications. IV isotonic fluid restores perfusion while stopping the ACE inhibitor (impairs autoregulation of efferent tone), diuretic (worsens volume loss), and NSAID (impairs afferent vasodilation). A loop diuretic is wrong and harmful in a volume-depleted patient. A biopsy is unnecessary and premature for a clinically obvious prerenal picture. Antibiotics are not indicated: he is afebrile with a self-limited foodborne illness and no signs of sepsis or a urinary source.

stages.label: LABS & URINE STUDIES
stages.content:
Na 148 mEq/L
K 3.6 mEq/L
Cl 108 mEq/L
HCO3 22 mEq/L
BUN 56 mg/dL
Creatinine 2.4 mg/dL (baseline 1.0)
BUN:Cr ratio 23
Urine Na 12 mEq/L
Fractional excretion of sodium (FENa) 0.4%
Urine osmolality 680 mOsm/kg
Urinalysis: SG 1.028, no blood, no protein, occasional hyaline casts

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which laboratory pattern best supports a prerenal etiology of this patient's acute kidney injury?
options: Urine sodium 60 mEq/L with isosthenuric urine
FENa 0.4% with urine osmolality 680 mOsm/kg
Red cell casts with dysmorphic erythrocytes on microscopy
FENa 3% with muddy brown granular casts in this clinical context
stages.explanation: In prerenal AKI the tubules are intact and avidly reabsorb sodium and water in response to hypoperfusion, producing a low FENa (<1%), low urine sodium (<20 mEq/L), concentrated urine (osmolality >500 mOsm/kg), and a disproportionately elevated BUN:Cr ratio (>20) from enhanced urea reabsorption. A FENa >2% with muddy brown granular casts indicates acute tubular necrosis, not prerenal disease. A high urine sodium with isosthenuria reflects loss of tubular concentrating ability seen in ATN. Red cell casts with dysmorphic RBCs indicate glomerulonephritis, an intrinsic process.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After 2 L of normal saline his blood pressure is 118/74 mmHg, heart rate is 84 bpm, and he has voided 400 mL. What is the best next step?
options: Discharge immediately with no further monitoring as initial therapy
Restart the ACE inhibitor and NSAID now that pressure has normalized
Initiate hemodialysis for the elevated BUN as initial therapy
Continue IV fluids guided by volume status
stages.explanation: His hemodynamic response and urine output confirm reversible prerenal physiology, so the correct course is continued volume repletion titrated to clinical status with serial labs to confirm the creatinine trends down. Immediate discharge is unsafe before AKI resolution and oral tolerance are demonstrated. Dialysis is not indicated: there is no refractory hyperkalemia, acidosis, volume overload, uremic complication, or toxic ingestion. Restarting the ACE inhibitor and NSAID now risks blunting recovery; the NSAID should be avoided and the ACE inhibitor resumed only after renal function and volume status have normalized.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which counseling point is most important to prevent recurrence of his kidney injury?
options: He requires lifelong dialysis access placement with appropriate follow-up
He should restrict fluid intake to prevent future episodes based on current risk
He should avoid NSAIDs
He should permanently avoid all antihypertensive medications based on current risk
stages.explanation: The combination of an NSAID (afferent constriction), ACE inhibitor (efferent dilation), and diuretic (volume loss) — the so-called triple whammy — markedly raises the risk of AKI during dehydrating illness. Counseling him to avoid NSAIDs and to hold the ACE inhibitor and diuretic when ill with vomiting or diarrhea is the highest-yield preventive measure. Permanently stopping all antihypertensives is unnecessary and risks uncontrolled blood pressure. Fluid restriction would worsen, not prevent, prerenal injury. He does not need dialysis access, as his injury is reversible.

cases.diagnosis: Prerenal acute kidney injury from volume depletion (gastroenteritis) potentiated by ACE inhibitor, diuretic, and NSAID use
pearls: Prerenal AKI shows FENa <1%, urine Na <20 mEq/L, urine osmolality >500 mOsm/kg, and BUN:Cr ratio >20 — hallmarks of intact tubules reclaiming salt and water.
The triple whammy (NSAID + ACE inhibitor/ARB + diuretic) is a classic precipitant of AKI in dehydrated elderly patients.
FENa is most useful when the patient is oliguric and has not received diuretics; loop diuretics invalidate it (use FEurea instead).
Prerenal azotemia is fully reversible with restoration of renal perfusion if treated before it progresses to ischemic ATN.

cases.title: Falling Urine Output After a Long Operation
cases.chiefComplaint: Rising creatinine and low urine output noted on postoperative day 2.
cases.history: A 64-year-old woman is on the surgical ward recovering from an emergency open repair of a ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm performed 2 days ago. The operation was complicated by major hemorrhage; intraoperatively her systolic blood pressure fell below 70 mmHg for nearly an hour before she was stabilized with transfusion and vasopressors. Since surgery her urine output has progressively declined to less than 0.3 mL/kg/hr despite adequate fluid resuscitation, and her creatinine has risen each day. She received perioperative cefazolin and a single dose of gentamicin. Past medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a 40-pack-year smoking history. Outpatient medications are amlodipine and atorvastatin; she has no known drug allergies. She does not use NSAIDs. There was no iodinated contrast administered. Family history is noncontributory. Her preoperative creatinine was 0.9 mg/dL. She denies flank pain. She is now hemodynamically stable on the ward with no further bleeding.
cases.exam: The patient is alert and resting comfortably. Mucous membranes are moist and jugular venous pressure is normal to slightly elevated. The lungs have mild bibasilar crackles. The heart is regular with no murmurs. The abdominal incision is clean and dry without surrounding erythema; the abdomen is mildly distended but soft with appropriate postoperative tenderness and no costovertebral angle tenderness. There is trace pedal edema. The Foley catheter drains a small amount of dark urine. There is no rash and no asterixis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given her clinical course, what is the most likely cause of her acute kidney injury?
options: Acute interstitial nephritis from cefazolin as the first step today
Ischemic acute tubular necrosis from prolonged intraoperative hypotension
Prerenal azotemia from ongoing volume depletion as the first step today
Postrenal obstruction from a blocked Foley catheter in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Sustained intraoperative hypotension producing renal hypoperfusion is the prototypical cause of ischemic acute tubular necrosis, and her oliguria persists despite restoration of volume and pressure — the hallmark distinguishing established ATN from reversible prerenal disease. Pure prerenal azotemia would be expected to improve once perfusion is restored, which has not happened. A blocked Foley is possible but she is producing urine and the catheter is draining, making obstruction unlikely. Acute interstitial nephritis from a beta-lactam typically presents over days to weeks, often with rash, fever, or eosinophilia, and does not fit this acute ischemic timeline.

stages.label: LABS & URINE MICROSCOPY
stages.content:
Na 139 mEq/L
K 5.1 mEq/L
HCO3 19 mEq/L
BUN 48 mg/dL
Creatinine 3.6 mg/dL (baseline 0.9)
BUN:Cr ratio 13
Urine Na 58 mEq/L
Fractional excretion of sodium (FENa) 3.2%
Urine osmolality 295 mOsm/kg
Urinalysis: SG 1.010, trace protein, no nitrites
Urine microscopy: numerous muddy brown granular casts and renal tubular epithelial cells
Renal ultrasound: normal-sized kidneys, no hydronephrosis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding on the urine studies most strongly confirms acute tubular necrosis rather than a prerenal state?
options: Urine osmolality of 295 mOsm/kg in this clinical context
Trace proteinuria on dipstick in this clinical context
Specific gravity of 1.010 as the primary diagnosis
Muddy brown granular casts with FENa 3.2%
stages.explanation: Muddy brown (pigmented) granular casts together with a high FENa (>2%) and elevated urine sodium (>40 mEq/L) reflect tubular injury with loss of sodium-reabsorbing and concentrating capacity — the diagnostic signature of ATN. While isosthenuria (urine osmolality near plasma, ~300 mOsm/kg) and a specific gravity of 1.010 also indicate impaired concentration, they are nonspecific and supportive rather than confirmatory. Trace proteinuria is a minor, nonspecific finding. The casts plus the elevated FENa together constitute the strongest evidence distinguishing intrinsic ATN from a prerenal process where the tubules avidly retain sodium.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of management for her established ischemic ATN?
options: Supportive care: maintain euvolemia
Low-dose dopamine to enhance renal blood flow as initial therapy
Empiric pulse-dose corticosteroids as initial therapy
High-dose loop diuretic infusion to convert oliguric to nonoliguric failure and improve outcomes
stages.explanation: ATN has no specific reversal therapy; management is supportive while the tubules regenerate over days to weeks. This means optimizing volume status and renal perfusion, removing or avoiding nephrotoxins (including the gentamicin), renally dosing medications, and monitoring for electrolyte and acid-base disturbances and indications for dialysis. Loop diuretics may aid volume control but do not improve renal recovery or mortality and should not be used solely to increase urine output. Low-dose dopamine has been shown not to prevent or treat AKI and can cause harm. Corticosteroids are appropriate for allergic interstitial nephritis, not ischemic ATN.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: On the next morning she is somnolent; potassium is 6.8 mEq/L with peaked T waves and a widened QRS on ECG, and she remains anuric with a bicarbonate of 12 mEq/L. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Administer IV calcium gluconate, insulin with dextrose,
Give oral sodium polystyrene sulfonate and recheck in 6 hours
Increase IV fluids to flush out the potassium as the leading concern
Start a thiazide diuretic as the leading concern
stages.explanation: She has life-threatening hyperkalemia with ECG changes plus severe refractory metabolic acidosis in an anuric patient — clear indications for emergent dialysis. Immediate measures are IV calcium gluconate to stabilize the myocardium and insulin with dextrose to shift potassium intracellularly while hemodialysis is mobilized for definitive removal. Oral binders work too slowly and unreliably for an emergency. Increasing fluids will not lower potassium in an anuric patient and risks volume overload. Thiazides are weak and ineffective at this level of renal failure. The combination of refractory hyperkalemia and acidosis with anuria meets standard indications for renal replacement therapy.

cases.diagnosis: Ischemic acute tubular necrosis following prolonged perioperative hypotension
pearls: Oliguria that persists after restoration of volume and blood pressure points to established ATN rather than reversible prerenal azotemia.
Muddy brown granular casts plus FENa >2% and urine osmolality near 300 mOsm/kg are the classic urinary signature of ATN.
ATN management is supportive — euvolemia, perfusion, nephrotoxin avoidance, renal dosing; dopamine and diuretics do not improve renal recovery.
Emergent dialysis indications (AEIOU): refractory Acidosis, Electrolytes (hyperkalemia), Ingestions, Overload, Uremia.

cases.title: Worsening Kidney Numbers After a Heart Procedure
cases.chiefComplaint: A rising creatinine discovered on routine labs after coronary angiography.
cases.history: A 68-year-old man remains hospitalized after presenting with an NSTEMI 3 days ago and undergoing coronary angiography with drug-eluting stent placement to the right coronary artery, which required a substantial volume of iodinated contrast. He feels well and is ambulating without chest pain. Routine morning labs today (about 60 hours after the procedure) show his creatinine has climbed from a baseline of 1.3 mg/dL to 2.1 mg/dL. His urine output remains adequate. Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus for 15 years, hypertension, and chronic kidney disease. Medications include metformin (held since admission), insulin glargine, aspirin, ticagrelor, atorvastatin, lisinopril, and metoprolol; he has no known drug allergies. He took no NSAIDs. He is a former smoker. Family history includes diabetes and coronary disease. He was adequately hydrated periprocedurally. There has been no hypotension, no new nephrotoxic antibiotics, and no episodes of acute hemodynamic instability since the catheterization.
cases.exam: The patient is comfortable and in no distress. Mucous membranes are moist and jugular venous pressure is normal. The lungs are clear bilaterally. The heart is regular with no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. The right femoral access site is clean and dry with no hematoma, bruit, or tenderness. The abdomen is soft and nontender. There is no flank tenderness and no peripheral edema. Importantly, there are no livedo reticularis, no blue toes, and no retinal cholesterol emboli. He is alert and oriented with no asterixis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the timing and clinical picture, what is the most likely cause of his acute kidney injury?
options: Atheroembolic (cholesterol embolization) renal disease
Prerenal azotemia from heart failure
Contrast-associated acute kidney injury
Acute interstitial nephritis from atorvastatin
stages.explanation: A creatinine rise beginning 48-72 hours after iodinated contrast, peaking around days 3-5 and typically resolving within 1-2 weeks, in a high-risk patient with diabetes and pre-existing CKD is the classic course of contrast-associated AKI. Atheroembolic disease can follow arterial catheterization but usually appears over weeks, often with livedo reticularis, blue toes, eosinophilia, and hypocomplementemia — none present here. Statin-induced interstitial nephritis is uncommon and would typically show pyuria, eosinophiluria, or rash. There are no clinical signs of heart failure or volume depletion to support a prerenal cause.

stages.label: LABS & STUDIES
stages.content:
Na 140 mEq/L
K 4.4 mEq/L
HCO3 23 mEq/L
BUN 32 mg/dL
Creatinine 2.1 mg/dL (baseline 1.3)
Urine Na 18 mEq/L
Fractional excretion of sodium (FENa) 0.8%
Urinalysis: SG 1.018, trace protein, no blood, occasional granular casts
Complete blood count: WBC 7.8 ×10³/µL, eosinophils 2%
C3 and C4 normal
Renal ultrasound: normal-sized kidneys, no hydronephrosis

stages.label: INTERPRETATION
stages.question: The labs show a low FENa, no eosinophilia, and normal complement. How do these findings help confirm the diagnosis?
options: They indicate a glomerulonephritis with low complement as the immediate next step
They confirm atheroembolic disease, which typically causes a low FENa before discharge planning
They are consistent with contrast nephropathy, which often produces a low FENa
They establish acute interstitial nephritis as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Contrast-associated AKI is mediated by renal medullary ischemia and direct tubular toxicity but characteristically presents with a relatively benign sediment and frequently a low FENa (often <1%) early in its course, distinguishing it from typical ATN. The absence of peripheral eosinophilia and the normal complement argue strongly against atheroembolic disease, which classically causes eosinophilia and low complement, and against an immune-complex glomerulonephritis. The lack of pyuria, eosinophiluria, rash, or fever makes acute interstitial nephritis unlikely. Together these findings fit the expected profile of contrast nephropathy in a diabetic with CKD.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management of his current kidney injury?
options: Start high-dose N-acetylcysteine and IV sodium bicarbonate to reverse the injury
Provide supportive care with adequate hydration, avoid further nephrotoxins,
Begin corticosteroids as initial therapy
Initiate prophylactic hemodialysis to clear the contrast as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Contrast-associated AKI is usually self-limited; management is supportive — maintaining euvolemia, avoiding additional nephrotoxic exposures, holding metformin until function recovers, and trending creatinine, which typically returns toward baseline within 1-2 weeks. N-acetylcysteine and post-procedure bicarbonate have not been shown to treat established contrast nephropathy and are not recommended. Prophylactic dialysis does not prevent or improve the injury and is not indicated absent standard dialysis criteria. Corticosteroids treat allergic interstitial nephritis or certain glomerulonephritides, neither of which is present here.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: If this patient needs another contrast study in the future, which strategy is best supported for reducing his risk of contrast-associated AKI?
options: Mannitol diuresis immediately before the study as the immediate next step
Periprocedural IV isotonic crystalloid hydration
Furosemide before and after contrast to maintain urine flow in this setting
Routine prophylactic N-acetylcysteine as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Volume expansion with isotonic saline before and after contrast plus minimizing the contrast dose (and using iso- or low-osmolar agents) are the best-supported measures to reduce contrast-associated AKI in high-risk patients with diabetes and CKD. Routine N-acetylcysteine is no longer recommended after large trials showed no benefit. Mannitol and forced diuresis with furosemide can worsen outcomes by causing volume depletion and have not demonstrated protective benefit; in fact diuretic-induced hypovolemia increases risk. Adequate hydration is the single most important preventive intervention.

cases.diagnosis: Contrast-associated (contrast-induced) acute kidney injury after coronary angiography
pearls: Contrast nephropathy typically raises creatinine 48-72 hours post-exposure, peaks at days 3-5, and resolves within 1-2 weeks.
Diabetes plus pre-existing CKD is the highest-risk combination; volume of contrast and baseline renal function drive risk.
Periprocedural isotonic IV hydration and minimizing contrast volume are the best preventive measures; N-acetylcysteine is not recommended.
Distinguish from atheroembolic disease (weeks-later onset, livedo, blue toes, eosinophilia, low complement) which also follows arterial catheterization.

cases.title: Foamy Urine and Swelling in a Diabetic Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Foamy urine and mild ankle swelling noted over the past few months.
cases.history: A 56-year-old man with a 14-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus comes to clinic for routine follow-up and mentions his urine has appeared foamy and his ankles swell by evening. His glycemic control has been suboptimal, with a recent hemoglobin A1c of 9.2%. He has known background diabetic retinopathy diagnosed by his ophthalmologist last year and reports tingling in both feet. Past medical history includes hypertension and dyslipidemia. Medications are metformin 1000 mg twice daily, glipizide, atorvastatin, and amlodipine; he is not on an ACE inhibitor or ARB. He has no known drug allergies. He works as an accountant, does not smoke, and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history includes type 2 diabetes in both parents and his father required dialysis. He denies hematuria, dysuria, flank pain, recent illness, or NSAID use. He has not had his urine albumin checked in over 2 years. Blood pressure readings at home have been running in the 140s/90s.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and overweight. Funduscopic exam shows dot-blot hemorrhages and microaneurysms consistent with diabetic retinopathy. The lungs are clear and the heart is regular without murmurs. The abdomen is soft and nontender. There is symmetric pitting edema to the mid-shins bilaterally. Neurologic exam reveals decreased sensation to monofilament and vibration in a stocking distribution over both feet, with intact strength. There is no rash, no joint swelling, and no costovertebral angle tenderness. Peripheral pulses are intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which test is most appropriate to screen for and quantify early diabetic kidney disease in this patient?
options: Urine eosinophils in this clinical context
24-hour urine creatinine clearance
Serum complement levels (C3 and C4)
Spot urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio
stages.explanation: The spot urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio is the recommended screening and monitoring test for diabetic kidney disease because it detects and quantifies albuminuria conveniently and reproducibly without a timed collection. A 24-hour creatinine clearance estimates GFR but is cumbersome and does not specifically assess albuminuria, the earliest marker of diabetic nephropathy. Urine eosinophils are used when interstitial nephritis is suspected, which is not the clinical picture. Complement levels help evaluate immune-complex glomerulonephritides; diabetic nephropathy is a non-immune process and does not lower complement, so these would not aid the diagnosis.

stages.label: LABS & URINE STUDIES
stages.content:
Urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio 880 mg/g
Urinalysis: 3+ protein, no blood, no cellular casts
Serum creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (eGFR 50 mL/min/1.73 m²)
Na 139 mEq/L, K 4.6 mEq/L, HCO3 24 mEq/L
Hemoglobin A1c 9.2%
C3 and C4 normal
ANA negative
Hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL
Renal ultrasound: normal-sized kidneys, no obstruction

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The constellation of severely increased albuminuria, diabetic retinopathy, bland sediment, and normal complement most supports which diagnosis?
options: Membranous nephropathy
Post-infectious glomerulonephritis
Lupus nephritis
Diabetic nephropathy
stages.explanation: Long-standing diabetes with progressive albuminuria, a bland urinary sediment, and concomitant diabetic retinopathy is the textbook presentation of diabetic nephropathy; the strong association between retinopathy and nephropathy is a key clinical clue, and the characteristic histology is nodular glomerulosclerosis (Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules). Membranous nephropathy causes nephrotic proteinuria but lacks the retinopathy link and is often associated with anti-PLA2R antibodies. Post-infectious glomerulonephritis features hematuria, red cell casts, and low complement after an infection. Lupus nephritis would typically show a positive ANA, low complement, and an active sediment, all absent here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond glycemic control, which medication change provides the greatest renal protection given his albuminuria and hypertension?
options: Increase the calcium channel blocker dose only
Switch amlodipine to an ACE inhibitor or ARB
Add a loop diuretic for the proteinuria
Start chronic corticosteroids as initial therapy
stages.explanation: An ACE inhibitor or ARB is the cornerstone of renoprotection in diabetic nephropathy: by reducing intraglomerular pressure it lowers albuminuria and slows progression to end-stage disease, and it is specifically indicated in a hypertensive diabetic with albuminuria. Replacing the dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker, which does not reduce proteinuria, with RAAS blockade is the highest-yield change. A loop diuretic treats edema but does not slow nephropathy. Simply uptitrating amlodipine lowers blood pressure without the antiproteinuric, kidney-protective benefit. Corticosteroids have no role in diabetic nephropathy, which is not an inflammatory immune process.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which additional agent is now recommended to further reduce cardiorenal risk in this patient with diabetic kidney disease and albuminuria?
options: A sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitor
A thiazolidinedione in this setting
A sulfonylurea at higher dose
An alpha-glucosidase inhibitor
stages.explanation: SGLT2 inhibitors are now recommended in patients with diabetic kidney disease and albuminuria because large trials demonstrated they slow GFR decline, reduce progression to end-stage renal disease, and lower cardiovascular and heart-failure events independent of glucose lowering. Adding them to an ACE inhibitor or ARB provides additive renal protection. Increasing the sulfonylurea improves glycemia but offers no specific renoprotection and raises hypoglycemia risk. Thiazolidinediones can cause fluid retention and worsen edema. Alpha-glucosidase inhibitors have modest glycemic effects and no proven renal benefit. The SGLT2 inhibitor is the evidence-based addition.

cases.diagnosis: Diabetic nephropathy (diabetic kidney disease) with severely increased albuminuria
pearls: Albuminuria is the earliest marker of diabetic nephropathy; screen with a spot urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio.
Concurrent diabetic retinopathy strongly supports diabetic nephropathy as the cause of proteinuria; its absence should prompt consideration of an alternative diagnosis.
Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodular glomerulosclerosis is the pathognomonic histologic lesion of diabetic nephropathy.
RAAS blockade (ACE inhibitor or ARB) plus an SGLT2 inhibitor are the foundational renoprotective therapies in diabetic kidney disease with albuminuria.

cases.title: Slowly Declining Kidney Function in a Man With High Blood Pressure
cases.chiefComplaint: An abnormal creatinine found on routine bloodwork in a man with longstanding hypertension.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man presents to establish care after being told his kidney function is mildly abnormal on screening labs. He has had hypertension for over 20 years but admits to poor adherence with medications and rarely checks his pressure. He feels well and denies edema, foamy urine, gross hematuria, dysuria, or flank pain. He has no recent illnesses and uses no NSAIDs or herbal supplements. Past medical history includes hypertension and mild obesity. He is intermittently prescribed amlodipine and chlorthalidone but frequently runs out. He has no known drug allergies. He works in construction, drinks two or three beers on weekends, and has a 25-pack-year smoking history. Family history is significant for hypertension and stroke in his father; there is no family history of polycystic kidney disease or hereditary nephritis. His prior records show blood pressures consistently in the 160s/100s over the years and a creatinine that has gradually drifted upward from 1.0 to 1.7 mg/dL over the last 5 years.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and overweight. Funduscopic exam shows arteriolar narrowing and arteriovenous nicking consistent with chronic hypertensive changes, without hemorrhages or papilledema. The cardiac apical impulse is sustained, and a soft S4 gallop is heard; there are no murmurs. The lungs are clear. The abdomen is soft and nontender with no bruits and no palpable masses or enlarged kidneys. There is no peripheral edema. Peripheral pulses are intact and symmetric. There is no rash and no joint involvement.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which initial workup is most appropriate to characterize the cause of his chronic kidney impairment?
options: CT angiography of the renal arteries as the first step today
Serum and urine protein electrophoresis as the first test
Urinalysis with microscopy, urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio,
Immediate renal biopsy as the first step today
stages.explanation: The appropriate first step in chronic kidney disease is noninvasive: a urinalysis with microscopy to characterize the sediment, quantification of proteinuria with an albumin-to-creatinine ratio, and a renal ultrasound to assess kidney size and exclude obstruction or structural disease. This stratifies the likely etiology and guides further testing. A biopsy is invasive and unnecessary when the clinical picture (long-standing poorly controlled hypertension with bland urine) is characteristic. CT angiography evaluates renovascular disease, not indicated without features suggesting it. Protein electrophoresis screens for paraproteinemia and would be reserved for atypical presentations, not the appropriate first-line test here.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Urinalysis: SG 1.015, trace protein, no blood, no cellular casts (bland sediment)
Urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio 160 mg/g
Serum creatinine 1.7 mg/dL (eGFR 46 mL/min/1.73 m²)
Na 140 mEq/L, K 4.3 mEq/L, HCO3 24 mEq/L
Hemoglobin A1c 5.6%
C3 and C4 normal
ANA negative
Renal ultrasound: kidneys 9.2 cm and 9.0 cm with mildly increased echogenicity; no hydronephrosis, no cysts

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The bland sediment, modest albuminuria, small echogenic kidneys, and chronic uncontrolled hypertension best fit which diagnosis?
options: Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis
Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease
Hypertensive nephrosclerosis
Diabetic nephropathy in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Hypertensive nephrosclerosis classically produces a slowly progressive decline in GFR with a bland urinary sediment, only modest proteinuria, and bilaterally small, echogenic kidneys in a patient with long-standing poorly controlled hypertension — exactly this presentation. Diabetic nephropathy is excluded by a normal A1c and absence of retinopathy. Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis would show an active sediment with red cell casts, hematuria, and a rapid rise in creatinine, none present. Polycystic kidney disease produces enlarged kidneys with multiple cysts and often a family history, the opposite of the small noncystic kidneys seen here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most important intervention to slow progression of his kidney disease?
options: Initiate immunosuppression with corticosteroids as initial therapy
Aggressive blood pressure control, preferably including an ACE inhibitor or ARB given his albuminuria
Begin a high-protein diet to preserve muscle mass as initial therapy
Refer urgently for renal artery stenting as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Controlling blood pressure to guideline targets is the cornerstone of slowing hypertensive nephrosclerosis, and because he has albuminuria an ACE inhibitor or ARB is preferred for its added antiproteinuric and renoprotective effect. Immunosuppression has no role in this non-inflammatory vascular process. A high-protein diet can increase intraglomerular pressure and proteinuria; moderate protein intake is generally advised in CKD. Renal artery stenting addresses hemodynamically significant renovascular disease, which is not suggested here (no abdominal bruit, no flash pulmonary edema, no asymmetric kidneys), and routine stenting has not been shown to improve outcomes.

stages.label: MONITORING
stages.question: Which set of parameters should be followed to monitor his chronic kidney disease over time?
options: Only serum creatinine at annual visits as the immediate next step
Repeated renal biopsies every 6 months as the immediate next step
Serial eGFR and urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio along with blood pressure
Daily 24-hour urine collections as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: CKD is staged and monitored by both eGFR (the G category) and albuminuria (the A category), and tracking these alongside blood pressure detects progression and gauges the response to therapy. Following creatinine alone at annual intervals misses the prognostic information from albuminuria and may detect decline too late. Serial biopsies are invasive and unnecessary for monitoring an established clinical diagnosis. Daily 24-hour urine collections are impractical and not needed; a spot albumin-to-creatinine ratio suffices for routine surveillance. Combined GFR and albuminuria assessment is the standard monitoring approach.

cases.diagnosis: Hypertensive nephrosclerosis
pearls: Hypertensive nephrosclerosis presents with a bland sediment, modest proteinuria, and small echogenic kidneys after years of poorly controlled hypertension.
An active sediment with red cell casts or heavy proteinuria should redirect the workup away from simple nephrosclerosis toward glomerular disease.
Blood pressure control is the key to slowing progression; ACE inhibitors/ARBs are preferred when albuminuria is present.
CKD is staged by both eGFR (G stage) and albuminuria (A stage) — monitor both, not creatinine alone.

cases.title: Fatigue and Pallor in an Older Woman With Long-standing Kidney Disease
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive fatigue, weakness, and shortness of breath on exertion.
cases.history: A 70-year-old woman with known chronic kidney disease attributed to long-standing hypertension presents for follow-up reporting several months of worsening fatigue, generalized weakness, and dyspnea climbing stairs. She has noticed she looks pale. She denies bleeding, melena, chest pain, or fever. Past medical history includes hypertension for 25 years, type 2 diabetes, and CKD that has slowly progressed over years. Medications are lisinopril 40 mg daily, amlodipine, carvedilol, atorvastatin, and a recently added oral phosphate-rich diet she follows poorly; she takes no iron and no erythropoiesis-stimulating agent. She has no known drug allergies. She is a lifelong nonsmoker, drinks no alcohol, and lives with her daughter. Family history includes diabetes and hypertension. She has not started dialysis and has no symptoms of overt uremia such as nausea, pruritus, or confusion. Her most recent eGFR several months ago was around 25 mL/min/1.73 m². She reports bone aches but no fractures.
cases.exam: The patient appears pale and mildly fatigued. Conjunctivae are pale. The lungs are clear and the heart is regular with a soft systolic flow murmur at the left sternal border. The abdomen is soft and nontender without organomegaly. There is trace bilateral pedal edema. There is no asterixis, no pericardial rub, and no scleral icterus. Skin shows no excoriations or ecchymoses. Neurologic exam is nonfocal. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness, and peripheral pulses are intact.

stages.label: LABS & STUDIES
stages.content:
Serum creatinine 2.9 mg/dL (eGFR 22 mL/min/1.73 m²)
BUN 48 mg/dL
Na 138 mEq/L, K 4.8 mEq/L, HCO3 20 mEq/L
Calcium 8.4 mg/dL
Phosphorus 5.6 mg/dL
Intact PTH 410 pg/mL (markedly elevated)
25-hydroxyvitamin D 18 ng/mL (low)
Hemoglobin 9.1 g/dL, MCV 88 fL
Ferritin 180 ng/mL, transferrin saturation 28%
Reticulocyte count low
Urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio 540 mg/g

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How would you stage her chronic kidney disease and characterize her anemia?
options: CKD stage 2 with iron deficiency anemia in this clinical context
CKD stage 5 requiring immediate dialysis with hemolytic anemia
CKD stage 4 with anemia of CKD due to erythropoietin deficiency
CKD stage 3a with anemia of chronic inflammation
stages.explanation: An eGFR of 22 mL/min/1.73 m² places her in CKD stage 4 (G4 = eGFR 15-29). Her normocytic anemia with a low reticulocyte count and adequate iron stores (ferritin 180, transferrin saturation 28%) reflects inadequate erythropoietin production by the failing kidneys — the anemia of CKD. Stage 2 (eGFR 60-89) is incorrect for her GFR, and her iron indices argue against iron deficiency. Stage 5 (eGFR <15) is not yet reached and she lacks dialysis indications; there is no evidence of hemolysis. Stage 3a (eGFR 45-59) does not match her GFR. The combination of stage 4 CKD and erythropoietin-deficiency anemia fits best.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Before considering an erythropoiesis-stimulating agent for her anemia, what must be ensured?
options: That she receives a blood transfusion regardless of symptoms
That she is started on dialysis as initial therapy
That her iron stores are adequate
That her PTH is normalized first as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Iron stores must be assessed and repleted before or alongside starting an erythropoiesis-stimulating agent, because ESAs are ineffective without adequate iron and functional iron deficiency is common in CKD. Targets generally call for ferritin and transferrin saturation above defined thresholds. Dialysis is not a prerequisite for treating anemia and is not yet indicated. Correcting PTH addresses mineral-bone disease, a separate problem, and is not required before ESA therapy. Routine transfusion is avoided when possible, especially in transplant candidates, due to alloimmunization risk; it is reserved for symptomatic or severe anemia. Ensuring iron sufficiency is the key first step.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her phosphorus is 5.6 mg/dL, PTH is markedly elevated, and vitamin D is low. What is the most appropriate combination to address her secondary hyperparathyroidism?
options: Start a high-dose calcium-based binder between meals to raise calcium
Restrict dietary phosphate, add a phosphate binder taken with meals,
Begin a thiazide diuretic to lower phosphorus as initial therapy
Encourage a high-phosphorus diet and stop all binders as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Secondary hyperparathyroidism in CKD is driven by phosphate retention, low calcitriol, and hypocalcemia. Management combines dietary phosphate restriction, a phosphate binder taken with meals to reduce gut absorption, and repletion of vitamin D (often with active vitamin D analogues once nutritional vitamin D is corrected) to suppress PTH. A high-phosphorus diet would worsen the problem. Calcium-based binders work only when taken with meals to bind dietary phosphate, not between meals, and excessive calcium loading risks vascular calcification. Thiazides do not lower serum phosphorus. The integrated approach of phosphate control plus vitamin D is correct.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which step is most important now to prepare her for the trajectory of her CKD?
options: Stop her ACE inhibitor permanently because of CKD with appropriate follow-up
Restrict her to bed rest to preserve kidney function with appropriate follow-up
Refer to nephrology for education
Place a tunneled dialysis catheter today with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: At stage 4 CKD she should be co-managed with nephrology for education about modality options (hemodialysis, peritoneal dialysis, transplantation) and for timely arteriovenous fistula planning if she approaches the need for dialysis — advance planning improves outcomes and avoids urgent catheter starts. A tunneled catheter today is premature; she has no current dialysis indication and catheters carry infection risk. ACE inhibitors remain renoprotective and antiproteinuric in CKD and are continued with monitoring unless hyperkalemia or acute decline mandates otherwise. Bed rest does not preserve renal function. Early nephrology referral and access planning is the priority.

cases.diagnosis: Chronic kidney disease stage 4 with anemia of CKD and secondary hyperparathyroidism
pearls: CKD G-staging: G3a 45-59, G3b 30-44, G4 15-29, G5 <15 mL/min/1.73 m²; stage 4 warrants nephrology co-management and access planning.
Anemia of CKD is normocytic and hypoproliferative from erythropoietin deficiency; correct iron deficiency before starting an ESA.
Secondary hyperparathyroidism stems from phosphate retention, low calcitriol, and hypocalcemia — treat with phosphate restriction, meal-time binders, and vitamin D.
Calcium-based phosphate binders must be taken with meals to bind dietary phosphate; excess calcium load risks vascular calcification.

cases.title: A Young Boy With Sudden Puffy Eyes
cases.chiefComplaint: Swelling around the eyes and decreased urination over the past several days.
cases.history: A 6-year-old boy is brought to clinic by his mother for puffiness around both eyes that started about a week ago and is worst in the mornings. Over the past few days she has also noticed swelling of his abdomen and ankles, and his urine appears foamy. He has been more tired but has had no fever, sore throat, rash, joint pain, or gross blood in the urine. He had a mild cold two weeks ago that has since resolved. Past medical history is unremarkable; he is fully immunized and meets developmental milestones. He takes no regular medications and has no known drug allergies. He attends first grade, lives with both parents and a younger sister, and has no sick contacts currently. Family history is negative for kidney disease, autoimmune disease, and hereditary nephritis. There is no history of recent skin infection, no abdominal pain, and no recent travel. His appetite is slightly reduced and his weight is up about 2 kg from his last visit.
cases.exam: The child is alert and interactive but has obvious bilateral periorbital edema. There is mild pitting edema of the lower extremities and a fluid wave suggesting modest ascites; the abdomen is soft and nontender with normoactive bowel sounds. The lungs are clear and the heart is regular without murmurs. There is no rash, no purpura, and no joint swelling or tenderness. The throat is not erythematous and there is no lymphadenopathy. He is well-perfused with brisk capillary refill. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial test to confirm the suspected cause of his edema?
options: Renal biopsy as the first step today
Throat culture as the first step today
Abdominal CT scan as the first step today
Urinalysis with quantification of proteinuria
stages.explanation: Generalized edema with periorbital swelling and foamy urine in a young child is the classic presentation of nephrotic syndrome, and a urinalysis demonstrating heavy proteinuria (with quantification by a urine protein-to-creatinine ratio) is the appropriate first step to confirm it. A throat culture would evaluate streptococcal pharyngitis, which is associated with post-infectious glomerulonephritis (a nephritic, hematuric picture), not this nephrotic presentation. A renal biopsy is not the initial step in a typical-aged child, who is presumed to have minimal change disease and treated empirically. An abdominal CT does not establish the renal diagnosis and adds radiation without benefit.

stages.label: LABS & URINE STUDIES
stages.content:
Urinalysis: 4+ protein, no blood, no cellular casts, oval fat bodies present
Urine protein-to-creatinine ratio 6.8 mg/mg (nephrotic range)
Serum albumin 1.9 g/dL
Total cholesterol 320 mg/dL
Serum creatinine 0.4 mg/dL (normal for age)
Na 138 mEq/L, K 4.2 mEq/L
C3 and C4 normal
Urine protein electrophoresis: highly selective proteinuria (predominantly albumin)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The selective proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, hyperlipidemia, normal complement, and age make which diagnosis most likely?
options: Membranous nephropathy
IgA nephropathy
Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis
Minimal change disease
stages.explanation: Minimal change disease is by far the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in young children and characteristically produces highly selective (mostly albumin) proteinuria, profound hypoalbuminemia, hyperlipidemia, edema, normal complement, and a normal creatinine — exactly this picture. Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis is nephritic with hematuria, red cell casts, hypertension, and low C3. Membranous nephropathy is a leading cause of nephrotic syndrome in adults, not children, and produces nonselective proteinuria. IgA nephropathy typically causes hematuria, often synpharyngitic, rather than isolated selective nephrotic-range proteinuria. The clinical and laboratory profile fits minimal change disease.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the appropriate first-line treatment in this child?
options: Empiric oral corticosteroids without an initial biopsy
Observation alone with no pharmacotherapy
Renal biopsy followed by rituximab as initial therapy
Cyclophosphamide as initial therapy
stages.explanation: In a child of typical age with this classic nephrotic presentation, empiric oral corticosteroids are first-line without an initial biopsy, because minimal change disease is overwhelmingly likely and is highly steroid-responsive, with most children entering remission within weeks. Cyclophosphamide and other steroid-sparing agents are reserved for frequently relapsing, steroid-dependent, or steroid-resistant disease, not initial therapy. Biopsy is reserved for atypical features (age outside the typical range, hematuria, hypertension, renal insufficiency, low complement, or steroid resistance). Observation alone is inappropriate given symptomatic nephrotic syndrome with its risks of infection and thrombosis.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication is the nephrotic state most likely to predispose this child to?
options: Macrocytic anemia as the leading concern
Hypercalcemia as the leading concern
Hyperkalemia as the leading concern
Increased risk of infection
stages.explanation: Nephrotic syndrome causes urinary loss of immunoglobulins and complement factors and predisposes to serious infections, classically pneumococcal spontaneous bacterial peritonitis in children, and the urinary loss of antithrombotic proteins such as antithrombin produces a hypercoagulable state with increased thromboembolic risk. Hyperkalemia is not characteristic of uncomplicated minimal change disease with preserved GFR. Macrocytic anemia is unrelated to the nephrotic state. Hypercalcemia does not occur; if anything, loss of vitamin D-binding protein and hypoalbuminemia can lower total calcium. The hallmark complications to anticipate are infection and thromboembolism.

cases.diagnosis: Minimal change disease (nephrotic syndrome of childhood)
pearls: Minimal change disease is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children and produces highly selective (albumin-predominant) proteinuria with normal complement.
Treat typical pediatric nephrotic syndrome empirically with corticosteroids; biopsy only for atypical features or steroid resistance.
Electron microscopy shows diffuse podocyte foot process effacement with normal light microscopy and no immune deposits.
Anticipate infection (pneumococcal peritonitis) and thromboembolism from urinary loss of immunoglobulins and antithrombin.

cases.title: Heavy Proteinuria and Swelling in a Young Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive leg swelling and foamy urine over the past two months.
cases.history: A 34-year-old African American man presents with two months of progressive lower-extremity edema and persistently foamy urine. He has also gained weight and noticed his shoes feel tight. He denies gross hematuria, dysuria, flank pain, fever, rash, or joint pain. He has no history of recent infection. Past medical history is notable only for borderline hypertension; he takes no regular medications and uses no NSAIDs, heroin, or anabolic steroids. He has no known drug allergies. He works as a warehouse supervisor, drinks alcohol occasionally, and does not smoke. He has no risk factors elicited for HIV but agrees to testing. Family history includes hypertension and a maternal uncle who required dialysis at a young age for unclear reasons. He has not had prior urine studies. Review of systems is otherwise negative for hemoptysis, sinus symptoms, or photosensitivity. He reports no recent weight loss, night sweats, or new medications.
cases.exam: The patient is well-developed with notable pitting edema to the knees bilaterally and mild periorbital fullness. The lungs are clear and the heart is regular without murmurs or rubs. The abdomen is soft and nontender without organomegaly; there is no fluid wave. There is no rash, no oral ulcers, no synovitis, and no lymphadenopathy. Neurologic exam is nonfocal. Peripheral pulses are intact and there is no costovertebral angle tenderness. There are no stigmata of chronic liver disease and no track marks.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic workup for his nephrotic-range edema?
options: Renal artery duplex ultrasound as the first step today
24-hour Holter monitor as the first step today
Empiric high-dose corticosteroids without any laboratory testing
Urinalysis with microscopy, quantification of proteinuria, serum albumin
stages.explanation: The appropriate first step is to confirm and quantify proteinuria and characterize the syndrome with a urinalysis and microscopy, urine protein-to-creatinine ratio, serum albumin and lipid panel, and a serologic workup that includes HIV, hepatitis B and C, complement, and ANA — particularly important in a young African American man in whom HIV-associated and collapsing FSGS are considerations. Empiric steroids before establishing the diagnosis is inappropriate in an adult, where biopsy guides therapy. Renal artery duplex evaluates renovascular hypertension, not nephrotic syndrome. A Holter monitor is irrelevant to this renal presentation.

stages.label: LABS & SEROLOGIES
stages.content:
Urinalysis: 3+ protein, few RBCs, no red cell casts
Urine protein-to-creatinine ratio 7.5 mg/mg (nephrotic range)
Serum albumin 2.4 g/dL
Total cholesterol 310 mg/dL
Serum creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (eGFR 58 mL/min/1.73 m²)
C3 and C4 normal
ANA negative
HIV antibody/antigen negative
Hepatitis B and C serologies negative
Anti-PLA2R antibody negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given nephrotic-range proteinuria with normal complement and negative serologies in this patient, what is the most appropriate next step to establish the diagnosis?
options: Repeat the urinalysis in 6 months and observe
Treat empirically for membranous nephropathy
Diagnose diabetic nephropathy and start an ACE inhibitor
Perform a renal biopsy
stages.explanation: In an adult with unexplained nephrotic syndrome, negative serologies, and normal complement, a renal biopsy is required to establish the histologic diagnosis and guide treatment. The biopsy will distinguish among the adult causes of nephrotic syndrome — focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (including the collapsing variant of particular concern in this demographic), membranous nephropathy, and minimal change disease. There is no history of diabetes to diagnose diabetic nephropathy, and the negative anti-PLA2R does not support empiric treatment for membranous nephropathy. Watchful observation is inappropriate for symptomatic nephrotic syndrome with an already elevated creatinine; tissue diagnosis must come first.

stages.label: RENAL BIOPSY
stages.content:
Light microscopy: focal and segmental sclerosis of some glomeruli with hyalinosis; remaining glomeruli appear normal
Immunofluorescence: nonspecific segmental IgM and C3 trapping in sclerotic areas
Electron microscopy: widespread (diffuse) podocyte foot process effacement
No spike-and-dome deposits; no subepithelial immune complexes
APOL1 high-risk genotype noted on genetic risk assessment

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis do these biopsy findings confirm?
options: Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis
Minimal change disease
Membranous nephropathy
Membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis
stages.explanation: Focal (some glomeruli) and segmental (part of each affected glomerulus) sclerosis with hyalinosis, nonspecific IgM/C3 trapping, and diffuse foot process effacement on electron microscopy is diagnostic of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis; the APOL1 high-risk genotype and the patient's demographics raise particular concern for the aggressive collapsing variant. Membranous nephropathy shows subepithelial spike-and-dome deposits, absent here. Minimal change disease shows foot process effacement but no segmental sclerosis on light microscopy. Membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis features a lobular hypercellular pattern with double-contour basement membranes and usually low complement, none of which is present.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment for biopsy-proven primary FSGS in this patient?
options: High-dose corticosteroids together with renin-angiotensin system blockade
Plasmapheresis as first-line therapy as initial therapy
RAAS blockade plus blood pressure control alone, with no immunosuppression
Observation with no specific treatment as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Primary FSGS presenting with nephrotic syndrome is treated with high-dose corticosteroids as first-line immunosuppression, combined with renin-angiotensin system blockade and blood pressure control to reduce proteinuria and slow progression; calcineurin inhibitors are used for steroid-resistant or steroid-dependent disease. RAAS blockade and blood pressure control alone are appropriate adjuncts but are insufficient for primary nephrotic FSGS, which warrants immunosuppression. Plasmapheresis is reserved for recurrent FSGS after transplantation, not initial native-kidney therapy. Observation alone is inappropriate given symptomatic nephrotic syndrome and declining renal function.

cases.diagnosis: Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (with collapsing-variant and APOL1 risk considerations)
pearls: FSGS is a leading cause of nephrotic syndrome in adults, especially in African American patients, where APOL1 high-risk variants increase susceptibility.
Biopsy shows focal and segmental sclerosis with hyalinosis and diffuse foot process effacement; the collapsing variant is the most aggressive.
Always screen for secondary causes (HIV, heroin, obesity, reflux) before labeling FSGS primary; HIV is a classic cause of collapsing FSGS.
Primary nephrotic FSGS is treated with corticosteroids plus RAAS blockade; calcineurin inhibitors are used for steroid-resistant disease.

cases.title: New Swelling and Frothy Urine in a Middle-aged Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Several weeks of leg swelling and frothy urine.
cases.history: A 52-year-old man presents with about six weeks of bilateral leg swelling, frothy urine, and a 4 kg weight gain. He denies gross hematuria, dysuria, flank pain, fever, rash, or joint pain. He has no recent infections. Past medical history includes well-controlled hypertension and seasonal allergies. Medications are amlodipine and occasional loratadine; he takes no NSAIDs and no new drugs. He has no known drug allergies. He is up to date on age-appropriate cancer screening, including a normal colonoscopy last year. He does not smoke, drinks alcohol occasionally, and works as an engineer. Family history is unremarkable for kidney disease. Review of systems is negative for hemoptysis, weight loss, night sweats, sinus symptoms, and photosensitivity. He reports no calf pain or chest symptoms. He has had no recent travel and no known hepatitis exposure. He is sexually active with one long-term partner.
cases.exam: The patient is comfortable with pitting edema to the mid-shins bilaterally and mild periorbital puffiness. The lungs are clear and the heart is regular without murmurs or rubs. The abdomen is soft and nontender without organomegaly or a fluid wave. There is no rash, no synovitis, and no lymphadenopathy. Calves are nontender and symmetric with negative findings for deep vein thrombosis. Peripheral pulses are intact and there is no costovertebral angle tenderness. Neurologic exam is nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial workup to characterize his edema?
options: Empiric diuretics alone with no laboratory evaluation in this clinical context
Urinalysis with quantification of proteinuria, serum albumin
Lower-extremity venous Doppler only as the first step today
Liver biopsy as the first step today
stages.explanation: Bilateral edema with frothy urine suggests nephrotic syndrome, so the initial workup quantifies proteinuria (urine protein-to-creatinine ratio), measures serum albumin and lipids, and sends a serologic panel including anti-PLA2R antibody, hepatitis B and C, HIV, ANA, and complement to evaluate primary versus secondary glomerular disease. Empiric diuretics treat the symptom without diagnosing the cause and risk overdiuresis in a hypoalbuminemic patient. A venous Doppler is reasonable only if unilateral leg findings suggest thrombosis, but his edema is bilateral and symmetric. A liver biopsy is not indicated; there are no stigmata of liver disease.

stages.label: LABS & SEROLOGIES
stages.content:
Urinalysis: 3+ protein, no blood, no cellular casts
Urine protein-to-creatinine ratio 8.2 mg/mg (nephrotic range)
Serum albumin 2.2 g/dL
Total cholesterol 340 mg/dL
Serum creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (eGFR 78 mL/min/1.73 m²)
C3 and C4 normal
ANA negative
Hepatitis B and C serologies negative
HIV negative
Anti-PLA2R (phospholipase A2 receptor) antibody POSITIVE

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given nephrotic-range proteinuria with normal complement and a positive anti-PLA2R antibody, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Primary membranous nephropathy
Lupus nephritis
IgA nephropathy
Minimal change disease
stages.explanation: A positive anti-phospholipase A2 receptor (anti-PLA2R) antibody in an adult with nephrotic syndrome and normal complement is highly specific for primary membranous nephropathy, the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in older non-diabetic adults. Minimal change disease lacks a specific serologic marker and is far more common in children. Lupus nephritis would typically show a positive ANA, low complement, and an active sediment, all absent here. IgA nephropathy presents with hematuria rather than isolated nephrotic-range proteinuria and is not associated with anti-PLA2R. The serologic and clinical profile points clearly to primary membranous nephropathy.

stages.label: RENAL BIOPSY
stages.content:
Light microscopy: diffuse thickening of the glomerular basement membranes without hypercellularity
Immunofluorescence: granular subepithelial deposits of IgG (IgG4 predominant) and C3 along the capillary loops; PLA2R staining positive
Electron microscopy: subepithelial electron-dense deposits with intervening basement membrane projections producing a spike-and-dome appearance
No endocapillary proliferation; no subendothelial deposits

stages.label: INTERPRETATION
stages.question: Which biopsy finding is most characteristic of and confirms membranous nephropathy?
options: Mesangial hypercellularity with IgA deposits as the immediate next step
Diffuse foot process effacement with no deposits as the immediate next step
Subepithelial immune deposits with a spike-and-dome pattern on electron microscopy
Crescent formation in Bowman's space as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Membranous nephropathy is defined by subepithelial immune-complex deposits that, with the basement membrane reaction around them, produce the classic spike-and-dome appearance on electron microscopy, accompanied by granular capillary-loop IgG (IgG4-predominant in primary disease) and PLA2R positivity on immunofluorescence. Mesangial hypercellularity with IgA deposits indicates IgA nephropathy. Crescents indicate rapidly progressive (crescentic) glomerulonephritis. Diffuse foot process effacement without deposits is the hallmark of minimal change disease. The subepithelial spike-and-dome pattern with PLA2R-positive IgG is diagnostic of membranous nephropathy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has nephrotic-range proteinuria and a positive anti-PLA2R titer. What is the most appropriate management approach?
options: Supportive antiproteinuric therapy with risk stratification, reserving immunosuppression for moderate-to-high-risk or progressive disease
No treatment, as membranous nephropathy never progresses as initial therapy
Plasmapheresis as first-line therapy as initial therapy
Immediate immunosuppression for all patients regardless of risk as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Primary membranous nephropathy is managed by first optimizing supportive antiproteinuric therapy with RAAS blockade and blood pressure control while risk-stratifying using proteinuria, renal function, and anti-PLA2R titer; a meaningful fraction of patients undergo spontaneous remission. Immunosuppression — rituximab, or cyclophosphamide-based or calcineurin-inhibitor regimens — is reserved for moderate-to-high-risk patients or those with progressive disease or persistent heavy proteinuria. Treating everyone immediately overexposes low-risk patients to toxicity. Plasmapheresis is not first-line for native membranous nephropathy. Asserting it never progresses is false; untreated high-risk disease can advance to end-stage kidney disease, so monitoring and selective therapy are essential.

cases.diagnosis: Primary membranous nephropathy (anti-PLA2R positive)
pearls: Membranous nephropathy is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in older non-diabetic adults; anti-PLA2R antibody is highly specific for primary disease.
Electron microscopy shows subepithelial deposits with a spike-and-dome pattern; immunofluorescence shows granular IgG4 and C3 along capillary loops.
Always evaluate for secondary causes (malignancy, hepatitis B, lupus, drugs like NSAIDs) before labeling membranous nephropathy primary.
Risk-stratify before immunosuppressing — many low-risk patients remit spontaneously on supportive RAAS blockade; rituximab is preferred for higher-risk disease.

cases.title: Visible Blood in the Urine During a Cold
cases.chiefComplaint: Reddish-brown urine that appeared during an upper respiratory infection.
cases.history: A 24-year-old man presents with two episodes of visibly reddish-brown (cola-colored) urine, the first noticed two days ago. He notes the discoloration began only a day or two after the onset of a sore throat, runny nose, and low-grade malaise typical of a cold. He has had similar but milder episodes of dark urine with prior colds over the past few years that resolved on their own. He denies dysuria, frequency, flank pain, fever, rash, joint pain, or recent vigorous exercise. He takes no medications and uses no NSAIDs or supplements. He has no known drug allergies. He is a graduate student, drinks alcohol socially, and does not smoke. Family history is notable for a brother with intermittent hematuria of unclear cause. He has no history of kidney stones, recent skin infection, or hearing loss. Review of systems is otherwise negative for hemoptysis, abdominal pain, and weight loss. He is otherwise healthy and active.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress. The oropharynx shows mild erythema without exudate, and there is small tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy. The lungs are clear and the heart is regular without murmurs. The abdomen is soft and nontender with no flank or costovertebral angle tenderness. There is no peripheral edema, no rash or palpable purpura, and no joint swelling. Genitourinary exam is unremarkable. Neurologic exam is nonfocal and hearing is grossly intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first test to evaluate his cola-colored urine?
options: Cystoscopy as the first step today
Coagulation studies as the first step today
Urinalysis with microscopy to look for dysmorphic RBCs
CT urogram to evaluate for nephrolithiasis
stages.explanation: A urinalysis with microscopy is the essential first step to determine whether the discoloration represents true hematuria and, critically, whether it is glomerular — dysmorphic red blood cells and red cell casts indicate a glomerular source, redirecting the workup toward glomerulonephritis. A CT urogram and cystoscopy evaluate urologic sources such as stones or bladder lesions and are more appropriate for an older patient or when microscopy suggests a non-glomerular source; they are not first-line in a young man with synpharyngitic hematuria. Coagulation studies are unhelpful without bleeding elsewhere and would not explain glomerular hematuria. Microscopy localizes the source and guides everything that follows.

stages.label: LABS & URINE STUDIES
stages.content:
Urinalysis: 3+ blood, 1+ protein
Microscopy: numerous dysmorphic RBCs and occasional red cell casts
Urine protein-to-creatinine ratio 0.8 mg/mg
Serum creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (eGFR 88 mL/min/1.73 m²)
Na 140 mEq/L, K 4.2 mEq/L
C3 and C4 NORMAL
ASO titer not elevated
ANA negative, ANCA negative
Throat swab: rapid strep negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Gross hematuria within 1-2 days of a URI, glomerular RBCs with red cell casts, and a normal complement most strongly suggest which diagnosis?
options: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis
Thin basement membrane disease
IgA nephropathy
Anti-GBM disease in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Synpharyngitic gross hematuria — visible hematuria occurring within 1-2 days of a respiratory infection — with glomerular (dysmorphic) red cells, red cell casts, and a NORMAL complement is the classic presentation of IgA nephropathy, the most common primary glomerulonephritis worldwide. Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis differs in timing (a 1-3 week latent period after infection), shows LOW C3, and elevated ASO. Thin basement membrane disease causes persistent microscopic hematuria, usually without red cell casts or the synpharyngitic gross hematuria pattern. Anti-GBM disease presents with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis, often with pulmonary hemorrhage, and positive anti-GBM antibodies, none of which is seen here.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding distinguishes IgA nephropathy from post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis in this patient?
options: Presence of proteinuria as the primary diagnosis
Presence of red cell casts as the primary diagnosis
Normal complement and hematuria concurrent with the infection
Dysmorphic red blood cells on microscopy in this clinical context
stages.explanation: The two key discriminators are timing and complement: IgA nephropathy produces hematuria concurrent with (synpharyngitic, within 1-2 days of) the infection and characteristically has normal C3 and C4, whereas post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis follows a latent period of roughly 1-3 weeks after pharyngitis or 3-6 weeks after skin infection and shows LOW C3. Red cell casts, dysmorphic red cells, and proteinuria can occur in both glomerulonephritides and therefore do not distinguish them. Recognizing the synchronous timing and the preserved complement is what points specifically to IgA nephropathy over a post-infectious process.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His proteinuria is sub-nephrotic, renal function is preserved, and blood pressure is mildly elevated. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Plasmapheresis as initial therapy
Immediate high-dose corticosteroids and cyclophosphamide as initial therapy
Renin-angiotensin system blockade with blood pressure control
Tonsillectomy as first-line therapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For IgA nephropathy with preserved renal function and sub-nephrotic proteinuria, first-line management is optimized supportive care: renin-angiotensin system blockade (ACE inhibitor or ARB) to lower proteinuria and control blood pressure, with serial monitoring of proteinuria and renal function. Immunosuppression with corticosteroids is considered only if significant proteinuria persists despite maximal supportive therapy, given its toxicity. Cyclophosphamide is reserved for crescentic, rapidly progressive disease. Tonsillectomy is not established first-line therapy in most guidelines. Plasmapheresis is reserved for rapidly progressive crescentic glomerulonephritis, which this stable patient does not have. Supportive RAAS blockade is the appropriate starting point.

cases.diagnosis: IgA nephropathy (Berger disease)
pearls: Synpharyngitic gross hematuria — within 1-2 days of a URI — with normal complement is classic for IgA nephropathy, the most common primary glomerulonephritis worldwide.
Contrast the timing with post-streptococcal GN: a 1-3 week latent period and LOW C3 point to the post-infectious process.
Biopsy shows mesangial proliferation with dominant IgA deposits on immunofluorescence; Henoch-Schonlein purpura is its systemic counterpart.
First-line therapy is RAAS blockade and blood pressure control; reserve corticosteroids for persistent proteinuria despite optimized supportive care.

cases.title: No Urine and a Swollen Joint
cases.chiefComplaint: A man hospitalized for chemotherapy develops markedly decreased urine output and a rising creatinine.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man with newly diagnosed high-grade non-Hodgkin lymphoma (bulky abdominal disease) was admitted for his first cycle of cyclophosphamide-based chemotherapy, started 48 hours ago. Over the past day his urine output has fallen to roughly 150 mL/24h. He reports nausea, fatigue, and mild left great-toe pain. He has a history of hypertension and two prior podagra attacks managed with NSAIDs; he never started urate-lowering therapy. Home medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, occasional ibuprofen. He was NOT given rasburicase or allopurinol before chemotherapy, and IV fluids were minimal because of mild peripheral edema. No prior CKD; baseline creatinine 1.0 mg/dL one month ago. Allergies: none. Social: retired electrician, 1 alcoholic drink weekly, never smoked. Family history: gout in his father. He has had no fevers, dysuria, flank trauma, or recent contrast exposure, and takes no herbal supplements.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable, mildly volume overloaded but alert and oriented. JVP estimated at 9 cm. Cardiac: regular rate and rhythm, no rub or murmur. Lungs: bibasilar crackles without wheeze. Abdomen: soft and nontender, with palpable bulky periumbilical lymphadenopathy; no flank tenderness, no palpable bladder, no hepatosplenomegaly. A post-void bladder scan shows only 40 mL, arguing against lower-tract obstruction. Extremities: trace lower-extremity edema; the left first metatarsophalangeal joint is mildly warm and tender without a tense effusion or overlying erythema. No rash, no asterixis, no costovertebral angle tenderness. Skin turgor is normal and mucous membranes are moist, consistent with the absence of overt volume depletion.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the oliguric AKI two days after starting chemotherapy for bulky lymphoma, which mechanism is most likely?
options: Contrast-induced nephropathy from staging imaging
Cyclophosphamide-induced hemorrhagic cystitis
Prerenal azotemia from gastrointestinal losses
Tumor lysis syndrome with acute uric acid nephropathy
stages.explanation: Bulky, chemosensitive lymphoma plus no urate prophylaxis sets the stage for tumor lysis syndrome; rapid cell turnover releases purines, and uric acid precipitates in acidic distal tubules causing obstructive, oliguric AKI. Contrast nephropathy is wrong because no recent contrast and the timing/setting point to tumor lysis. Prerenal azotemia is unlikely with euvolemic-to-overloaded exam (elevated JVP, crackles, edema) rather than volume depletion. Hemorrhagic cystitis from cyclophosphamide causes hematuria and dysuria, not oliguric intrinsic AKI, and is prevented with mesna/hydration; it would not explain a uric acid-driven metabolic picture. Recognizing tumor lysis early drives urgent labs and management.

stages.label: LABS & URINE
stages.content:
Na 138 mEq/L
K 6.1 mEq/L
HCO3 18 mEq/L
BUN 58 mg/dL
Creatinine 3.8 mg/dL (baseline 1.0)
Calcium 7.4 mg/dL
Phosphorus 7.2 mg/dL
Uric acid 16.5 mg/dL
LDH 980 U/L
Urinalysis: pH 5.0, no protein, no blood, rare urate crystals, no casts
Urine uric acid-to-creatinine ratio >1.0
Renal ultrasound: normal-sized kidneys, no hydronephrosis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding BEST distinguishes acute uric acid nephropathy from chronic gouty (urate) nephropathy in this patient?
options: An elevated serum uric acid level as the primary diagnosis
A spot urine uric acid-to-creatinine ratio greater than 1.0
Normal-sized kidneys on ultrasound as the primary diagnosis
Presence of hypertension and a history of podagra
stages.explanation: In acute uric acid nephropathy, massive purine load produces enormous filtered urate; a urine uric acid-to-creatinine ratio >1.0 reflects overproduction and intratubular precipitation and is characteristic, whereas chronic gouty nephropathy (now rare) shows a ratio <0.6-0.7 with insidious interstitial disease. Hypertension and prior podagra are nonspecific and occur in both. Normal kidney size does not distinguish acute from chronic, and uric acid is elevated in both conditions—chronic urate nephropathy can even have only mildly high levels. The acute/chronic distinction matters because acute nephropathy is reversible with prompt urate lowering and volume expansion.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which intervention is the most appropriate next step to lower this patient's uric acid?
options: Febuxostat plus a thiazide diuretic
Rasburicase
Urinary alkalinization with a sodium bicarbonate infusion
Allopurinol 300 mg daily as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Rasburicase (recombinant urate oxidase) enzymatically converts uric acid to highly soluble allantoin and rapidly lowers established, very high urate (16.5 mg/dL) in tumor lysis with AKI—the preferred agent here. Allopurinol blocks new urate formation but does NOT remove existing urate and works too slowly for established acute nephropathy. Routine urinary alkalinization is no longer recommended: it can worsen calcium phosphate precipitation (phosphorus is already 7.2) and offers no benefit when rasburicase is used. Febuxostat also only prevents formation, and adding a thiazide raises uric acid and worsens volume status—exactly wrong. Check G6PD status before rasburicase to avoid hemolysis.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: His potassium is 6.1 mEq/L with peaked T waves on ECG and persistent oliguria despite fluids and rasburicase. What is the best next step?
options: Repeat the potassium before any treatment as the leading concern
IV furosemide 40 mg and recheck in 6 hours as the leading concern
Oral sodium polystyrene sulfonate alone as the leading concern
IV calcium gluconate followed by insulin/dextrose, then arrange hemodialysis
stages.explanation: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes is an emergency: give IV calcium gluconate to stabilize the myocardium, then insulin/dextrose (and a beta-agonist) to shift potassium intracellularly. Because he has oliguric AKI from tumor lysis with refractory hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, and acidosis unresponsive to medical therapy, hemodialysis is the definitive treatment and should be arranged. Sodium polystyrene sulfonate is slow, of limited efficacy, and inadequate for symptomatic hyperkalemia. Furosemide may help if any urine response remains but is insufficient as sole therapy with ECG changes and oliguria. Delaying treatment to recheck the level given peaked T waves risks lethal arrhythmia.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Considering the next chemotherapy cycle, which strategy would best have prevented this complication and should be used going forward?
options: A low-purine diet alone as the immediate next step
Aggressive IV hydration with rasburicase prophylaxis before cytotoxic therapy
Prophylactic hemodialysis before each cycle as the immediate next step
Routine sodium bicarbonate urinary alkalinization as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: In high-risk tumor lysis (bulky, chemosensitive lymphoma with already-high uric acid), the proven prevention is aggressive IV hydration to maintain brisk urine flow plus rasburicase prophylaxis before cytotoxic therapy, with close monitoring of electrolytes, phosphate, and urate—this is what was omitted on the first cycle. Routine sodium bicarbonate alkalinization is no longer recommended and risks calcium phosphate deposition. Prophylactic hemodialysis before each cycle is excessive and not standard; dialysis is reserved for established refractory metabolic derangements. A low-purine diet does nothing to counter the massive endogenous purine release from lysing tumor cells. Hydration plus rasburicase is the cornerstone of prophylaxis.

cases.diagnosis: Acute uric acid nephropathy due to tumor lysis syndrome
pearls: Acute uric acid nephropathy presents as oliguric AKI with a urine uric acid-to-creatinine ratio >1.0, distinguishing it from chronic gouty nephropathy (ratio <0.6-0.7).
Prevent tumor lysis in high-risk malignancies with aggressive IV hydration and rasburicase (or allopurinol for lower risk) before cytotoxic therapy.
Rasburicase rapidly degrades existing uric acid to allantoin; allopurinol/febuxostat only block new formation and act too slowly for established nephropathy.
Routine urine alkalinization is no longer recommended in tumor lysis—it promotes calcium phosphate deposition when phosphate is high.

cases.title: Recurrent Stones in a Healthy Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: A woman presents to clinic with her fourth episode of kidney stones in three years and recurrent urinary infections.
cases.history: A 41-year-old woman is referred for recurrent nephrolithiasis. She has passed at least five small stones over three years; two prior stone analyses showed calcium oxalate and calcium phosphate mixtures. She also reports three culture-proven urinary tract infections in the past two years and intermittent painless microscopic hematuria noted on routine screening. She drinks adequate fluids and follows a typical diet. PMH otherwise unremarkable; no inflammatory bowel disease, no prior bowel surgery, no gout.
