options: Rituximab monotherapy as initial therapy
High-dose corticosteroids alone as initial therapy
Hemodialysis and supportive care while awaiting spontaneous recovery
Plasmapheresis plus corticosteroids
stages.explanation: Anti-GBM disease is treated urgently with the triple combination of plasmapheresis (to remove circulating pathogenic antibody), corticosteroids, and cyclophosphamide (to suppress further antibody production). Prompt treatment is critical because outcomes worsen sharply once oligo-anuria or dialysis-dependence develops. Corticosteroids alone are inadequate because they do not remove existing antibody and do not stop ongoing antibody synthesis effectively. Rituximab can be used in refractory cases or when cyclophosphamide is contraindicated but is not standard first-line monotherapy. Supportive care and dialysis without immunosuppression abandons the only chance to halt pulmonary hemorrhage and salvage renal function, and would be inappropriate given active alveolar hemorrhage.
stages.explanationCorrect: Anti-GBM disease requires urgent plasmapheresis to remove antibody PLUS corticosteroids and cyclophosphamide to suppress production; delay worsens outcomes.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which factor most strongly predicts poor renal recovery in anti-GBM disease?
options: A negative ANCA result with appropriate follow-up
Presence of hemoptysis at presentation
Patient's young age with appropriate follow-up
Dialysis-dependence or oligoanuria at presentation
stages.explanation: Dialysis-dependence (or oligoanuria) at presentation, along with a high percentage of crescents and severe creatinine elevation, is the strongest predictor of poor renal recovery in anti-GBM disease; once a patient requires dialysis, renal recovery is unlikely even with aggressive therapy. Hemoptysis indicates pulmonary involvement and is life-threatening but, when treated, does not by itself predict permanent renal failure. Young age is generally favorable, not unfavorable. A negative ANCA does not worsen prognosis; in fact, double-positive (ANCA and anti-GBM) patients have a relapsing course but variable renal outcomes. The degree of renal impairment at presentation is the key prognostic driver.
stages.explanationCorrect: Dialysis-dependence or oligoanuria at presentation predicts poor renal recovery; once dialysis is needed, kidneys rarely recover despite aggressive therapy.

cases.diagnosis: Anti-GBM disease (Goodpasture syndrome)
pearls: Anti-GBM antibodies target the alpha-3 chain of type IV collagen, shared by alveolar and glomerular basement membranes.
Linear IgG staining along the GBM on immunofluorescence is pathognomonic.
Treat urgently with plasmapheresis + steroids + cyclophosphamide; smoking and inhaled toxins worsen pulmonary hemorrhage.
Dialysis-dependence at presentation predicts poor renal recovery; about one third of patients are also ANCA-positive.

cases.title: Chronic Sinus Trouble Now with Kidney Failure
cases.chiefComplaint: A woman is admitted for months of sinus problems, bloody nasal discharge, and new acute kidney injury.
cases.history: A 58-year-old woman is admitted with several months of chronic sinusitis, recurrent epistaxis, and bloody crusting nasal discharge that was repeatedly treated as bacterial sinusitis without improvement. Over the past few weeks she has developed fatigue, a dry cough, intermittent hemoptysis, migratory joint pains, and a 5-kg weight loss. In the last week she noticed reduced urine output and dark urine. PMH is notable only for seasonal allergies. Medications: loratadine 10 mg daily and several courses of antibiotics. Allergies: none. She is a never-smoker, works as a librarian, and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history is unremarkable. ED labs revealed an elevated creatinine and an active urine sediment, prompting admission. Review of systems is positive for nasal symptoms, cough, hemoptysis, arthralgias, and constitutional symptoms, and negative for abdominal pain, hearing loss, and skin rash. She reports a sensation of nasal obstruction and a recent change in the shape of her nasal bridge.
cases.exam: The patient appears chronically ill. Nasal exam shows crusting, mucosal ulceration, and early flattening of the nasal bridge (saddle-nose). There is mild scleral injection. Lungs have scattered crackles in the upper fields; cardiac exam is normal. Abdomen is benign. There is trace lower-extremity edema. Skin shows a few scattered palpable purpuric lesions on the lower legs. Joints are mildly tender without effusions. Neurologic exam reveals an early right foot drop suggestive of mononeuritis multiplex. There is no lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hemoglobin: 9.8 g/dL
WBC: 12.4 ×10³/µL
Platelets: 410 ×10³/µL
Serum creatinine: 3.2 mg/dL (baseline 0.9)
BUN: 44 mg/dL
Urinalysis: blood 3+, protein 2+, dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts present
ESR: 88 mm/hr; CRP: 14 mg/dL
C3 and C4: normal
CT chest: multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules, some cavitating

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The triad of upper airway disease, cavitary lung nodules, and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis most suggests which category of disease?
options: ANCA-associated small-vessel vasculitis
Immune-complex glomerulonephritis
Thrombotic microangiopathy
Anti-GBM disease as the first step today
stages.explanation: The combination of upper respiratory tract disease (chronic sinusitis, saddle-nose), cavitary pulmonary nodules, and rapidly progressive GN with an active sediment but NORMAL complements is classic for ANCA-associated small-vessel vasculitis, specifically granulomatosis with polyangiitis. Immune-complex GN (e.g., lupus, post-infectious, MPGN) typically shows low complements and granular immunofluorescence, and does not produce destructive sinonasal disease or cavitary nodules. Anti-GBM disease causes pulmonary-renal syndrome but not chronic destructive sinus disease or cavitary nodules, and shows linear IgG. Thrombotic microangiopathy causes microangiopathic hemolysis and thrombocytopenia with a bland sediment, not granulomatous airway disease.
stages.explanationCorrect: Upper-airway destruction, cavitary lung nodules, and RPGN with NORMAL complements point to ANCA-associated pauci-immune small-vessel vasculitis (GPA).

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which ANCA pattern and antigen specificity is most characteristic of granulomatosis with polyangiitis?
options: c-ANCA targeting proteinase 3 (PR3)
Atypical ANCA targeting lactoferrin
p-ANCA targeting myeloperoxidase (MPO)
Anti-GBM antibody in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA) is most strongly associated with c-ANCA directed against proteinase 3 (PR3). p-ANCA/MPO is more typical of microscopic polyangiitis and eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis, though overlap exists. Atypical ANCA targeting lactoferrin or other minor antigens is seen in inflammatory bowel disease, autoimmune hepatitis, and drug-induced vasculitis, not classic GPA. Anti-GBM antibody defines Goodpasture disease, not GPA. The c-ANCA/PR3 pattern, in the context of this patient's destructive upper-airway disease and cavitary nodules, strongly supports GPA and should be confirmed with biopsy showing pauci-immune necrotizing granulomatous inflammation.
stages.explanationCorrect: GPA is most strongly associated with c-ANCA against proteinase 3 (PR3); p-ANCA/MPO is more typical of microscopic polyangiitis.

stages.label: SEROLOGIES & BIOPSY
stages.content:
c-ANCA (PR3-ANCA): strongly positive
p-ANCA (MPO-ANCA): negative
Anti-GBM antibody: negative
ANA: negative
Kidney biopsy: focal necrotizing crescentic glomerulonephritis
Immunofluorescence: pauci-immune (scant immunoglobulin/complement deposition)
Sinus biopsy: necrotizing granulomatous inflammation with vasculitis

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate induction therapy for this patient with severe organ-threatening GPA?
options: High-dose corticosteroids plus rituximab
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole monotherapy as monotherapy
Low-dose prednisone alone, tapered over 6 weeks
Methotrexate monotherapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Severe, organ-threatening GPA (here with RPGN and alveolar/pulmonary involvement) requires induction with high-dose corticosteroids combined with either rituximab or cyclophosphamide; rituximab is now often preferred and is at least as effective, especially for relapsing or PR3-positive disease. Low-dose prednisone alone is grossly inadequate for life- and organ-threatening vasculitis. Methotrexate is reserved for non-severe, non-organ-threatening disease and is contraindicated with significant renal impairment. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole has a role in limited upper-airway GPA and for Pneumocystis prophylaxis during immunosuppression, but it is not induction therapy for severe systemic disease. Plasmapheresis may be added for severe renal failure or alveolar hemorrhage in selected patients.
stages.explanationCorrect: Severe organ-threatening GPA requires high-dose corticosteroids plus rituximab or cyclophosphamide for remission induction; rituximab is often preferred.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: During cyclophosphamide-based therapy, which preventive measure is most important to reduce a well-known urologic toxicity?
options: Allopurinol to prevent urate nephropathy
Calcium and vitamin D to prevent nephrolithiasis
Bicarbonate infusion to prevent contrast nephropathy
Aggressive hydration
stages.explanation: Cyclophosphamide's metabolite acrolein is toxic to the bladder urothelium and causes hemorrhagic cystitis (and increases long-term bladder cancer risk). Aggressive hydration plus mesna, which binds and neutralizes acrolein in the urine, is the key preventive strategy, particularly with IV pulse dosing. Allopurinol prevents urate nephropathy in tumor lysis syndrome, not cyclophosphamide cystitis, and importantly interacts with azathioprine. Bicarbonate for contrast nephropathy is unrelated to cyclophosphamide toxicity. Calcium and vitamin D are used to address steroid-induced osteoporosis, not bladder toxicity. Patients on cyclophosphamide also warrant Pneumocystis prophylaxis and monitoring for cytopenias and infection.
stages.explanationCorrect: Cyclophosphamide's metabolite acrolein causes hemorrhagic cystitis; aggressive hydration plus mesna neutralizes acrolein and protects the bladder.

cases.diagnosis: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis
pearls: GPA = upper airway (saddle-nose, sinusitis) + lung (cavitary nodules) + kidney (pauci-immune crescentic GN).
c-ANCA/PR3 is the classic serology; complements are normal because it is pauci-immune, not immune-complex mediated.
Induction is steroids + rituximab or cyclophosphamide; rituximab is preferred for relapsing/PR3 disease.
Give mesna and hydration with cyclophosphamide to prevent acrolein-induced hemorrhagic cystitis.

cases.title: An Older Man with Fatigue and Vanishing Kidney Function
cases.chiefComplaint: A man is admitted with weeks of malaise and a creatinine that has tripled.
cases.history: A 66-year-old man is admitted for six weeks of progressive fatigue, low-grade fevers, anorexia, a 6-kg weight loss, and new arthralgias and myalgias. Over the last two weeks he developed numbness and weakness in his right hand and left foot, and his outpatient labs showed a creatinine rising from 1.1 to 3.4 mg/dL. He denies hemoptysis, sinus symptoms, epistaxis, or oral ulcers. PMH includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes (well controlled, HbA1c 6.8%). Medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily. Allergies: none. He is a former smoker (quit 15 years ago) and retired carpenter. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for constitutional symptoms, arthralgias, and asymmetric sensorimotor deficits, and negative for upper-airway disease, asthma/wheezing, and skin nodules. He has noticed mild lower-extremity swelling and tea-colored urine. There is no history of recent infection, new medications, or hepatitis exposure.
cases.exam: The patient is fatigued but alert. HEENT exam is normal with no nasal crusting, septal lesions, or saddle-nose deformity. Lungs are clear without crackles or wheeze. Cardiac exam is normal. Abdomen is benign without organomegaly. There is 1+ pitting edema of the lower legs and scattered palpable purpura over the shins. Neurologic exam shows decreased sensation and weakness in the right median nerve distribution and left common peroneal distribution (foot drop), consistent with mononeuritis multiplex. There is no synovitis. No splinter hemorrhages or digital ischemia are noted.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hemoglobin: 10.1 g/dL
WBC: 9.8 ×10³/µL (eosinophils normal)
Platelets: 365 ×10³/µL
Serum creatinine: 3.4 mg/dL (baseline 1.1)
BUN: 48 mg/dL
Urinalysis: blood 3+, protein 2+, dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts present
C3 and C4: normal
ESR: 95 mm/hr; CRP: 16 mg/dL
Chest X-ray: clear lungs, no nodules or infiltrates
Hepatitis B and C serologies: negative

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given RPGN with mononeuritis multiplex, normal complements, and a CLEAR chest without upper-airway or granulomatous features, which diagnosis is most likely?
options: Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis
Granulomatosis with polyangiitis
Polyarteritis nodosa in this clinical context
Microscopic polyangiitis
stages.explanation: Microscopic polyangiitis (MPA) is a pauci-immune small-vessel vasculitis causing rapidly progressive GN, often with mononeuritis multiplex and constitutional symptoms, and crucially WITHOUT granulomatous inflammation, upper-airway destruction, or asthma. Normal complements fit pauci-immune disease. Granulomatosis with polyangiitis features destructive sinonasal disease and cavitary lung nodules, absent here. Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Churg-Strauss) requires asthma and prominent eosinophilia, neither present. Polyarteritis nodosa is a medium-vessel vasculitis that classically SPARES the glomeruli (causing renovascular hypertension and microaneurysms rather than glomerulonephritis) and does not produce RBC casts or an ANCA-positive pauci-immune crescentic GN; it is also frequently hepatitis-B associated, and serologies here are negative.
stages.explanationCorrect: RPGN with mononeuritis multiplex, normal complements, and NO granulomas/upper-airway disease/asthma is classic microscopic polyangiitis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which ANCA serology is most characteristic of microscopic polyangiitis?
options: c-ANCA targeting proteinase 3 (PR3)
Anti-dsDNA antibody
p-ANCA targeting myeloperoxidase
Anti-GBM antibody
stages.explanation: Microscopic polyangiitis is most strongly associated with p-ANCA directed against myeloperoxidase (MPO). c-ANCA/PR3 is more characteristic of granulomatosis with polyangiitis, which this patient lacks given the absence of granulomatous and upper-airway features. Anti-GBM antibody defines Goodpasture disease (pulmonary-renal syndrome with linear IgG), not pauci-immune MPA. Anti-dsDNA is specific for systemic lupus erythematosus, which would typically show low complements and a positive ANA with full-house immunofluorescence on biopsy, not pauci-immune histology. The p-ANCA/MPO pattern, combined with pauci-immune necrotizing crescentic GN on biopsy and the clinical picture, confirms MPA.
stages.explanationCorrect: MPA is most strongly associated with p-ANCA against myeloperoxidase (MPO); c-ANCA/PR3 favors GPA instead.

stages.label: SEROLOGIES & BIOPSY
stages.content:
p-ANCA (MPO-ANCA): strongly positive
c-ANCA (PR3-ANCA): negative
Anti-GBM antibody: negative
ANA: negative; anti-dsDNA: negative
Kidney biopsy: necrotizing crescentic glomerulonephritis, no granulomas
Immunofluorescence: pauci-immune (minimal immune deposits)
Electron microscopy: no electron-dense immune-complex deposits

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this patient with severe MPA and a creatinine of 3.4 mg/dL with active crescentic GN, what is the most appropriate induction therapy?
options: High-dose glucocorticoids plus rituximab or cyclophosphamide
Mycophenolate mofetil monotherapy as initial therapy
Observation with repeat creatinine in 4 weeks as monotherapy
ACE inhibitor titration and blood pressure control alone
stages.explanation: Severe organ-threatening MPA with crescentic GN requires prompt remission induction using high-dose glucocorticoids combined with rituximab or cyclophosphamide. Rituximab is non-inferior to cyclophosphamide and is often chosen, particularly to avoid cyclophosphamide toxicity in older patients. Observation risks irreversible nephron loss; ANCA crescentic GN can progress to dialysis within days to weeks. Mycophenolate is generally used for maintenance or as an alternative in selected cases, not as primary induction for severe disease. ACE inhibitors and blood pressure control are supportive adjuncts but do not treat the underlying vasculitis and would allow continued glomerular destruction. Plasmapheresis may be considered for very severe renal failure (e.g., dialysis-dependence) or concurrent alveolar hemorrhage.
stages.explanationCorrect: Severe MPA with crescentic GN needs prompt induction with high-dose glucocorticoids plus rituximab or cyclophosphamide; rituximab spares older patients toxicity.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: In which scenario would adding plasmapheresis to induction therapy be most clearly indicated in ANCA-associated vasculitis?
options: Dialysis-dependent severe renal failure or concurrent diffuse alveolar hemorrhage
Mild disease with creatinine of 1.4 mg/dL and no hemorrhage in this setting
Isolated arthralgias without renal involvement as the immediate next step
Stable disease in clinical remission on maintenance therapy in this setting
stages.explanation: Plasmapheresis is added to glucocorticoids plus rituximab/cyclophosphamide in selected severe scenarios, most clearly with dialysis-requiring severe renal failure or life-threatening diffuse alveolar hemorrhage, where rapid removal of circulating ANCA and inflammatory mediators may improve outcomes (though its benefit in renal recovery has become more debated, it is still used for the sickest patients). Mild disease with a near-normal creatinine and no hemorrhage does not warrant the risks of plasmapheresis. Isolated arthralgias without organ-threatening involvement is treated with immunosuppression alone, not apheresis. A patient already in remission on maintenance therapy has no role for plasmapheresis, which is an induction-phase adjunct for severe, active disease, not a maintenance strategy.
stages.explanationCorrect: Plasmapheresis is reserved for the sickest ANCA vasculitis patients: dialysis-dependent severe renal failure or diffuse alveolar hemorrhage.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Several weeks into high-dose steroids plus immunosuppression, the patient develops fever, dry cough, and progressive hypoxemia with diffuse interstitial infiltrates on CT. What is the most likely complication?
options: Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia
Pulmonary embolism as the leading concern
Cardiogenic pulmonary edema
Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage from vasculitis relapse
stages.explanation: Patients on high-dose glucocorticoids plus rituximab or cyclophosphamide are profoundly immunosuppressed and at high risk for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP), which classically presents with fever, dry cough, progressive hypoxemia, and diffuse interstitial infiltrates, hence the recommendation for prophylactic trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole. Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage from relapse is possible but would typically cause hemoptysis, a falling hemoglobin, and alveolar (not purely interstitial) infiltrates while on active induction therapy, making it less likely. Pulmonary embolism causes acute pleuritic pain and hypoxemia but not diffuse interstitial infiltrates or fever as the dominant picture. Cardiogenic pulmonary edema would show cardiomegaly, an elevated BNP, and a fluid-overload pattern rather than infectious symptoms.
stages.explanationCorrect: Heavy immunosuppression predisposes to Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (fever, dry cough, hypoxemia, diffuse interstitial infiltrates); hence routine TMP-SMX prophylaxis.

cases.diagnosis: Microscopic polyangiitis
pearls: MPA is pauci-immune necrotizing crescentic GN WITHOUT granulomas, upper-airway disease, or asthma (distinguishing it from GPA and EGPA).
Classic serology is p-ANCA/MPO; complements are normal.
Induction is glucocorticoids + rituximab or cyclophosphamide; add plasmapheresis for dialysis-level renal failure or alveolar hemorrhage.
Always give PJP prophylaxis (TMP-SMX) with high-dose steroids plus cytotoxic/biologic therapy.

cases.title: A Young Woman with Swelling, Rash, and Bloody Urine
cases.chiefComplaint: A young woman is admitted with leg swelling, joint pain, and frothy, blood-tinged urine.
cases.history: A 26-year-old woman is admitted for two weeks of worsening bilateral leg and facial swelling, fatigue, and frothy urine that she noticed was tea-colored on a few occasions. She also reports several months of intermittent joint pains in her hands and wrists, a facial rash that worsens in sunlight, oral ulcers, and hair thinning. She denies fever, dysuria, or recent infection. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: only an oral contraceptive pill and occasional ibuprofen. Allergies: none. She is a graduate student, does not smoke, and drinks alcohol socially. Family history is notable for a maternal aunt with 'a thyroid problem and lupus.' ED labs revealed elevated creatinine, low albumin, and significant proteinuria. Review of systems is positive for photosensitive rash, arthralgias, oral ulcers, alopecia, and Raynaud phenomenon, and negative for hemoptysis, sinus disease, and seizures. She reports recent unintentional weight gain attributed to edema and increasing abdominal fullness.
cases.exam: The patient has periorbital and bilateral lower-extremity pitting edema to the knees. There is an erythematous malar rash sparing the nasolabial folds and a few shallow palatal ulcers. Cardiac exam is normal without rub; lungs are clear, though there is dullness at both bases suggesting small effusions. Abdomen is mildly distended with a fluid wave consistent with ascites and is nontender. Mild synovitis is present at several metacarpophalangeal joints. Skin shows no palpable purpura. Neurologic exam is nonfocal. There is no lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hemoglobin: 9.4 g/dL
WBC: 3.1 ×10³/µL (lymphopenia)
Platelets: 110 ×10³/µL
Serum creatinine: 1.9 mg/dL (baseline ~0.7)
Serum albumin: 2.4 g/dL
Urinalysis: blood 2+, protein 3+, dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts present
Urine protein/creatinine ratio: 3.8 mg/mg
C3: low; C4: low
ANA: positive 1:1280; anti-dsDNA: strongly positive

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The combination of a nephritic-nephrotic picture, LOW C3 and C4, positive ANA, and high anti-dsDNA most strongly suggests which diagnosis?
options: Lupus nephritis
Minimal change disease
Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis
ANCA-associated vasculitis
stages.explanation: This young woman has multisystem features of SLE (malar rash, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, arthritis, cytopenias) plus glomerulonephritis with depressed C3 AND C4 (classical and alternative pathway consumption by immune complexes), a positive ANA, and high anti-dsDNA, all pointing to lupus nephritis. Post-streptococcal GN shows low C3 with NORMAL C4 and lacks systemic lupus features and high-titer anti-dsDNA. ANCA-associated vasculitis is pauci-immune with NORMAL complements and is not associated with anti-dsDNA or malar rash. Minimal change disease causes pure nephrotic syndrome with a bland sediment (no RBC casts), normal complements, and normal serologies, which does not match this active nephritic-nephrotic presentation with hypocomplementemia.
stages.explanationCorrect: Multisystem lupus features plus GN with LOW C3 AND C4, positive ANA, and high anti-dsDNA indicate lupus nephritis (immune-complex disease).

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which immunofluorescence pattern on kidney biopsy is most characteristic of lupus nephritis?
options: Linear IgG along the basement membrane
Granular IgA in the mesangium only
Full-house staining
Pauci-immune (scant deposits)
stages.explanation: Lupus nephritis classically shows a 'full-house' immunofluorescence pattern with staining for IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, and C1q, reflecting deposition of multiple immune-complex components; C1q positivity is particularly suggestive of lupus. Linear IgG along the basement membrane is diagnostic of anti-GBM disease. Pauci-immune staining (minimal deposits) characterizes ANCA-associated vasculitis. Granular IgA confined to the mesangium is the hallmark of IgA nephropathy, not lupus. The full-house pattern, combined with subendothelial deposits and the clinical/serologic picture, supports diffuse proliferative (class IV) lupus nephritis, the most common and severe form.
stages.explanationCorrect: Lupus nephritis shows full-house immunofluorescence (IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q); C1q positivity is especially suggestive.

stages.label: KIDNEY BIOPSY
stages.content:
Light microscopy: diffuse endocapillary proliferation involving >50% of glomeruli, with wire-loop lesions
Immunofluorescence: full-house pattern (IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q)
Electron microscopy: prominent subendothelial electron-dense deposits
Classification: ISN/RPS Class IV (diffuse proliferative) lupus nephritis
Activity index: elevated; chronicity index: low

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate induction therapy for biopsy-proven Class IV lupus nephritis?
options: Hydroxychloroquine alone as initial therapy
Plasmapheresis as initial therapy
Rituximab monotherapy without steroids as monotherapy
High-dose corticosteroids plus mycophenolate mofetil
stages.explanation: Class IV (diffuse proliferative) lupus nephritis is aggressive and requires induction immunosuppression with high-dose corticosteroids combined with mycophenolate mofetil or cyclophosphamide; belimumab or a calcineurin inhibitor may be added. Hydroxychloroquine is recommended as background therapy for ALL lupus patients and improves outcomes, but it is not sufficient as sole treatment for proliferative nephritis. Plasmapheresis is not standard for lupus nephritis (it is used for TTP, anti-GBM disease, and severe alveolar hemorrhage). Rituximab is reserved for refractory disease and is not used as steroid-free monotherapy for induction. All patients should also receive an ACE inhibitor/ARB for proteinuria, blood pressure control, and bone/infection prophylaxis as appropriate.
stages.explanationCorrect: Class IV lupus nephritis needs induction with high-dose steroids plus mycophenolate or cyclophosphamide; hydroxychloroquine is background therapy, not sole treatment.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which serologic markers are most useful for monitoring lupus nephritis activity over time?
options: ESR alone with appropriate follow-up
Anti-Smith antibody titers
Rheumatoid factor levels
Anti-dsDNA titers and complement levels
stages.explanation: Anti-dsDNA titers and complement levels (C3/C4) correlate with lupus nephritis activity: rising anti-dsDNA and falling complements often signal a flare, while improvement in these markers tracks treatment response, alongside proteinuria and creatinine. Anti-Smith antibody is highly specific for the diagnosis of SLE but does NOT fluctuate usefully with disease activity. Rheumatoid factor is nonspecific and not used to monitor lupus nephritis. ESR is a nonspecific inflammatory marker and is far less informative than the lupus-specific markers; relying on it alone would miss serologically active renal disease. Combining anti-dsDNA and complements with urinary indices gives the best longitudinal assessment.
stages.explanationCorrect: Anti-dsDNA titers and complements (C3/C4) track lupus nephritis activity; anti-Smith confirms diagnosis but does not follow activity.

cases.diagnosis: Lupus nephritis (Class IV, diffuse proliferative)
pearls: Lupus nephritis consumes BOTH classical and alternative complement: low C3 AND low C4 (contrast PSGN's low C3, normal C4).
Full-house immunofluorescence (IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q) with subendothelial deposits and wire-loop lesions defines Class IV.
Induce with steroids + mycophenolate or cyclophosphamide; hydroxychloroquine is background therapy for all lupus patients.
Follow anti-dsDNA and C3/C4 to track activity; anti-Sm and anti-dsDNA are diagnostically specific for SLE.

cases.title: Persistent Bloody, Foamy Urine in a College Student
cases.chiefComplaint: A college student presents with months of foamy, occasionally bloody urine and mild leg swelling.
cases.history: A 19-year-old woman presents to clinic with several months of persistently foamy urine, occasional visible blood in the urine, and intermittent mild ankle swelling. She reports fatigue but denies fever, joint pain, rash, sinus symptoms, or hemoptysis. She had what she thought was a 'cold' a few months ago but no clear sore throat. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: none. Allergies: none. She does not smoke, drinks alcohol occasionally at social events, and is sexually active using condoms. Family history is negative for kidney disease, deafness, or autoimmune disease. She has not traveled recently. ED-style screening labs from student health showed proteinuria and microscopic hematuria, prompting referral. Review of systems is positive for foamy urine, intermittent gross hematuria, and mild edema, and negative for dysuria, flank pain, arthralgias, oral ulcers, and photosensitivity. She has noted that her hematuria and proteinuria have not resolved despite several months of observation.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing. There is no malar or other rash and no oral ulcers. Cardiac exam is normal without murmur or rub; lungs are clear. Abdomen is soft and nontender without organomegaly or masses. There is trace to 1+ pitting edema of both ankles. Skin shows no palpable purpura. Joints are nontender with full range of motion. There is no lymphadenopathy. Neurologic exam is normal. Fundoscopic and gross hearing exam are unremarkable, and there is no peripheral edema beyond the ankles.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Serum creatinine: 1.3 mg/dL
BUN: 18 mg/dL
Urinalysis: blood 3+, protein 3+, dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts present
Urine protein/creatinine ratio: 4.2 mg/mg
Serum albumin: 3.0 g/dL
C3: persistently low; C4: normal
ANA: negative; anti-dsDNA: negative
ASO titer: not elevated; hepatitis B/C and HIV: negative
Complement repeated 10 weeks apart: C3 remains low

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A young patient with combined nephritic AND nephrotic features and PERSISTENTLY low C3 (with normal C4) over 10 weeks most suggests which diagnosis?
options: Thin basement membrane disease as the first step today
Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis in this clinical context
Membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis / C3 glomerulopathy
Minimal change disease as the first step today
stages.explanation: Persistent hypocomplementemia (low C3 with normal C4, indicating alternative-pathway dysregulation) beyond 8 weeks in a patient with a mixed nephritic-nephrotic picture is characteristic of membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis (MPGN), particularly the C3 glomerulopathy/dense deposit spectrum. Post-streptococcal GN can transiently lower C3, but it normalizes within 6-8 weeks and would not persist for 10 weeks, nor would it typically cause nephrotic-range proteinuria; the absent ASO and lack of preceding pharyngitis also argue against it. Minimal change disease causes pure nephrotic syndrome with a bland sediment (no RBC casts) and normal complements. Thin basement membrane disease causes isolated microscopic hematuria with minimal proteinuria and normal complements, not this nephritic-nephrotic, hypocomplementemic picture.
stages.explanationCorrect: Persistently low C3 (normal C4) beyond 8 weeks plus a nephritic-nephrotic picture points to MPGN / C3 glomerulopathy from alternative-pathway dysregulation.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which kidney biopsy finding is most characteristic of membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis?
options: Splitting/duplication of the GBM producing a 'tram-track' appearance
Linear IgG along the basement membrane as the primary diagnosis
Effacement of foot processes with otherwise normal glomeruli
Subepithelial 'humps' on electron microscopy in this clinical context
stages.explanation: MPGN classically shows mesangial proliferation and duplication/splitting of the glomerular basement membrane from mesangial cell interposition, creating the 'tram-track' (double-contour) appearance on light microscopy with silver stain, plus a lobular glomerular architecture. Subepithelial 'humps' are the hallmark of post-infectious/post-streptococcal GN. Foot-process effacement with otherwise normal glomeruli is minimal change disease. Linear IgG along the basement membrane is anti-GBM disease. The tram-track appearance, combined with persistently low C3, supports MPGN; immunofluorescence and electron microscopy then subclassify it as immune-complex-mediated versus C3 glomerulopathy (including dense deposit disease).
stages.explanationCorrect: MPGN shows GBM duplication/splitting (mesangial interposition) producing the 'tram-track' double-contour appearance with lobular glomeruli.

stages.label: KIDNEY BIOPSY
stages.content:
Light microscopy: lobular accentuation with mesangial proliferation and GBM duplication ('tram-track' double contours) on silver stain
Immunofluorescence: dominant C3 deposition with scant immunoglobulin
Electron microscopy: subendothelial and mesangial electron-dense deposits
Serviceable complement studies: low C3, normal C4; C3 nephritic factor positive
Diagnosis: MPGN pattern, C3 glomerulopathy

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Immunofluorescence shows DOMINANT C3 with scant immunoglobulin, plus a positive C3 nephritic factor. What does this finding indicate about the mechanism?
options: Immune-complex deposition with abundant immunoglobulin driving injury
Dysregulation of the alternative complement pathway
T-cell-mediated injury without complement involvement
Anti-GBM antibody binding the basement membrane in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Dominant C3 staining with little or no immunoglobulin, together with a positive C3 nephritic factor (an autoantibody that stabilizes the alternative-pathway C3 convertase), indicates dysregulation of the ALTERNATIVE complement pathway, defining C3 glomerulopathy within the MPGN pattern. Abundant immunoglobulin with C3 would instead indicate an immune-complex-mediated MPGN (e.g., from hepatitis C cryoglobulinemia or a monoclonal protein), which is not the dominant-C3 pattern seen here. Anti-GBM antibody produces linear IgG, not granular dominant-C3 staining, and causes crescentic GN rather than a tram-track MPGN pattern. T-cell-mediated injury without complement is not consistent with conspicuous C3 deposition and persistent hypocomplementemia. This distinction matters because C3 glomerulopathy may respond to complement-directed therapy.
stages.explanationCorrect: Dominant C3 with scant immunoglobulin plus a C3 nephritic factor signals alternative-pathway dysregulation, defining C3 glomerulopathy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond treating any identified underlying cause, which is the most appropriate baseline therapy for proteinuria and blood pressure in this patient?
options: Immediate initiation of chronic hemodialysis
ACE inhibitor or ARB with blood pressure control
Empiric high-dose IV antibiotics as monotherapy
Loop diuretic monotherapy indefinitely
stages.explanation: For glomerular disease with proteinuria, an ACE inhibitor or ARB is foundational: it lowers intraglomerular pressure, reduces proteinuria, and slows CKD progression, while also controlling hypertension. A thorough search for secondary causes of the MPGN pattern (infections like hepatitis C, monoclonal gammopathy, autoimmune disease, and complement-pathway abnormalities) guides additional therapy, and immunosuppression or complement-directed agents (e.g., eculizumab) may be used in selected C3 glomerulopathy cases. Empiric IV antibiotics are not indicated without an identified infection. Loop diuretics treat edema symptomatically but do not address proteinuria or progression and are not adequate sole therapy. Chronic hemodialysis is premature for a patient with a creatinine of 1.3 mg/dL and no indication for renal replacement.
stages.explanationCorrect: An ACE inhibitor or ARB is foundational for proteinuric glomerular disease, reducing intraglomerular pressure, proteinuria, and CKD progression while controlling BP.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which workup is most important to identify a treatable underlying cause of the MPGN pattern in this patient?
options: Renal artery Doppler ultrasound as the immediate next step
24-hour ambulatory blood pressure monitoring before discharge planning
Hepatitis C serology, serum/urine protein electrophoresis,
Echocardiogram for valvular disease as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The MPGN pattern is a pattern of injury, not a single disease, so the priority is to find treatable secondary causes: chronic infections (especially hepatitis C, also hepatitis B, endocarditis, chronic abscess), monoclonal gammopathy (serum/urine protein electrophoresis and free light chains), autoimmune disease, and dysregulation of the alternative complement pathway (C3 nephritic factor, factor H/I, complement gene studies). Identifying these directs cause-specific therapy. An echocardiogram is useful only if endocarditis is suspected, not as a primary universal step. Ambulatory BP monitoring helps manage hypertension but does not reveal the etiology. Renal artery Doppler evaluates renovascular hypertension, which does not cause a glomerular MPGN pattern with nephritic-nephrotic features.
stages.explanationCorrect: MPGN is a pattern, not a disease; workup for hepatitis C, monoclonal gammopathy (SPEP/UPEP), autoimmune disease, and complement dysregulation finds treatable causes.

cases.diagnosis: Membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis (C3 glomerulopathy spectrum)
pearls: MPGN causes a mixed nephritic-nephrotic picture with PERSISTENTLY low C3 (unlike PSGN, which normalizes by 6-8 weeks).
Biopsy hallmark: 'tram-track' GBM double contours from mesangial interposition, with lobular glomeruli.
Always hunt for secondary causes: hepatitis C, monoclonal gammopathy, infections, and alternative-pathway complement dysregulation (C3 nephritic factor).
Baseline therapy is RAAS blockade (ACE inhibitor/ARB); cause-specific or complement-directed therapy is added as indicated.

cases.title: Microscopic Blood in the Urine on a Routine Check
cases.chiefComplaint: A healthy woman is referred for microscopic blood in her urine found on a routine screening.
cases.history: A 30-year-old woman is referred to nephrology after a routine pre-employment urinalysis showed microscopic hematuria. She feels well, with no gross hematuria, dysuria, flank pain, frothy urine, edema, or recent illness. She has had blood detected on urine dipsticks several times over the past few years, always without symptoms. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: none. Allergies: none. She does not smoke, drinks alcohol rarely, and exercises regularly. Importantly, her father and one brother also have 'blood in the urine' that has never caused kidney problems, and there is NO family history of kidney failure, dialysis, deafness, or eye disease. ED-style stone and infection screens have been negative in the past. Review of systems is entirely negative, including for hearing loss, visual changes, joint pains, and rashes. She is worried because the hematuria keeps appearing on routine checks, though she has never felt unwell, and she has no history of nephrolithiasis or recurrent urinary tract infections.
cases.exam: The patient is healthy-appearing and in no distress. Cardiac and pulmonary exams are normal. The abdomen is soft and nontender, with no palpable kidneys, masses, or costovertebral angle tenderness. There is no peripheral edema. Skin is clear without rash or purpura. There is no lymphadenopathy. Gross hearing appears normal to conversational voice, and there are no obvious ocular abnormalities. Neurologic exam is normal. Blood pressure is normal, and there are no stigmata of systemic disease.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first step to characterize this microscopic hematuria?
options: Immediate kidney biopsy as the first step today
CT urogram as the first step today
Cystoscopy as the first step today
Repeat urinalysis with microscopy to confirm
stages.explanation: The first step in microscopic hematuria is to confirm it with a repeat urinalysis and microscopy, assessing for dysmorphic RBCs and RBC casts (glomerular origin) versus isometric RBCs (urologic source), and to check for proteinuria and renal function, which determine whether a glomerular or urologic workup is needed. Cystoscopy and CT urogram are appropriate for evaluating a urologic source (e.g., malignancy) in older patients or those with risk factors, but are not the first step in a young, low-risk patient with a likely glomerular pattern. Immediate kidney biopsy is too aggressive before characterizing the urine and excluding significant proteinuria or renal impairment; many causes of isolated glomerular hematuria are benign and do not require biopsy.
stages.explanationCorrect: Confirm with repeat UA/microscopy first: dysmorphic RBCs/casts suggest glomerular origin, and checking proteinuria and renal function directs the workup.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Repeat urinalysis: blood 2+, protein negative, no leukocyte esterase/nitrite
Microscopy: 15-25 RBC/hpf, predominantly dysmorphic; NO RBC casts; no WBCs
Urine protein/creatinine ratio: 0.08 mg/mg (normal)
Serum creatinine: 0.8 mg/dL (eGFR >90)
C3 and C4: normal; ANA negative
Renal ultrasound: normal-sized kidneys, no cysts, stones, or masses
Audiometry: normal hearing

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given isolated dysmorphic microscopic hematuria, normal renal function, no proteinuria, and a benign family history of hematuria without kidney failure, which diagnosis is most likely?
options: IgA nephropathy as the primary diagnosis
Alport syndrome as the primary diagnosis
Thin basement membrane disease
Bladder carcinoma as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Thin basement membrane disease (benign familial hematuria) classically causes persistent isolated microscopic hematuria with dysmorphic (glomerular) RBCs, normal renal function, minimal or no proteinuria, and a family history of hematuria WITHOUT progression to renal failure or extrarenal features. IgA nephropathy can cause hematuria but often features episodic gross hematuria (synpharyngitic), variable proteinuria, and can progress, so the bland, nonprogressive familial pattern here fits thin BM disease better. Alport syndrome causes hematuria but progresses to renal failure and is associated with sensorineural hearing loss and ocular abnormalities (lenticonus), absent here with a normal audiogram and benign family history. Bladder carcinoma is very unlikely in a young, asymptomatic, non-smoking woman with dysmorphic (glomerular) RBCs.
stages.explanationCorrect: Isolated dysmorphic hematuria with normal function, no proteinuria, and benign familial hematuria without renal failure or extrarenal disease is thin basement membrane disease.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: If a kidney biopsy were performed, what is the characteristic finding in thin basement membrane disease?
options: Splitting and lamellation ('basket-weave') of the GBM in this clinical context
Mesangial IgA deposition on immunofluorescence as the primary diagnosis
Subepithelial humps on electron microscopy as the primary diagnosis
Diffuse thinning of the glomerular basement membrane on electron microscopy
stages.explanation: Thin basement membrane disease is defined by diffuse, uniform thinning of the glomerular basement membrane on electron microscopy, with otherwise normal light microscopy and negative immunofluorescence. Splitting and lamellation of the GBM producing a 'basket-weave' appearance is the hallmark of Alport syndrome, which is progressive and has extrarenal features. Mesangial IgA deposition characterizes IgA nephropathy. Subepithelial humps are seen in post-infectious glomerulonephritis. Notably, biopsy is usually NOT required to diagnose thin basement membrane disease when the clinical picture is classic (isolated hematuria, normal function, no proteinuria, benign family history); the diagnosis is often clinical, and the patient is managed with observation.
stages.explanationCorrect: Thin basement membrane disease shows diffuse uniform GBM thinning on EM with normal light microscopy and negative immunofluorescence.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management plan for this patient?
options: Reassurance and periodic monitoring of blood pressure, urinalysis, and renal function
Begin chronic hemodialysis planning as initial therapy
Refer for nephrectomy as initial therapy
Start corticosteroids to suppress the hematuria as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Thin basement membrane disease has an excellent, benign prognosis, so management is reassurance plus periodic monitoring of blood pressure, urinalysis (for new proteinuria), and renal function to detect the rare patient who develops progressive disease. Corticosteroids are inappropriate because there is no active inflammatory or immune-mediated process to treat, and they expose the patient to needless harm. Dialysis planning is unwarranted in a patient with normal renal function and a nonprogressive condition. Nephrectomy is never indicated for benign glomerular hematuria. The key teaching point is recognizing a benign cause of isolated hematuria to avoid overtreatment, while still following the patient since a minority can have an Alport carrier state with later progression.
stages.explanationCorrect: Thin basement membrane disease is benign; reassurance with periodic monitoring of BP, urinalysis, and renal function is appropriate, avoiding needless treatment.

cases.diagnosis: Thin basement membrane disease (benign familial hematuria)
pearls: Thin BM disease = persistent isolated microscopic hematuria, normal function, little/no proteinuria, and benign familial hematuria without renal failure.
EM shows diffuse uniform GBM thinning; light microscopy and immunofluorescence are normal.
Distinguish from Alport (progressive, hearing loss, lenticonus, 'basket-weave' GBM) and IgA nephropathy (mesangial IgA, can progress).
Management is reassurance and periodic monitoring; biopsy and immunosuppression are usually unnecessary.

cases.title: A Teen with Bloody Urine and Trouble Hearing
cases.chiefComplaint: A teenage boy is evaluated for recurrent blood in the urine and worsening hearing.
cases.history: A 14-year-old boy is evaluated for recurrent episodes of visible blood in the urine, often after upper respiratory infections, plus persistent microscopic hematuria between episodes. His parents have also noticed he turns up the television volume and struggles to hear in noisy rooms over the past year. He has no dysuria, flank pain, or rash, but reports mild fatigue. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Family history is striking: his maternal uncle developed kidney failure requiring dialysis in his 30s and also wore hearing aids, and his mother has had 'a trace of blood' in her urine for years without kidney problems. He is a 9th-grade student and does not use tobacco or alcohol. Review of systems is positive for hematuria and progressive hearing difficulty and negative for joint pain, oral ulcers, and hemoptysis. On further questioning, an optometrist previously noted an unusual lens shape during an eye exam. He has had no prior nephrolithiasis, and his blood pressure has been creeping upward at recent checkups.
cases.exam: The adolescent is well-developed. Ophthalmologic exam reveals anterior lenticonus (conical protrusion of the lens) and a few perimacular flecks. Whisper test and bedside testing suggest high-frequency hearing loss. Cardiac and pulmonary exams are normal. Abdomen is soft and nontender with no palpable masses or organomegaly. There is no peripheral edema. Skin is clear without rash or purpura. Neurologic exam is otherwise normal. There is mild elevation of blood pressure for age, but no other systemic findings. No saddle-nose deformity or sinus tenderness is present.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Serum creatinine: 1.1 mg/dL (mildly elevated for age)
Urinalysis: blood 3+, protein 1+, dysmorphic RBCs, occasional RBC casts
Urine protein/creatinine ratio: 0.6 mg/mg
C3 and C4: normal; ANA negative
Renal ultrasound: normal-sized kidneys, increased echogenicity, no cysts
Audiometry: bilateral high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss
Ophthalmology: anterior lenticonus confirmed

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The combination of glomerular hematuria, progressive sensorineural hearing loss, and anterior lenticonus most strongly suggests which diagnosis?
options: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis
Thin basement membrane disease
IgA nephropathy in this clinical context
Alport syndrome
stages.explanation: The triad of progressive glomerular hematuria, sensorineural hearing loss, and ocular abnormalities (anterior lenticonus, perimacular flecks) is the classic presentation of Alport syndrome, a hereditary disorder of type IV collagen. Thin basement membrane disease causes isolated, nonprogressive hematuria WITHOUT hearing loss, ocular disease, or renal failure, and the prominent extrarenal features and rising creatinine here exclude it. IgA nephropathy can cause synpharyngitic gross hematuria but does not cause sensorineural hearing loss or lenticonus, and lacks this X-linked family pattern. Post-streptococcal GN is an acute, self-limited illness with low C3 and no chronic deafness or eye findings; complements here are normal. The extrarenal triad is the key to recognizing Alport syndrome.
stages.explanationCorrect: Glomerular hematuria + sensorineural hearing loss + anterior lenticonus is the classic Alport syndrome triad (type IV collagen defect).

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most common inheritance pattern and molecular defect in Alport syndrome?
options: X-linked defect in the COL4A5 gene
Autosomal recessive defect in type I collagen
Autosomal dominant defect in PKD1 as the primary diagnosis
Mitochondrial inheritance affecting tRNA in this clinical context
stages.explanation: The most common form of Alport syndrome (about 80%) is X-linked, caused by mutations in COL4A5, which encodes the alpha-5 chain of type IV collagen; this explains the more severe phenotype in males (like this boy and his maternal uncle) and milder, often hematuria-only disease in carrier females (like his mother). Type I collagen defects cause osteogenesis imperfecta and Ehlers-Danlos variants, not Alport syndrome. PKD1 mutations cause autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease, characterized by cysts rather than this hereditary nephritis with deafness. Mitochondrial tRNA mutations cause syndromes such as MELAS, not Alport syndrome. Autosomal recessive and dominant forms of Alport (COL4A3/COL4A4) exist but are less common than the X-linked COL4A5 form.
stages.explanationCorrect: Alport syndrome is most commonly X-linked from COL4A5 mutations (alpha-5 chain of type IV collagen), explaining severe disease in males and carrier hematuria in females.

stages.label: KIDNEY BIOPSY
stages.content:
Light microscopy: relatively unremarkable early; later glomerulosclerosis and tubular atrophy
Immunofluorescence: negative for immune complexes
Electron microscopy: irregular thickening, thinning, splitting, and lamellation of the GBM ('basket-weave' appearance)
Collagen IV alpha-5 staining: absent along the GBM (consistent with X-linked Alport)
Genetic testing: COL4A5 pathogenic variant identified

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which electron-microscopy feature best distinguishes this boy's Alport syndrome from thin basement membrane disease, which his carrier mother likely has?
options: Subepithelial humps as the immediate next step
Uniform diffuse thinning of the GBM only in this setting
Effacement of podocyte foot processes only in this setting
Irregular thickening, thinning,
stages.explanation: Alport syndrome shows irregular thickening, thinning, splitting, and lamellation of the GBM, the classic 'basket-weave' appearance, reflecting the abnormal type IV collagen network; this is the key EM finding that distinguishes progressive Alport disease from the benign thin basement membrane disease seen in many carrier females (including his mother), which shows only uniform diffuse GBM thinning. Subepithelial humps are seen in post-infectious GN, not Alport syndrome. Isolated foot-process effacement with an otherwise normal GBM characterizes minimal change disease, a pure nephrotic process unrelated to this hereditary nephritis. Importantly, early Alport disease can also show thinning, so absent alpha-5 collagen staining and genetic testing help confirm Alport when EM is ambiguous.
stages.explanationCorrect: Alport shows irregular GBM thickening/thinning/lamellation ('basket-weave'); thin BM disease shows only uniform diffuse GBM thinning.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which intervention best slows progression of kidney disease in Alport syndrome?
options: Lifelong corticosteroids as initial therapy
Cochlear implant to protect kidney function as initial therapy
Early initiation of ACE inhibitor/ARB therapy to reduce proteinuria
High-protein diet to support the kidneys as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Early ACE inhibitor or ARB therapy reduces proteinuria and intraglomerular pressure and is the cornerstone of slowing renal progression in Alport syndrome; it is started when proteinuria or hypertension develops and delays progression to end-stage kidney disease. Corticosteroids do not benefit Alport syndrome because it is a structural collagen disorder, not an immune-mediated glomerulonephritis. Cochlear implants address hearing loss but have no effect on renal progression. A high-protein diet increases glomerular hyperfiltration and proteinuria and would be harmful; moderate protein restriction is generally preferred in progressive CKD. Patients also need audiology and ophthalmology follow-up, blood pressure control, and counseling about progression to ESKD and the eventual need for transplantation.
stages.explanationCorrect: ACE inhibitor/ARB therapy lowers proteinuria and intraglomerular pressure and is the cornerstone for slowing renal progression in Alport syndrome.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: After kidney transplantation for ESKD, which rare complication is unique to some Alport patients?
options: BK virus nephropathy as the leading concern
Anti-GBM disease in the allograft against the now-foreign collagen
Recurrence of Alport syndrome in the allograft
Hyperacute rejection from ABO incompatibility in this presentation
stages.explanation: A small subset of Alport patients (especially those with severe X-linked disease lacking the alpha-5 chain) can develop de novo anti-GBM disease in the transplanted kidney: because their native basement membranes never expressed the normal collagen IV chains, their immune system recognizes the donor's normal GBM antigens as foreign and produces anti-GBM antibodies, causing crescentic GN in the allograft. Alport syndrome itself does NOT recur in the graft, since the donor kidney has normal collagen, making 'recurrence' incorrect. Hyperacute rejection from ABO incompatibility and BK virus nephropathy are general transplant complications not specific to Alport syndrome. This phenomenon, although uncommon, is a classic, high-yield association linking Alport syndrome to anti-GBM disease.
stages.explanationCorrect: Some Alport patients develop de novo anti-GBM disease in the allograft because their immune system sees the donor's normal collagen IV as foreign.

cases.diagnosis: Alport syndrome
pearls: Alport syndrome = hereditary type IV collagen defect: progressive hematuria + sensorineural hearing loss + ocular changes (anterior lenticonus).
Most common form is X-linked (COL4A5); males are severely affected, carrier females often have isolated hematuria.
EM shows GBM thickening, thinning, and splitting ('basket-weave'); ACE inhibitor/ARB slows progression.
After transplant, a minority develop de novo anti-GBM disease against the donor's normal (foreign) collagen IV.

cases.title: Flank Pain and High Blood Pressure in Midlife
cases.chiefComplaint: A woman presents with recurrent flank pain and newly elevated blood pressure.
cases.history: A 42-year-old woman presents to clinic with several months of intermittent bilateral flank/abdominal discomfort and a recent finding of elevated blood pressure at a pharmacy kiosk. She has had a couple of episodes of visible blood in the urine and one prior urinary tract infection. She denies fever currently, dysuria, or weight loss. PMH is otherwise unremarkable; she has never been formally diagnosed with hypertension. Medications: none. Allergies: none. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is significant: her father had 'large kidneys,' developed kidney failure in his 50s, and a paternal aunt died of a ruptured brain aneurysm (subarachnoid hemorrhage). She has two children and is concerned about whether they could be affected. Review of systems is positive for flank discomfort, occasional gross hematuria, and headaches, and negative for fevers, urinary obstruction, and abdominal trauma. She reports early satiety and a sense of abdominal fullness, and her blood pressure readings at home have consistently been elevated.
cases.exam: The patient is comfortable. Cardiac exam reveals a regular rhythm with no murmurs; lungs are clear. Abdominal exam is notable for bilateral palpable, irregular flank masses (enlarged kidneys) that are mildly tender to deep palpation; there is no rebound or guarding. There is no peripheral edema. Skin is clear. Neurologic exam is nonfocal with no cranial nerve deficits or focal weakness. There is mild costovertebral angle fullness bilaterally. No hepatomegaly is appreciated separate from the renal masses, and there are no abdominal bruits.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given palpable bilateral flank masses, hypertension, hematuria, and a family history of kidney failure, what is the best initial imaging study?
options: Renal ultrasound
Renal angiography
Plain abdominal radiograph
Voiding cystourethrogram
stages.explanation: Renal ultrasound is the best initial imaging study to evaluate for autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD): it is noninvasive, avoids radiation and contrast, and readily demonstrates multiple bilateral renal cysts and enlarged kidneys, and age-based cyst-count criteria can establish the diagnosis. A plain abdominal radiograph cannot characterize renal parenchyma or cysts and is not useful here. A voiding cystourethrogram evaluates vesicoureteral reflux and bladder/urethral anatomy, not renal cysts. Renal angiography is invasive and used to evaluate renal vasculature (e.g., renal artery stenosis), not cystic kidney disease; it is not an appropriate first test. If ultrasound is equivocal, CT or MRI can further characterize the kidneys and screen for complications.
stages.explanationCorrect: Renal ultrasound is the noninvasive first-line test for ADPKD, showing multiple bilateral cysts and enlarged kidneys with age-based diagnostic criteria.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Serum creatinine: 1.2 mg/dL (eGFR ~58)
Urinalysis: blood 1+, protein trace, no infection
Renal ultrasound: bilaterally enlarged kidneys with numerous cysts of varying sizes in both kidneys; a few hepatic cysts noted
Blood pressure (clinic): 156/98 mmHg
Family history: father with ESKD and 'large kidneys'; paternal aunt with fatal subarachnoid hemorrhage

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: This patient most likely has autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease. Which extrarenal complication should prompt consideration of screening given her family history?
options: Pulmonary arteriovenous malformation
Intracranial (berry) aneurysm
Medullary thyroid carcinoma
Pheochromocytoma
stages.explanation: ADPKD is associated with intracranial 'berry' aneurysms, and the risk is higher in patients with a family history of aneurysm or subarachnoid hemorrhage, as in this patient (paternal aunt with fatal SAH). Such patients warrant screening with MR angiography of the brain. Pheochromocytoma is associated with MEN2, neurofibromatosis, and von Hippel-Lindau disease, not ADPKD. Medullary thyroid carcinoma is a feature of MEN2, not ADPKD. Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations are characteristic of hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (Osler-Weber-Rendu), not ADPKD. Other ADPKD-associated features include hepatic and pancreatic cysts, mitral valve prolapse, colonic diverticula, and abdominal wall/inguinal hernias.
stages.explanationCorrect: ADPKD is associated with intracranial berry aneurysms; screening with MR angiography is warranted given a family history of aneurysm/subarachnoid hemorrhage.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most important intervention to slow progression of kidney disease in ADPKD?
options: Aggressive blood pressure control, typically with an ACE inhibitor or ARB
Routine surgical cyst decortication as initial therapy
High-protein diet as initial therapy
Chronic prophylactic antibiotics as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Rigorous blood pressure control, preferably with an ACE inhibitor or ARB, is the most important intervention to slow progression and reduce cardiovascular risk in ADPKD; hypertension is common and accelerates decline. Adequate hydration and, in selected rapidly progressing patients, the vasopressin antagonist tolvaptan can further slow cyst growth and GFR loss. Surgical cyst decortication does not preserve overall renal function and is reserved for symptomatic pain relief in specific cases. Chronic prophylactic antibiotics are not standard; infections are treated when they occur (with cyst-penetrating agents). A high-protein diet promotes hyperfiltration and is not recommended; moderate protein and sodium restriction is preferred. Genetic counseling and screening of at-risk relatives are also important.
stages.explanationCorrect: Tight BP control with an ACE inhibitor/ARB is the most important intervention to slow ADPKD progression; tolvaptan helps selected rapidly progressing patients.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The patient asks about her children. What is the inheritance risk for each child of an affected parent in ADPKD?
options: 25% with appropriate follow-up
Negligible unless both parents are carriers
100% of sons only
stages.explanation: ADPKD follows autosomal dominant inheritance, so each child of an affected parent has a 50% chance of inheriting the disease, regardless of sex. A 25% risk corresponds to autosomal recessive conditions (where both parents are carriers), which is the pattern of autosomal recessive polycystic kidney disease, not ADPKD. '100% of sons only' describes neither ADPKD nor any standard pattern; X-linked recessive disease affects sons of carrier mothers differently, but ADPKD is not X-linked. ADPKD does not require both parents to be carriers, so 'negligible unless both parents are carriers' is incorrect. Genetic counseling and discussion of screening (often deferred until adulthood when management decisions arise) are appropriate for at-risk children.
stages.explanationCorrect: ADPKD is autosomal dominant, so each child of an affected parent has a 50% chance of inheriting it, regardless of sex.

cases.diagnosis: Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease
pearls: ADPKD: bilateral enlarged cystic kidneys, hypertension, hematuria, flank pain, and a positive family history; mostly PKD1/PKD2 mutations.
Screen for intracranial berry aneurysms (MRA) when there is a family history of aneurysm or subarachnoid hemorrhage.
Tight BP control with ACE inhibitor/ARB slows progression; tolvaptan helps selected rapidly progressing patients.
Autosomal dominant inheritance means each child has a 50% risk; associated with hepatic cysts, MVP, and diverticulosis.

cases.title: Weakness and Palpitations in a Dialysis Patient
cases.chiefComplaint: A dialysis patient arrives in the ED with weakness and palpitations after missing treatments.
cases.history: A 61-year-old man with end-stage kidney disease on maintenance hemodialysis (Monday/Wednesday/Friday schedule) presents to the ED with generalized weakness, muscle cramps, and palpitations. He missed his last two dialysis sessions because of a family emergency and transportation problems, so it has been about a week since his last treatment. He admits to eating bananas, oranges, and a large amount of potatoes over the past several days. He denies chest pain, fever, or focal neurologic deficits but reports nausea. PMH includes hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and ESKD on hemodialysis via an arteriovenous fistula. Medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily, amlodipine 10 mg daily, insulin glargine, sevelamer with meals; he ran out of his potassium-related dietary counseling materials. Allergies: none. He is a former smoker. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for weakness, cramps, palpitations, and nausea, and negative for chest pain, syncope, and shortness of breath at rest. His fistula has a good thrill on self-exam.
cases.exam: The patient appears uncomfortable but is alert and oriented. Cardiac exam reveals bradycardia with an irregular rhythm and no murmur. Lungs are clear bilaterally. The arteriovenous fistula in the left forearm has a palpable thrill and audible bruit. Abdomen is soft and nontender. There is generalized mild proximal muscle weakness and diminished deep tendon reflexes. There is no focal neurologic deficit and no peripheral edema beyond trace at the ankles. Skin is intact. The patient is mentating normally but visibly fatigued.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In a dialysis patient who missed sessions and is now bradycardic and weak, which test is most urgent to obtain immediately?
options: Electrocardiogram (ECG)
Echocardiogram
CT head without contrast
Chest X-ray in this clinical context
stages.explanation: An immediate ECG is the most urgent test because hyperkalemia, strongly suspected here (missed dialysis, high-potassium diet, ACE inhibitor use, bradycardia, weakness), causes life-threatening cardiac conduction abnormalities that the ECG can detect within seconds: peaked T waves, PR prolongation, widened QRS, loss of P waves, and ultimately a sine wave and arrest. The ECG guides emergent treatment decisions while labs are pending. A chest X-ray evaluates pulmonary edema or pneumonia but does not address the acute arrhythmia risk. An echocardiogram is not the emergent first step for suspected hyperkalemia. A CT head is unwarranted without focal neurologic findings and would dangerously delay recognition of a cardiac emergency.
stages.explanationCorrect: An immediate ECG detects life-threatening hyperkalemic conduction changes (peaked T waves, wide QRS) within seconds and guides emergent treatment.

stages.label: ECG & LABS
stages.content:
ECG: peaked (tented) T waves, prolonged PR interval, widened QRS complexes, flattened P waves
Serum potassium: 7.4 mEq/L
Bicarbonate: 16 mEq/L
Serum creatinine: 9.8 mg/dL
BUN: 92 mg/dL
Calcium: 8.6 mg/dL
Glucose: 150 mg/dL
pH: 7.28

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: With a potassium of 7.4 mEq/L and ECG changes, what is the FIRST medication to administer?
options: Oral sodium polystyrene sulfonate
Intravenous regular insulin with dextrose
Nebulized albuterol as initial therapy
Intravenous calcium gluconate
stages.explanation: When hyperkalemia produces ECG changes, the FIRST step is IV calcium gluconate (or calcium chloride), which stabilizes the cardiac myocyte membrane and rapidly antagonizes the arrhythmogenic effect of potassium, buying time. Calcium does NOT lower the potassium level; it protects the heart. IV insulin with dextrose shifts potassium intracellularly and is given next, along with nebulized albuterol, which also drives potassium into cells. Sodium polystyrene sulfonate (or newer binders like patiromer/sodium zirconium cyclosilicate) removes potassium from the body but acts slowly via the gut and is not the immediate life-saving step. The definitive treatment in this anuric dialysis patient is urgent hemodialysis to actually remove the potassium.
stages.explanationCorrect: With ECG changes, give IV calcium gluconate FIRST to stabilize the cardiac membrane; it protects the heart but does not lower potassium.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After membrane stabilization, which combination best shifts potassium intracellularly while awaiting definitive treatment?
options: Oral potassium binder alone as initial therapy
Insulin with dextrose plus a beta-2 agonist
Calcium gluconate repeated every 5 minutes as initial therapy
Loop diuretic plus normal saline bolus as initial therapy
stages.explanation: After calcium stabilizes the membrane, the next goal is to temporarily shift potassium into cells: IV regular insulin with dextrose (to prevent hypoglycemia) is the mainstay, a nebulized beta-2 agonist (albuterol) adds further intracellular shift, and sodium bicarbonate can help when significant metabolic acidosis is present. A loop diuretic plus saline relies on renal potassium excretion, which is futile in an anuric dialysis patient. An oral potassium binder removes potassium but works too slowly for an acute emergency and does not shift it. Repeated calcium gluconate only continues membrane stabilization and does not lower or shift potassium. Definitive removal still requires emergent hemodialysis given his ESKD and anuria.
stages.explanationCorrect: Insulin with dextrose plus a beta-2 agonist (and bicarbonate if acidotic) shifts potassium into cells; diuretics are futile in an anuric dialysis patient.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the definitive treatment to actually remove potassium from this anuric dialysis patient?
options: Emergent hemodialysis
Increased dose of oral potassium binder
Aggressive IV fluid hydration
Continued IV insulin infusion overnight
stages.explanation: Because this patient is anuric with ESKD, emergent hemodialysis is the definitive treatment to actually remove the excess potassium from the body; the temporizing measures (calcium, insulin/dextrose, albuterol, bicarbonate) only stabilize the heart and transiently shift potassium intracellularly, after which it can rebound. A continued insulin infusion only keeps shifting potassium and risks hypoglycemia without removing it. Increasing the oral binder dose works too slowly and unreliably for a severe, symptomatic emergency. Aggressive IV fluids are dangerous in an anuric dialysis patient and will not remove potassium, risking volume overload and pulmonary edema. Arranging urgent dialysis is the priority once the patient is temporarily stabilized.
stages.explanationCorrect: In an anuric ESKD patient, emergent hemodialysis is the definitive treatment to remove potassium; temporizing measures only stabilize and shift it.

cases.diagnosis: Hyperkalemia (life-threatening, in a missed-dialysis ESKD patient)
pearls: ECG progression of hyperkalemia: peaked T waves → PR prolongation/wide QRS → loss of P waves → sine wave → arrest.
Treatment order: (1) IV calcium to stabilize the membrane, (2) insulin/dextrose + albuterol (± bicarbonate) to shift K+, (3) remove K+.
Calcium protects the heart but does NOT lower potassium; binders/dialysis remove it from the body.
Emergent hemodialysis is definitive in anuric ESKD patients; diuretics and fluids are ineffective or harmful.

cases.title: Weakness and Leg Cramps After a Stomach Bug
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive generalized weakness and muscle cramps for two days.
cases.history: A 45-year-old woman presents to the ED with two days of worsening generalized weakness, leg cramps, and fatigue. She reports a stomach virus one week ago with several days of vomiting, and she has continued to feel nauseated. She has hypertension and lower-extremity edema, for which she takes furosemide 40 mg twice daily and recently doubled her own dose to reduce ankle swelling. She also takes occasional over-the-counter laxatives for constipation. She admits to restricting fluids because eating and drinking make her nauseated. She denies chest pain, but notes intermittent palpitations. No diarrhea currently. PMH: hypertension, mild diastolic dysfunction. Medications: furosemide 80 mg twice daily (self-escalated), amlodipine 5 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: works as a teacher, drinks alcohol socially, does not smoke. Family history: hypertension in both parents. She has never had her potassium checked outside routine visits and stopped her last clinic appointment due to scheduling conflicts. Review of systems is positive for constipation and mild palpitations.
cases.exam: The patient is alert but appears tired. Mucous membranes are dry and skin turgor is slightly reduced. Cardiac exam reveals a regular tachycardia without murmurs or extra heart sounds. Lungs are clear. The abdomen is soft, mildly distended, with hypoactive bowel sounds and no tenderness. Neuromuscular exam shows symmetric 4/5 proximal muscle strength in the lower extremities with diminished deep tendon reflexes; sensation is intact. There is trace pedal edema. No focal neurologic deficits, no asterixis. Capillary refill is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given her diuretic use, vomiting, and muscle weakness, which initial test is most important to obtain first?
options: Serum lactate as the first step today
Basic metabolic panel including potassium
Chest radiograph as the first step today
Serum lipase as the first step today
stages.explanation: A basic metabolic panel with potassium is the priority: her loop diuretic overuse plus vomiting are classic causes of potassium loss, and her weakness, cramps, hyporeflexia, and palpitations point to an electrolyte derangement that must be quantified immediately. Serum lipase would evaluate pancreatitis, which is not suggested by her painless presentation. A chest radiograph does not address the electrolyte question driving her symptoms. Serum lactate screens for tissue hypoperfusion or sepsis, but she is not septic and her vitals do not suggest shock. Identifying and correcting the electrolyte abnormality is both diagnostic and potentially life-saving given arrhythmia risk.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Na 138 mEq/L
K 2.6 mEq/L
Cl 92 mEq/L
HCO3 32 mEq/L
BUN 28 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL
Glucose 96 mg/dL
Mg 1.8 mg/dL
Calcium 9.2 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: An ECG is obtained. Which finding is most characteristic of her electrolyte abnormality?
options: Delta waves in this clinical context
U waves and flattened T waves
Peaked T waves
Shortened QT interval
stages.explanation: Hypokalemia classically produces flattened or inverted T waves, ST depression, and prominent U waves, with QT prolongation and increased arrhythmia risk. Peaked T waves are the hallmark of hyperkalemia, the opposite derangement. A shortened QT interval is seen with hypercalcemia, not hypokalemia. Delta waves indicate ventricular pre-excitation in Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome and are unrelated to potassium. Recognizing U waves on her ECG corroborates the laboratory finding of a potassium of 2.6 mEq/L and underscores the urgency of repletion before a dangerous arrhythmia develops.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: With a potassium of 2.6 mEq/L, symptoms, and ECG changes, what is the most appropriate management?
options: IV potassium chloride with cardiac monitoring, plus checking
Oral potassium chloride alone and discharge home as monotherapy
IV insulin and dextrose as initial therapy
Sodium polystyrene sulfonate as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Symptomatic hypokalemia with ECG changes warrants IV potassium chloride with continuous cardiac monitoring; concurrently magnesium should be checked and repleted because hypomagnesemia promotes ongoing renal potassium wasting and makes hypokalemia refractory. Oral potassium alone with discharge is inadequate for symptomatic, ECG-positive hypokalemia at this level. Sodium polystyrene sulfonate lowers potassium and would be dangerous here. IV insulin and dextrose drive potassium into cells, treating hyperkalemia, and would worsen her already low potassium. Combined IV repletion with magnesium correction and monitoring is the standard approach.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which intervention best prevents recurrence after her potassium is corrected?
options: Start sodium polystyrene sulfonate daily with appropriate follow-up
Reduce the loop diuretic, address volume status,
Continue self-escalated furosemide 80 mg twice daily with appropriate follow-up
Restrict all dietary potassium with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Recurrence is best prevented by correcting the underlying cause: her self-escalated loop diuretic plus vomiting and poor intake drove urinary and gastrointestinal potassium loss. Reducing the diuretic dose, restoring euvolemia, and considering a potassium-sparing diuretic with dietary counseling addresses the mechanism. Continuing 80 mg twice daily would perpetuate renal potassium wasting. Sodium polystyrene sulfonate lowers potassium and is contraindicated. Restricting dietary potassium is exactly backward and would deepen the deficit. The goal is to remove the offending driver and support potassium balance going forward.

cases.diagnosis: Hypokalemia due to loop diuretic overuse and vomiting
pearls: Vomiting causes hypokalemia both directly (gastric K loss) and indirectly via metabolic alkalosis and secondary hyperaldosteronism driving renal K wasting.
Always check and replete magnesium in hypokalemia; hypomagnesemia causes refractory renal potassium loss.
ECG signs of hypokalemia include flattened T waves, ST depression, U waves, and QT prolongation, raising arrhythmia risk.
Symptomatic hypokalemia or K below ~2.5–3.0 mEq/L with ECG changes warrants IV repletion with cardiac monitoring.

cases.title: Confusion and Low Sodium in a Man With Lung Cancer
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive confusion and lethargy over the past week.
cases.history: A 67-year-old man admitted for evaluation of weight loss and a lung mass develops worsening confusion and lethargy over the past week. His family reports he has become forgetful and unsteady. He has a 50-pack-year smoking history. Recent imaging showed a central lung mass with mediastinal lymphadenopathy, and a biopsy is pending. He denies vomiting, diarrhea, or excessive sweating, and has had normal oral intake without fluid restriction. He has not been on diuretics. PMH: COPD, hypertension.
