Medications: tiotropium inhaler, lisinopril 10 mg daily; no recent changes. Allergies: none. Social: retired factory worker, current smoker, drinks alcohol rarely. Family history: father with lung cancer. On review of systems he reports mild headache and nausea but denies orthostatic dizziness, chest pain, or shortness of breath at rest. There is no history of heart failure, cirrhosis, or kidney disease. He has not received hypotonic IV fluids. Nursing notes describe him as intermittently disoriented to time but arousable and cooperative.
cases.exam: The patient is somnolent but arousable and oriented only to person. Mucous membranes are moist and skin turgor is normal. There is no jugular venous distention and no peripheral edema. Cardiac exam is regular without murmurs. Lung exam reveals decreased breath sounds at the right apex. The abdomen is soft and nontender without ascites or organomegaly. Neurologic exam shows no focal deficits; gait is mildly unsteady. There is no asterixis. Capillary refill is normal and the patient appears clinically euvolemic with no signs of dehydration or fluid overload.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His basic metabolic panel returns sodium of 122 mEq/L. What is the most important next step to classify his hyponatremia?
options: Order a head CT to look for cerebral edema as the first step today
Assess volume status
Empirically restrict fluids and recheck in 24 hours
Administer 3% hypertonic saline immediately as the first step today
stages.explanation: Classifying hyponatremia requires assessing volume status alongside serum osmolality, urine osmolality, and urine sodium, which together distinguish SIADH from hypovolemic and hypervolemic causes. Empiric fluid restriction without data risks treating the wrong process. Hypertonic saline is reserved for severe symptoms such as seizures or coma, not mild confusion at this stage, and giving it blindly is unsafe. A head CT may be appropriate if focal deficits or trauma were present, but it would not explain or classify the hyponatremia. The osmolality and urine studies are the cornerstone of the diagnostic workup.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Na 122 mEq/L
K 4.0 mEq/L
Serum osmolality 258 mOsm/kg
Urine osmolality 480 mOsm/kg
Urine sodium 65 mEq/L
TSH 2.1 µIU/mL
8 a.m. cortisol 16 µg/dL
BUN 8 mg/dL
Creatinine 0.7 mg/dL
Uric acid 2.4 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on euvolemia, low serum osmolality, and these urine studies, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion
Hypovolemic hyponatremia from poor intake
Psychogenic polydipsia as the primary diagnosis
Hypothyroidism causing hyponatremia in this clinical context
stages.explanation: SIADH is defined by euvolemic hypotonic hyponatremia with inappropriately concentrated urine (osmolality above 100, here 480) and elevated urine sodium (above 30, here 65) in a patient with normal thyroid and adrenal function. His low BUN and low uric acid further support SIADH. Hypovolemic hyponatremia would show low urine sodium and clinical dehydration, neither present. Psychogenic polydipsia produces maximally dilute urine (osmolality below 100), not concentrated urine. Hypothyroidism is excluded by a normal TSH. His small cell lung cancer is a classic ectopic ADH source, completing the clinical picture.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is mildly confused but not seizing. What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
options: Large-volume normal saline boluses as monotherapy
Start a thiazide diuretic as initial therapy
Fluid restriction with close monitoring of sodium
Rapid correction with 3% saline to normal sodium within 6 hours
stages.explanation: For chronic SIADH with mild-to-moderate symptoms, fluid restriction is first-line, with careful monitoring to avoid overly rapid correction. Rapid correction to normal within 6 hours risks osmotic demyelination syndrome; the safe limit is roughly 6–8 mEq/L per 24 hours in chronic hyponatremia. Large-volume normal saline can paradoxically worsen SIADH because the kidney retains free water while excreting the sodium load. Thiazide diuretics impair urinary dilution and characteristically cause or worsen hyponatremia. Treating the underlying malignancy and restricting free water are the cornerstones, with vaptans or salt tablets considered if restriction fails.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Over the next 12 hours his sodium rises from 122 to 134 mEq/L. What complication is he at greatest risk for?
options: Acute kidney injury
Hyperkalemia in this presentation
Cerebral edema
Osmotic demyelination syndrome
stages.explanation: A 12 mEq/L rise in 12 hours far exceeds the safe correction limit of about 6–8 mEq/L per 24 hours, placing him at high risk for osmotic demyelination syndrome (central pontine myelinolysis), which presents days later with dysarthria, dysphagia, and quadriparesis. Cerebral edema results from rapid drops in sodium, not rapid correction. Hyperkalemia is unrelated to the rate of sodium correction. Acute kidney injury is not a direct consequence of overcorrection. The appropriate response is to relower sodium with hypotonic fluids and/or desmopressin to bring the 24-hour rise back within target.

cases.diagnosis: SIADH secondary to small cell lung cancer
pearls: SIADH is euvolemic hypotonic hyponatremia with urine osmolality >100 mOsm/kg and urine sodium >30 mEq/L, with normal thyroid and adrenal function.
Low BUN and low serum uric acid are supportive clues for SIADH.
Small cell lung cancer is a classic cause of ectopic ADH production.
Correct chronic hyponatremia by no more than ~6–8 mEq/L per 24 hours to avoid osmotic demyelination syndrome.

cases.title: Lethargy and Dry Skin in a Nursing Home Resident
cases.chiefComplaint: Increasing lethargy and decreased responsiveness noted by nursing home staff.
cases.history: An 83-year-old woman with advanced dementia is admitted from her nursing home for several days of worsening lethargy and poor oral intake. Staff report she rarely asks for water and must be prompted to drink. Over the past week she had a febrile respiratory illness with reduced appetite. She is bed-bound and dependent for all activities of daily living. There is no reported vomiting or diarrhea, and she is not on diuretics. PMH: Alzheimer dementia, hypertension, osteoarthritis. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, acetaminophen as needed; no lithium, no diuretics. Allergies: none known. Social: lives in a skilled nursing facility, never smoked, no alcohol. Family history: noncontributory. On review of systems, family notes she has seemed weaker and less verbal, with fewer wet diapers recently. There is no history of diabetes insipidus, head trauma, or pituitary disease. Nursing flowsheets show steadily declining fluid intake over the prior five days. She has no indwelling catheter and urine output appears reduced and concentrated.
cases.exam: The patient is lethargic, arousable to voice, and not oriented at baseline per dementia. Mucous membranes are very dry, and skin turgor is markedly decreased with tenting. Eyes appear sunken. Cardiac exam shows sinus tachycardia without murmurs. Lungs have scattered crackles at the right base. The abdomen is soft and nontender. There is no peripheral edema and no jugular venous distention. Neurologic exam shows generalized weakness without focal deficits; no asterixis is appreciated. Capillary refill is slightly prolonged and extremities are cool, consistent with volume depletion.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Initial labs show a sodium of 162 mEq/L. What is the most likely underlying mechanism in this patient?
options: Primary hyperaldosteronism causing sodium retention
Excess free water intake as the first step today
Cerebral salt wasting as the first step today
Inadequate free water intake with ongoing insensible
stages.explanation: In a bed-bound dementia patient with impaired thirst and reduced access to water, hypernatremia almost always reflects a free water deficit from inadequate intake while insensible (fever, respiratory) and renal losses continue. Excess free water intake would cause hyponatremia, the opposite. Primary hyperaldosteronism raises sodium only mildly and would not produce a value of 162 with clinical dehydration. Cerebral salt wasting causes hyponatremia with volume depletion, not hypernatremia. The hallmark of geriatric hypernatremia is impaired thirst and limited access to free water, exactly her situation, made worse by her recent febrile illness.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Na 162 mEq/L
K 4.4 mEq/L
Cl 128 mEq/L
HCO3 24 mEq/L
BUN 48 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.5 mg/dL
Glucose 110 mg/dL
Serum osmolality 334 mOsm/kg
Urine osmolality 720 mOsm/kg
Urine sodium 18 mEq/L
Chest radiograph: right lower lobe infiltrate

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Her urine osmolality is 720 mOsm/kg. What does this concentrated urine indicate about her renal water handling?
options: Diabetes insipidus with failure to concentrate urine
Osmotic diuresis from glucose as the primary diagnosis
Primary polydipsia as the primary diagnosis
Appropriate ADH response with intact renal water conservation
stages.explanation: A urine osmolality of 720 mOsm/kg in the face of hypernatremia indicates an appropriate ADH response, with the kidneys maximally conserving water; the problem is purely inadequate intake, not a renal concentrating defect. Diabetes insipidus would produce inappropriately dilute urine (low osmolality) despite hypernatremia. Osmotic diuresis from glucose is excluded by her normal glucose of 110. Primary polydipsia causes hyponatremia with dilute urine, not hypernatremia. The concentrated urine confirms her kidneys are doing the right thing, and treatment should focus on replacing the free water deficit.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is hemodynamically borderline with signs of volume depletion. What is the most appropriate initial fluid strategy?
options: Administer 3% hypertonic saline as initial therapy
Rapidly correct sodium to normal within 12 hours using free water as initial therapy
Restore intravascular volume with isotonic saline, then correct the free water deficit slowly with hypotonic fluid
Begin desmopressin as initial therapy
stages.explanation: In hypernatremia with hemodynamic compromise, the first priority is restoring intravascular volume with isotonic saline, after which the free water deficit is corrected gradually with hypotonic fluid (e.g., D5W or half-normal saline). Sodium should fall by no more than about 10–12 mEq/L per 24 hours to avoid cerebral edema. Rapid correction within 12 hours risks fatal cerebral edema. Hypertonic saline would worsen hypernatremia. Desmopressin treats diabetes insipidus, which she does not have, given her appropriately concentrated urine. Stabilize volume first, then correct water slowly.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the maximum safe rate of sodium correction to avoid neurologic complications in her chronic hypernatremia?
options: About 10–12 mEq/L per 24 hours
Correct fully within 6 hours
About 25 mEq/L per 24 hours
No limit is needed for hypernatremia
stages.explanation: Chronic hypernatremia should be corrected slowly, lowering sodium by no more than approximately 10–12 mEq/L per 24 hours (roughly 0.5 mEq/L per hour) to prevent cerebral edema and seizures, because brain cells have generated idiogenic osmoles to adapt. A rate of 25 mEq/L per day is dangerously fast. Full correction within 6 hours would precipitate cerebral edema. The notion that no limit is needed is incorrect; overly rapid water replacement is the major iatrogenic risk in treating hypernatremia.

cases.diagnosis: Hypernatremia from free water deficit (impaired thirst and access)
pearls: Hypernatremia almost always implies impaired thirst or restricted water access, since intact thirst is highly protective.
In hypernatremia, concentrated urine (high osmolality) signals an appropriate ADH response and points to a water-intake problem rather than diabetes insipidus.
Restore intravascular volume with isotonic fluid first if hemodynamically unstable, then correct the free water deficit with hypotonic fluid.
Correct chronic hypernatremia slowly—no more than ~10–12 mEq/L per 24 hours—to avoid cerebral edema.

cases.title: Excessive Urination After Brain Surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: Profuse urination and intense thirst beginning the day after surgery.
cases.history: A 38-year-old man is recovering on the ward after transsphenoidal resection of a pituitary macroadenoma the previous day. Overnight, nursing staff noted he produced more than 5 liters of dilute urine and complained of unrelenting thirst, repeatedly asking for water. He feels weak and lightheaded when standing. There has been no diarrhea or vomiting and he is not receiving diuretics or large IV fluid volumes beyond maintenance. PMH: pituitary macroadenoma with prior visual field changes; otherwise healthy. Medications: postoperative stress-dose hydrocortisone, acetaminophen; no lithium. Allergies: none. Social: software developer, nonsmoker, occasional alcohol. Family history: noncontributory. On review of systems he reports dry mouth and a strong craving for cold water but denies headache, fever, or vision changes today. He has an indwelling urinary catheter with high hourly outputs documented. Surgical team is monitoring strict intake and output. There is no history of diabetes mellitus and preoperative glucose values were normal.
cases.exam: The patient is alert and oriented but appears thirsty, repeatedly requesting water. Mucous membranes are dry. Cardiac exam reveals mild sinus tachycardia without murmurs. Lungs are clear. The surgical nasal packing is in place without active bleeding or CSF rhinorrhea. The abdomen is soft and nontender. Neurologic exam shows intact extraocular movements and grossly intact visual fields; no focal deficits. There is mild orthostatic lightheadedness on standing. Skin turgor is slightly reduced. The urinary catheter bag fills rapidly with clear, pale urine.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given polyuria and polydipsia after pituitary surgery, which initial labs best characterize the problem?
options: Coagulation panel as the first step today
Serum and urine osmolality with serum sodium
Liver function tests as the first step today
Serum lipase and amylase
stages.explanation: Post-pituitary-surgery polyuria with intense thirst suggests diabetes insipidus, best characterized by simultaneous serum sodium, serum osmolality, and urine osmolality: the expected pattern is rising serum sodium and osmolality with inappropriately dilute urine. Serum lipase and amylase assess pancreatitis, irrelevant here. Liver function tests do not address water handling. A coagulation panel would matter for bleeding but does not explain polyuria. The osmolality pair plus sodium is the foundational test set to confirm diabetes insipidus and gauge severity, especially important after pituitary manipulation that can damage ADH-producing pathways.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Na 148 mEq/L
Serum osmolality 302 mOsm/kg
Urine osmolality 110 mOsm/kg
Urine specific gravity 1.003
Glucose 98 mg/dL
K 4.0 mEq/L
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
24-hour urine output 6.2 L

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A water deprivation test is performed. Urine osmolality stays low after fluid restriction but rises substantially after desmopressin. What does this confirm?
options: Primary polydipsia
Osmotic diuresis
Central diabetes insipidus
Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus
stages.explanation: Failure to concentrate urine during water deprivation, followed by a robust rise in urine osmolality after exogenous desmopressin, confirms central diabetes insipidus: the kidneys can respond to ADH, but the pituitary cannot produce it—expected after transsphenoidal surgery. In nephrogenic DI, urine osmolality remains low even after desmopressin because the kidneys cannot respond. Primary polydipsia would show appropriate urine concentration with water deprivation alone. Osmotic diuresis is excluded by normal glucose and low urine specific gravity. The desmopressin response cleanly localizes the defect to ADH deficiency.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment for his central diabetes insipidus?
options: Hydrochlorothiazide as initial therapy
Fluid restriction alone as initial therapy
Indomethacin as initial therapy
Desmopressin with free water access
stages.explanation: Central DI is treated with desmopressin (a synthetic ADH analog) to replace the missing hormone, alongside ensuring adequate free water access and monitoring sodium to avoid overcorrection or iatrogenic hyponatremia. Hydrochlorothiazide is used adjunctively in nephrogenic DI, not as primary therapy for central DI. Fluid restriction alone is dangerous in a patient with massive obligate water loss and would worsen hypernatremia. Indomethacin is an adjunct in nephrogenic DI. Replacing ADH with desmopressin directly corrects the deficiency and abolishes the inappropriate water loss.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: After starting desmopressin, what is the most important parameter to monitor to prevent iatrogenic harm?
options: Serum calcium for hypercalcemia
Serum sodium for overcorrection
Serum potassium for hyperkalemia
Liver enzymes for hepatotoxicity
stages.explanation: After desmopressin, the chief risk is over-retention of free water leading to hyponatremia, so serial serum sodium monitoring is essential to titrate dosing and free water intake. Hyperkalemia is not a recognized complication of desmopressin therapy. Hypercalcemia is unrelated to ADH replacement. Desmopressin is not hepatotoxic, so liver enzyme monitoring is not the priority. Especially in the postoperative pituitary patient, the classic triphasic response (DI, then transient SIADH, then permanent DI) makes vigilant sodium monitoring critical to avoid swinging into dangerous hyponatremia.

cases.diagnosis: Central diabetes insipidus after transsphenoidal pituitary surgery
pearls: Central DI presents with polyuria, dilute urine, and rising serum sodium/osmolality; it responds to desmopressin on water deprivation testing.
In the water deprivation test, central DI shows a urine osmolality rise after desmopressin, whereas nephrogenic DI does not.
Pituitary or hypothalamic surgery is a classic cause; watch for a triphasic DI–SIADH–DI pattern.
Treat with desmopressin and monitor serum sodium closely to prevent iatrogenic hyponatremia.

cases.title: Constant Thirst and Urination on a Mood Stabilizer
cases.chiefComplaint: Excessive thirst and frequent urination, including waking multiple times at night.
cases.history: A 54-year-old man presents to clinic with several months of intense thirst and frequent, high-volume urination, including waking three to four times nightly to void. He carries a water bottle everywhere and estimates he drinks more than 4 liters daily. He has bipolar disorder, well controlled on lithium for the past nine years. He denies dysuria, hematuria, weight loss, or blurry vision. PMH: bipolar I disorder, hypertension. Medications: lithium carbonate 900 mg daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: accountant, nonsmoker, rare alcohol. Family history: mother with type 2 diabetes. On review of systems he reports daytime fatigue from disrupted sleep but no fever or flank pain. His most recent lithium level was therapeutic. He has not had recent illness, vomiting, or diarrhea. He is concerned about whether he has developed diabetes given his mother's history, but home glucose checks borrowed from his mother's meter have been normal.
cases.exam: The patient is well appearing and in no distress. Mucous membranes are slightly dry. Cardiac and lung exams are normal. The abdomen is soft and nontender without masses or organomegaly; there is no costovertebral angle tenderness. Neurologic exam shows no tremor, and reflexes are normal. There is no peripheral edema. He appears clinically euvolemic to mildly volume depleted. No thyromegaly is noted. The remainder of the focused examination, including skin and extremities, is unremarkable, and he denies any neurologic symptoms suggestive of lithium toxicity.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which first set of tests best distinguishes the cause of his polyuria?
options: Renal ultrasound only as the first step today
Serum sodium, serum and urine osmolality, and glucose
Urine culture as the first step today
Fasting glucose and HbA1c only in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Polyuria workup begins with serum sodium, serum and urine osmolality, and glucose to separate osmotic diuresis (e.g., diabetes mellitus) from a water diuresis (diabetes insipidus or primary polydipsia). Fasting glucose and HbA1c alone would catch diabetes mellitus but miss DI. A urine culture evaluates infection, which he does not have. A renal ultrasound may show chronic lithium changes but does not characterize the polyuria physiology. The osmolality panel with glucose efficiently localizes the problem; given his lithium use, nephrogenic DI is high on the differential and must be confirmed physiologically.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Na 146 mEq/L
Serum osmolality 298 mOsm/kg
Urine osmolality 130 mOsm/kg
Glucose 94 mg/dL
HbA1c 5.4%
K 4.2 mEq/L
Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL
Lithium level 0.8 mEq/L (therapeutic)
Urine specific gravity 1.004

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: After fluid restriction his urine stays dilute, and urine osmolality does NOT rise after desmopressin. What is the diagnosis?
options: Primary polydipsia
Diabetes mellitus
Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus
Central diabetes insipidus
stages.explanation: Failure of urine to concentrate after desmopressin indicates the kidneys cannot respond to ADH, defining nephrogenic diabetes insipidus; lithium is the classic culprit, impairing collecting-duct aquaporin-2 responsiveness. Central DI would show a urine osmolality rise after desmopressin because the kidney can respond once hormone is supplied. Primary polydipsia would concentrate urine with water deprivation alone. Diabetes mellitus is excluded by a normal glucose and HbA1c. The combination of chronic lithium use and a flat desmopressin response is essentially diagnostic of lithium-induced nephrogenic DI.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: In coordination with psychiatry, what is the most appropriate management of his lithium-induced nephrogenic DI?
options: Increase the desmopressin dose as initial therapy
Start high-dose loop diuretics as initial therapy
Discuss lithium discontinuation or alternative
Severely restrict fluids as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Management starts with addressing the offending drug: reviewing whether lithium can be stopped or switched with psychiatry. If lithium must continue, amiloride reduces lithium uptake by collecting-duct cells, and thiazides paradoxically reduce urine output in nephrogenic DI by inducing mild volume contraction; adequate hydration is essential. Increasing desmopressin is futile because the kidney cannot respond. Severe fluid restriction is dangerous given obligate water loss. Loop diuretics increase free water loss and would worsen polyuria. Drug modification plus amiloride/thiazide is the evidence-based approach.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which long-term monitoring is most important given his chronic lithium therapy?
options: Annual echocardiography in this setting
Periodic renal function
Routine bone density scans
Frequent liver biopsies in this setting
stages.explanation: Chronic lithium can cause progressive chronic kidney disease as well as nephrogenic DI, so periodic monitoring of renal function (creatinine, eGFR) and lithium levels is essential to detect toxicity and nephrotoxicity early and adjust therapy. Annual echocardiography is not indicated for lithium monitoring. Routine bone density scans are unrelated to lithium nephropathy. Liver biopsies are not part of lithium surveillance, as lithium is renally cleared and not hepatotoxic. Tracking kidney function and drug levels guides safe long-term use and timely intervention.

cases.diagnosis: Lithium-induced nephrogenic diabetes insipidus
pearls: Nephrogenic DI shows dilute urine that fails to concentrate after desmopressin, distinguishing it from central DI.
Lithium is the most common acquired cause of nephrogenic DI via impaired collecting-duct response to ADH.
Thiazides and amiloride paradoxically reduce urine output in nephrogenic DI; amiloride also limits lithium entry into tubular cells.
Chronic lithium therapy requires monitoring of renal function and drug levels due to nephrotoxicity risk.

cases.title: Rapid Breathing and Abdominal Pain in a Young Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and rapid breathing for one day.
cases.history: A 29-year-old woman presents to the ED with one day of nausea, vomiting, diffuse abdominal pain, and rapid breathing. She reports increasing thirst, frequent urination, and fatigue over the past week. She has type 1 diabetes diagnosed in adolescence and admits she ran out of insulin five days ago and could not refill it. She recently had a urinary tract infection treated with antibiotics. PMH: type 1 diabetes mellitus. Medications: insulin glargine and insulin aspart (both lapsed). Allergies: none. Social: graduate student, nonsmoker, rare alcohol. Family history: autoimmune thyroid disease in mother. On review of systems she reports blurry vision and lightheadedness but denies chest pain, fever now, or focal weakness. She describes her breathing as deep and labored. Her last meal was poor and she has been unable to keep fluids down. She has no known kidney or liver disease and denies toxic ingestions, including methanol, ethylene glycol, or salicylates.
cases.exam: The patient is ill appearing with deep, rapid Kussmaul respirations and a fruity odor on her breath. Mucous membranes are dry and skin turgor is reduced. Cardiac exam shows sinus tachycardia without murmurs. Lungs are clear with increased work of breathing. The abdomen is soft with diffuse mild tenderness and no rebound or guarding; bowel sounds are present. Neurologic exam shows she is alert and oriented but fatigued, with no focal deficits. Capillary refill is mildly prolonged and extremities are cool, consistent with dehydration. There is no asterixis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which combination of initial tests is most important to confirm her suspected diagnosis?
options: Liver function tests and lipase as the first step today
Chest radiograph and troponin as the first step today
Glucose, basic metabolic panel, venous blood gas,
CBC and blood cultures only as the first step today
stages.explanation: Suspected diabetic ketoacidosis is confirmed with glucose, a basic metabolic panel (to compute the anion gap and assess potassium and bicarbonate), a venous blood gas for pH, and serum or urine ketones. CBC and blood cultures may be added if infection is suspected but do not confirm DKA. A chest radiograph and troponin evaluate cardiopulmonary disease, not the metabolic emergency at hand. Liver tests and lipase address hepatobiliary or pancreatic disease. The metabolic panel, gas, and ketones together establish the diagnostic triad of hyperglycemia, anion gap acidosis, and ketosis.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Glucose 512 mg/dL
Na 132 mEq/L
K 5.2 mEq/L
Cl 96 mEq/L
HCO3 8 mEq/L
BUN 26 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.2 mg/dL
Venous pH 7.18
Serum beta-hydroxybutyrate 6.8 mmol/L
Urinalysis: glucose 4+, ketones 3+

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Using Na 132, Cl 96, and HCO3 8, what is her serum anion gap?
options: 28 mEq/L (elevated)
8 mEq/L (low) in this clinical context
12 mEq/L (normal)
16 mEq/L (borderline)
stages.explanation: Anion gap equals Na minus (Cl plus HCO3): 132 − (96 + 8) = 28 mEq/L, which is markedly elevated (normal roughly 8–12). This high anion gap reflects accumulation of ketoacids (beta-hydroxybutyrate and acetoacetate) consuming bicarbonate. A value of 12 would be normal and inconsistent with her data. A gap of 8 would suggest a non-gap process, contradicting her ketoacidosis. Sixteen miscalculates the arithmetic. The elevated gap of 28, paired with ketonemia and hyperglycemia, cements the diagnosis of high anion gap metabolic acidosis from DKA.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial step in managing her DKA?
options: Aggressive IV isotonic fluid resuscitation with electrolyte monitoring before/with insulin
Oral hydration and discharge with insulin prescription as initial therapy
Immediate IV insulin bolus before fluids or potassium check as initial therapy
IV sodium bicarbonate as first-line therapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of DKA management is aggressive isotonic fluid resuscitation to restore intravascular volume and renal perfusion, with close electrolyte monitoring; insulin follows once potassium is known to be safe (insulin shifts potassium intracellularly and can precipitate dangerous hypokalemia). Giving insulin before assessing potassium and starting fluids risks profound hypokalemia and worsening hypotension. Bicarbonate is reserved for severe acidemia (pH below ~6.9) and is not first-line. Oral hydration and discharge are unsafe for a patient with severe acidosis and dehydration who needs IV therapy and monitoring.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Her potassium is 5.2 mEq/L now. What will most likely happen to potassium as insulin and fluids are given, and what does this require?
options: Potassium will fall
Potassium will be unaffected; no monitoring needed as the leading concern
Potassium will fall; give sodium polystyrene sulfonate as the leading concern
Potassium will rise; stop all potassium intake as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Although her measured potassium is high-normal, total body potassium is depleted in DKA; insulin and fluid therapy drive potassium intracellularly, so serum potassium predictably falls. Potassium should be monitored closely and added to IV fluids once it drops to roughly 5.0–5.3 mEq/L (with adequate urine output) to prevent dangerous hypokalemia and arrhythmias. Stopping all potassium would invite severe hypokalemia. Assuming no effect ignores the well-known intracellular shift. Sodium polystyrene sulfonate removes potassium and is exactly the wrong move when potassium is about to fall.

cases.diagnosis: Diabetic ketoacidosis with high anion gap metabolic acidosis
pearls: Anion gap = Na − (Cl + HCO3); a gap >12 with ketones and hyperglycemia indicates DKA.
DKA management is fluids first, then insulin; never give insulin before knowing potassium is safe.
Total body potassium is depleted in DKA even when serum potassium appears normal or high; it falls with treatment.
Reserve IV bicarbonate for severe acidemia (pH <6.9); routine use is not recommended.

cases.title: Recurrent Kidney Stones and Persistent Weakness
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent kidney stones with chronic fatigue and muscle weakness.
cases.history: A 33-year-old woman presents to clinic with a history of three calcium-containing kidney stones over two years and persistent fatigue with intermittent muscle weakness. She also reports bone aches. She has dry eyes and dry mouth and was told she may have an autoimmune condition. She denies diarrhea or laxative use. PMH: recurrent nephrolithiasis, suspected Sjögren syndrome. Medications: artificial tears, occasional ibuprofen. Allergies: none. Social: librarian, nonsmoker, no alcohol. Family history: mother with autoimmune thyroid disease. On review of systems she reports polyuria and nocturia but denies vomiting, fevers, or flank pain currently. She has not used acetazolamide or topiramate. Prior imaging reportedly showed bilateral kidney calcifications. She has no history of chronic diarrhea, ureteral diversion, or known renal tubular disorders, and is not taking diuretics. She is frustrated by recurrent stones despite good hydration and a normal diet.
cases.exam: The patient is well appearing but reports generalized fatigue. Eyes show decreased tear film and the oral mucosa is dry. Cardiac and lung exams are normal. The abdomen is soft and nontender without costovertebral angle tenderness today. Neuromuscular exam reveals mild symmetric proximal weakness (4+/5) with diminished reflexes; sensation is intact. There is no edema. No tetany or Chvostek sign is elicited. The remainder of the focused exam is unremarkable. She appears euvolemic. There are no rashes, joint deformities, or focal neurologic deficits noted on examination today.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Labs show a low bicarbonate with a normal anion gap. What is the best next step to localize her acidosis?
options: Measure serum lactate as the first step today
Calculate the urine anion gap
Obtain a 24-hour urine protein only
Order an arterial ammonia level as the first step today
stages.explanation: A normal anion gap (hyperchloremic) metabolic acidosis is localized by the urine anion gap, urine pH, and serum potassium. A positive urine anion gap with inappropriately alkaline urine and hypokalemia points to distal (type 1) RTA, whereas a negative urine anion gap suggests gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss. Serum lactate evaluates a high-gap process, not relevant here. An arterial ammonia level assesses hepatic encephalopathy, unrelated to RTA. A 24-hour urine protein evaluates proteinuria but does not localize the acidosis. The urine anion gap and urine pH are the key discriminators.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Na 140 mEq/L
K 3.0 mEq/L
Cl 114 mEq/L
HCO3 16 mEq/L
Anion gap 10 mEq/L
Urine pH 6.5
Urine anion gap +18 (positive)
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
Renal ultrasound: bilateral nephrocalcinosis
Anti-Ro/SSA antibodies positive

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: With hypokalemic non-gap acidosis, urine pH 6.5, positive urine anion gap, and nephrocalcinosis, what is the diagnosis?
options: Chronic diarrhea-related acidosis
Type 1 (distal) RTA
Type 4 RTA in this clinical context
Type 2 (proximal) RTA
stages.explanation: Type 1 (distal) RTA is defined by an inability to acidify the urine: despite systemic acidosis, urine pH remains inappropriately high (above ~5.5), the urine anion gap is positive (impaired ammonium excretion), and there is hypokalemia. Chronic hypocitraturia and alkaline urine promote calcium phosphate stones and nephrocalcinosis, as seen here, and Sjögren syndrome is a classic cause. Type 2 RTA typically presents with acidic urine once bicarbonate falls and is associated with Fanconi features, not nephrocalcinosis. Type 4 RTA causes hyperkalemia, not hypokalemia. A diarrheal cause would show a negative urine anion gap and acidic urine.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment for her distal RTA?
options: Sodium polystyrene sulfonate
Loop diuretics as initial therapy
Fludrocortisone as initial therapy
Oral alkali therapy
stages.explanation: Distal RTA is treated with oral alkali, ideally potassium citrate, which corrects the acidosis, replaces potassium, and raises urinary citrate to reduce stone formation and nephrocalcinosis progression. Sodium polystyrene sulfonate lowers potassium and would worsen her hypokalemia. Loop diuretics increase calcium excretion and potassium loss, aggravating both stones and hypokalemia. Fludrocortisone is used in type 4 RTA (hypoaldosteronism), not distal RTA. Potassium citrate uniquely addresses the acidosis, the potassium deficit, and the stone risk simultaneously, making it the optimal therapy here.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If her distal RTA is left untreated, which complication is she most at risk for over time?
options: Nephrocalcinosis, recurrent stones,
Hepatic cirrhosis as the leading concern
Hyperkalemic cardiac arrest
Pulmonary fibrosis as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Untreated distal RTA leads to progressive nephrocalcinosis, recurrent calcium phosphate stones, and metabolic bone disease (osteomalacia/osteopenia) because chronic acidosis mobilizes bone buffer and alkaline urine with hypocitraturia favors stone formation. Hyperkalemic cardiac arrest is a risk of type 4 RTA, not type 1, which causes hypokalemia. Hepatic cirrhosis is unrelated to RTA. Pulmonary fibrosis is not a recognized complication of distal RTA. Persistent bone aches and weakness already hint at the skeletal consequences, underscoring the importance of alkali therapy.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given her positive anti-Ro/SSA and sicca symptoms, what underlying disease most likely explains her distal RTA?
options: Sarcoidosis in this setting
Systemic lupus erythematosus
Multiple myeloma in this setting
Sjögren syndrome
stages.explanation: Sjögren syndrome, suggested by dry eyes, dry mouth, and positive anti-Ro/SSA antibodies, is a classic autoimmune cause of distal (type 1) RTA through tubulointerstitial injury impairing distal acidification. Systemic lupus can cause renal disease but the sicca symptoms and anti-Ro pattern point to Sjögren. Multiple myeloma more characteristically causes proximal tubular dysfunction (Fanconi) rather than isolated distal RTA. Sarcoidosis can cause hypercalcemia and interstitial disease but does not fit the sicca and serologic picture as well. Recognizing the Sjögren association guides both nephrology and rheumatology management.

cases.diagnosis: Type 1 (distal) renal tubular acidosis from Sjögren syndrome
pearls: Distal (type 1) RTA: inability to acidify urine (urine pH >5.5), positive urine anion gap, hypokalemia, and nephrocalcinosis.
Treat with potassium citrate to correct acidosis, replete potassium, and reduce stone formation.
Sjögren syndrome is a classic autoimmune cause of distal RTA.
A positive urine anion gap indicates impaired renal ammonium excretion (renal cause); a negative one suggests GI bicarbonate loss.

cases.title: Poor Growth and Frequent Urination in a Young Boy
cases.chiefComplaint: Poor growth, fatigue, and increased urination noted by his parents.
cases.history: A 9-year-old boy is brought to clinic for poor linear growth, fatigue, and increased urination and thirst. His parents note he has fallen off his growth curve over the past two years and tires easily. He has had bowing of the legs. There is no history of diabetes; home glucose checks have been normal. He has no chronic diarrhea. PMH: developmental milestones normal; no prior chronic illness. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: third grader, lives with both parents; no toxic exposures known. Family history: a maternal uncle had unexplained 'kidney problems' in childhood. On review of systems his parents report polyuria, polydipsia, and decreased appetite but deny vomiting, fevers, or hematuria. He has no rashes or joint swelling. There is no history of cystinosis diagnosis, but it has not been excluded. The boy is not on any nephrotoxic medications and has had no toxic ingestions.
cases.exam: The boy is small for age with weight and height below the third percentile. He appears thin but not acutely ill. There is genu varum (bowing) of the lower legs and mild wrist widening consistent with rickets. Cardiac and lung exams are normal. The abdomen is soft and nontender without organomegaly. Neuromuscular exam shows mild generalized weakness without focal deficits. There is no edema. Mucous membranes are slightly dry. No costovertebral angle tenderness is present. The remainder of the focused examination is unremarkable, with normal-appearing eyes and no dysmorphic features noted today.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Initial labs show a non-gap metabolic acidosis. Given his rickets and polyuria, which urine studies are most informative?
options: Urine eosinophils as the first step today
Urine osmolality alone as the first step today
Urinalysis for glucose, protein, phosphate handling, amino acids,
Urine culture and cytology as the first step today
stages.explanation: A child with non-gap acidosis, rickets, and polyuria raises concern for proximal (type 2) RTA with Fanconi syndrome, so the key tests probe global proximal tubular dysfunction: urine glucose (with normal serum glucose), low-molecular-weight proteinuria, phosphate wasting, aminoaciduria, and urine pH. A urine culture and cytology assess infection or malignancy, not tubular function. Urine osmolality alone does not characterize proximal reabsorptive defects. Urine eosinophils suggest acute interstitial nephritis, not Fanconi. The panel demonstrating multiple proximal reabsorptive failures is what confirms Fanconi syndrome.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Na 139 mEq/L
K 3.1 mEq/L
Cl 113 mEq/L
HCO3 15 mEq/L
Anion gap 11 mEq/L
Glucose (serum) 92 mg/dL
Phosphate 1.8 mg/dL (low)
Urine glucose: 3+ (with normal serum glucose)
Urine protein: low-molecular-weight proteinuria present
Urine amino acids: generalized aminoaciduria
Urine pH 5.2

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Glucosuria with normal serum glucose, phosphaturia, aminoaciduria, and non-gap acidosis indicate which diagnosis?
options: Diabetes mellitus
Type 4 RTA as the primary diagnosis
Type 1 (distal) RTA
Type 2 RTA with Fanconi syndrome
stages.explanation: Generalized proximal tubular dysfunction—glucosuria despite normal serum glucose, phosphate wasting, aminoaciduria, and bicarbonate wasting causing non-gap acidosis—defines Fanconi syndrome, the basis of type 2 (proximal) RTA. Hypophosphatemia drives his rickets. Type 1 (distal) RTA does not cause glucosuria or aminoaciduria and features inappropriately alkaline urine. Type 4 RTA causes hyperkalemia, whereas he is hypokalemic. Diabetes mellitus is excluded by normal serum glucose despite heavy glucosuria. The constellation of multiple proximal reabsorptive defects is pathognomonic for proximal RTA with Fanconi syndrome.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: His urine pH is 5.2 during acidosis. How does this help distinguish proximal from distal RTA?
options: In proximal RTA, the distal nephron can still acidify urine once serum bicarbonate is low
Distal RTA can acidify urine below 5.5; proximal cannot as the immediate next step
A urine pH of 5.2 confirms type 4 RTA as the immediate next step
Urine pH is meaningless in RTA as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: In proximal (type 2) RTA, distal acidification mechanisms remain intact, so once the serum bicarbonate falls below the lowered proximal reabsorptive threshold, the distal nephron can acidify urine and urine pH drops below 5.5 (here 5.2). By contrast, distal (type 1) RTA cannot acidify urine, keeping pH inappropriately high despite acidosis. Saying urine pH is meaningless ignores its central diagnostic role. A urine pH of 5.2 does not confirm type 4 RTA, which is characterized by hyperkalemia and variable urine pH. The ability to acidify urine here supports a proximal rather than distal defect.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of managing his proximal RTA with Fanconi syndrome?
options: Large doses of alkali plus phosphate
Loop diuretics as initial therapy
Low-dose alkali alone as initial therapy
Fluid restriction as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Proximal RTA requires large amounts of alkali because filtered bicarbonate is wasted; potassium-containing alkali helps counter the worsened potassium loss that alkali therapy can cause. Phosphate and active vitamin D supplementation treat the hypophosphatemic rickets, and the underlying cause (e.g., cystinosis, which must be evaluated) should be addressed. Low-dose alkali alone is insufficient given heavy bicarbonate wasting. Loop diuretics worsen electrolyte losses. Fluid restriction is inappropriate and dangerous with his polyuria. High-dose alkali plus mineral and vitamin D repletion, with workup for the cause, is the standard approach.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which pediatric condition must be evaluated as a leading cause of his Fanconi syndrome?
options: Wilson disease only after age 40
Essential hypertension
Type 1 diabetes
Cystinosis
stages.explanation: Cystinosis is the most common inherited cause of Fanconi syndrome in children and must be evaluated, classically diagnosed by elevated leukocyte cystine and corneal cystine crystals; early cysteamine therapy slows organ damage. Wilson disease can cause Fanconi but typically presents later and is not limited to after age 40, making that option incorrect as worded. Essential hypertension does not cause Fanconi syndrome. Type 1 diabetes causes glucosuria only with hyperglycemia, not the normoglycemic glucosuria seen here. Identifying cystinosis is critical because targeted treatment can preserve renal and extrarenal function.

cases.diagnosis: Type 2 (proximal) renal tubular acidosis with Fanconi syndrome
pearls: Proximal (type 2) RTA reflects generalized proximal dysfunction: glucosuria with normal serum glucose, phosphaturia, aminoaciduria, and bicarbonate wasting.
Hypophosphatemia from phosphate wasting causes rickets/osteomalacia.
In proximal RTA the distal nephron still acidifies urine (pH <5.5) once serum bicarbonate is low.
Cystinosis is the most common inherited cause of Fanconi syndrome in children—evaluate and treat early.

cases.title: Fatigue and Abnormal Electrolytes in a Diabetic Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Fatigue and incidental finding of high potassium on routine labs.
cases.history: A 63-year-old man with long-standing type 2 diabetes presents to clinic for fatigue, and routine labs reveal hyperkalemia. He has had diabetes for 18 years with known diabetic kidney disease and peripheral neuropathy. He denies muscle weakness, palpitations, or chest pain but feels generally tired. PMH: type 2 diabetes, hypertension, diabetic nephropathy, mild CKD. Medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily, metformin, atorvastatin; no potassium supplements, no NSAIDs reported. Allergies: none. Social: retired mechanic, former smoker, rare alcohol. Family history: type 2 diabetes in multiple relatives. On review of systems he denies vomiting, diarrhea, or recent illness. He has stable urine output and no edema. He has not started any new medications recently other than continuing his ACE inhibitor. He has no history of adrenal disease or recent trauma, and denies use of trimethoprim or potassium-sparing diuretics.
cases.exam: The patient is well appearing and in no distress. Cardiac exam is regular without murmurs or extra sounds. Lungs are clear. The abdomen is soft and nontender. Neurologic exam shows decreased sensation in a stocking distribution consistent with diabetic neuropathy, with normal strength and no focal deficits. There is no peripheral edema and no signs of volume overload or depletion; he appears euvolemic. Skin and mucous membranes are normal. There is no abdominal bruit appreciated. The remainder of the focused examination is unremarkable, and he shows no signs of acute distress or hemodynamic instability.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His potassium is elevated and bicarbonate is mildly low. What is the best initial workup to characterize his acid-base and electrolyte problem?
options: CT abdomen as the first step today
Basic metabolic panel with anion gap, plus assessment of renal function
Echocardiogram as the first step today
Serum cortisol stimulation test first as the first step today
stages.explanation: The first step is a basic metabolic panel with the anion gap calculation, plus review of renal function and contributing medications (e.g., ACE inhibitors), to determine whether this is a non-gap (hyperchloremic) acidosis with hyperkalemia suggesting type 4 RTA. A CT abdomen would not characterize the acid-base disorder. A cortisol stimulation test may be considered later if primary adrenal insufficiency is suspected, but it is not the initial step. An echocardiogram evaluates cardiac structure, not electrolyte handling. Establishing the gap, renal function, and drug effects efficiently frames the diagnosis.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Na 138 mEq/L
K 5.8 mEq/L
Cl 112 mEq/L
HCO3 18 mEq/L
Anion gap 8 mEq/L
Glucose 162 mg/dL
BUN 30 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (eGFR ~48)
Urine pH 5.1
Plasma renin: low
Plasma aldosterone: low

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Hyperkalemia with a non-gap acidosis, acidic urine, and low renin/low aldosterone in a diabetic points to which diagnosis?
options: Type 1 (distal) RTA
Addison disease in this clinical context
Type 2 (proximal) RTA
Type 4 RTA
stages.explanation: Type 4 RTA results from aldosterone deficiency or resistance; in diabetics it classically arises from hyporeninemic hypoaldosteronism, producing hyperkalemia and a hyperchloremic non-gap acidosis with a low aldosterone and low renin. Hyperkalemia impairs renal ammoniagenesis, limiting acid excretion. Type 1 distal RTA causes hypokalemia and inappropriately alkaline urine. Type 2 proximal RTA also causes hypokalemia, not hyperkalemia. Addison disease (primary adrenal insufficiency) would show low aldosterone but high renin and typically hyponatremia with high renin; here renin is low, fitting hyporeninemic hypoaldosteronism rather than primary adrenal failure.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management of his type 4 RTA?
options: Dietary potassium restriction, review/adjust the ACE inhibitor,
Begin a high-potassium diet as initial therapy
Add a potassium supplement as initial therapy
Start spironolactone as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Type 4 RTA is managed by lowering potassium and supporting distal sodium/potassium exchange: restrict dietary potassium, reassess potassium-raising drugs such as ACE inhibitors, and use a loop or thiazide diuretic to enhance kaliuresis; fludrocortisone can be added in selected aldosterone-deficient patients. A potassium supplement or a high-potassium diet would dangerously worsen hyperkalemia. Spironolactone is an aldosterone antagonist that blocks the very pathway already deficient, aggravating hyperkalemia and acidosis. Reducing potassium load and augmenting renal potassium excretion, with mineralocorticoid replacement if needed, is the correct strategy.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which class of medications most commonly worsens type 4 RTA and should be reviewed in this patient?
options: Beta-2 agonists as the immediate next step
ACE inhibitors/ARBs and potassium-sparing diuretics
Proton pump inhibitors as the immediate next step
Statins as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: ACE inhibitors and ARBs reduce aldosterone, and potassium-sparing diuretics (spironolactone, amiloride, eplerenone) block its action, both worsening hyperkalemia and type 4 RTA; this patient is on lisinopril, which should be reviewed and possibly adjusted. Statins do not affect potassium handling. Beta-2 agonists actually drive potassium intracellularly and lower serum potassium. Proton pump inhibitors are not a recognized cause of hyperkalemic non-gap acidosis. Other culprits include NSAIDs, trimethoprim, and calcineurin inhibitors. Medication review is a key, modifiable component of managing type 4 RTA.

cases.diagnosis: Type 4 renal tubular acidosis from hyporeninemic hypoaldosteronism
pearls: Type 4 RTA is a hyperkalemic, non-gap (hyperchloremic) metabolic acidosis from aldosterone deficiency or resistance.
Diabetic kidney disease commonly causes hyporeninemic hypoaldosteronism—low renin and low aldosterone.
Hyperkalemia impairs renal ammoniagenesis, reducing acid excretion in type 4 RTA.
Avoid spironolactone, ACE inhibitors/ARBs, NSAIDs, and trimethoprim, which worsen hyperkalemia; treat with K restriction, diuretics, and fludrocortisone if needed.

cases.title: Persistent Vomiting and an Odd Blood Gas
cases.chiefComplaint: Ongoing nausea and weakness during hospitalization for a bowel obstruction.
cases.history: A 50-year-old woman is hospitalized for a small bowel obstruction managed with nasogastric decompression. Over several days she has had high-volume nasogastric output and intermittent vomiting before the tube was placed. She now reports fatigue and muscle weakness. She has received only modest IV fluids. PMH: prior abdominal surgery with adhesions, hypertension. Medications: home hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily (held on admission), now on IV fluids and NG suction. Allergies: none. Social: office manager, nonsmoker, social alcohol. Family history: noncontributory. On review of systems she denies diarrhea but notes decreased urine output and lightheadedness. She has not received diuretics during admission other than her held home thiazide. There is no history of surreptitious vomiting or laxative abuse, and no exogenous steroid use. Surgical team is monitoring her NG output and electrolytes closely, and she has been NPO with persistent gastric losses recorded each shift.
cases.exam: The patient appears mildly dehydrated and fatigued. Mucous membranes are dry and skin turgor is slightly reduced. A nasogastric tube is in place draining bilious fluid. Cardiac exam shows mild sinus tachycardia without murmurs. Lungs are clear. The abdomen is distended and mildly tender with high-pitched bowel sounds. Neuromuscular exam reveals mild generalized weakness and diminished reflexes; sensation is intact. There is no peripheral edema. Capillary refill is slightly prolonged. There is no asterixis or tetany, and she appears volume depleted overall on examination today.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given prolonged vomiting and NG suction, which acid-base disturbance do you most expect, and what test confirms it?
options: Metabolic alkalosis
High anion gap metabolic acidosis; serum lactate
Respiratory acidosis; chest radiograph in this clinical context
Non-gap metabolic acidosis; urine anion gap
stages.explanation: Loss of acidic gastric fluid through vomiting and NG suction depletes hydrogen and chloride, generating a metabolic alkalosis, confirmed by a basic metabolic panel (elevated bicarbonate) and a venous blood gas showing high pH with compensatory rise in pCO2. A high anion gap acidosis with lactate would suggest hypoperfusion or toxins, not gastric losses. Respiratory acidosis from hypoventilation does not fit her clear lungs and normal respiratory rate. A non-gap metabolic acidosis points to bicarbonate loss or RTA, the opposite of her expected picture. The metabolic panel and blood gas together establish the alkalosis.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Na 138 mEq/L
K 2.9 mEq/L
Cl 88 mEq/L
HCO3 36 mEq/L
Venous pH 7.50
pCO2 47 mmHg
BUN 32 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL
Urine chloride 8 mEq/L
Urine sodium 25 mEq/L

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Her urine chloride is 8 mEq/L. How does this classify her metabolic alkalosis?
options: Mixed respiratory and metabolic acidosis
Anion gap acidosis in this clinical context
Chloride-responsive (saline-responsive)
Chloride-resistant (saline-unresponsive)
stages.explanation: A low urine chloride (below ~20 mEq/L, here 8) indicates chloride-responsive (saline-responsive) metabolic alkalosis, typical of vomiting and NG suction, where the kidney avidly reabsorbs chloride and sodium due to volume depletion. Chloride-resistant alkalosis (urine chloride above ~20) is seen with mineralocorticoid excess such as hyperaldosteronism. Mixed respiratory and metabolic acidosis is inconsistent with her high pH and high bicarbonate. An anion gap acidosis is excluded by the alkalemic, high-bicarbonate profile. Urine chloride is the key test, more reliable than urine sodium here because diuretics or bicarbonaturia can raise urine sodium despite volume depletion.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment for her chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis?
options: IV isotonic saline with potassium chloride repletion
Spironolactone as initial therapy
Sodium bicarbonate infusion as initial therapy
Acetazolamide as first-line as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis is treated by restoring volume and chloride with IV normal saline, plus potassium chloride to correct her hypokalemia, which otherwise perpetuates the alkalosis. Spironolactone is used for chloride-resistant alkalosis from mineralocorticoid excess, not this volume-depleted state. Acetazolamide can be a second-line adjunct (e.g., in volume-overloaded patients) but is not first-line here given her dehydration. A sodium bicarbonate infusion would worsen the alkalosis. Normal saline with KCl directly reverses the chloride and potassium deficits driving the disorder, allowing the kidney to excrete excess bicarbonate.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Why is correcting her hypokalemia essential to resolving the alkalosis?
options: Hypokalemia causes a high anion gap as the immediate next step
Hypokalemia directly lowers serum bicarbonate as the immediate next step
Hypokalemia promotes renal hydrogen secretion
Hypokalemia has no effect on acid-base status as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Hypokalemia perpetuates metabolic alkalosis: low potassium drives intracellular potassium-hydrogen exchange and stimulates the kidney to secrete hydrogen and reabsorb bicarbonate (including enhanced ammoniagenesis and a shift of hydrogen into cells), maintaining the elevated bicarbonate. Saying hypokalemia has no acid-base effect is incorrect. Hypokalemia does not cause a high anion gap. It does not directly lower serum bicarbonate; if anything it raises it by promoting bicarbonate reabsorption. Therefore repleting potassium chloride is necessary, alongside saline, to allow the kidney to excrete the excess bicarbonate and correct the alkalosis.

cases.diagnosis: Chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from vomiting and NG suction
pearls: Vomiting and NG suction cause chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis via loss of HCl and volume depletion.
Urine chloride <20 mEq/L = chloride-responsive (saline-responsive); >20 suggests mineralocorticoid excess (chloride-resistant).
Treat with IV normal saline plus KCl; correcting hypokalemia is essential to resolve the alkalosis.
Urine chloride is more reliable than urine sodium for classifying metabolic alkalosis, especially with diuretic use.

cases.title: Dark Urine and Severe Muscle Pain After a Workout
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe thigh and calf pain with cola-colored urine for one day.
cases.history: A 22-year-old previously healthy man presents with two days of diffuse muscle aching and one day of dark, cola-colored urine. He returned to the gym after a long break and completed an intense "leg day" CrossFit session followed by a hot yoga class. The next morning his quadriceps and calves were exquisitely tender, swollen, and stiff, and he noticed decreased, tea-colored urine. He admits to poor fluid intake during and after exercise. He denies fevers, recent illness, seizures, prolonged immobilization, alcohol or recreational drug use, and statin or other new medications. He takes a pre-workout supplement containing caffeine and a creatine powder. No personal or family history of muscle disease, malignant hyperthermia, or kidney disease. Allergies: none. Social: college student, occasional alcohol, no tobacco or illicit drugs. He reports no chest pain, palpitations, or numbness. He has not urinated in roughly eight hours despite drinking water this morning.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable young man in moderate distress from pain. Cardiopulmonary exam normal with mildly tachycardic regular rhythm. Abdomen soft, nontender, no flank tenderness or costovertebral angle tenderness. Bilateral thighs and calves are markedly tender to palpation with firm, swollen muscle bellies; strength limited by pain but no focal weakness, no compartment tightness, distal pulses intact, sensation intact. No rash, joint swelling, or peripheral edema. Mucous membranes slightly dry. No flank ecchymosis. Neurologic exam otherwise nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A urine dipstick is strongly positive for blood, but microscopy shows no red blood cells. What is the most likely explanation?
options: Hematuria from a kidney stone the patient has not yet passed
Contamination of the sample by ascorbic acid in his supplement
Hemoglobinuria from intravascular hemolysis as the first step today
Myoglobinuria from muscle breakdown cross-reacting with the heme dipstick
stages.explanation: The urine heme dipstick detects the peroxidase activity of heme groups, so it turns positive with hemoglobin OR myoglobin even when no intact RBCs are present. In a young man with severe exertional muscle pain, swelling, and dark urine, myoglobinuria from rhabdomyolysis is the classic cause of a heme-positive dipstick with absent RBCs on microscopy. Hematuria from a stone would show RBCs on microscopy and typically colicky pain. Intravascular hemolysis would produce hemoglobinuria but is accompanied by anemia, low haptoglobin, and scleral icterus, none of which fit this exertional picture. Ascorbic acid causes false-NEGATIVE, not false-positive, dipstick results, so it cannot explain a strongly positive dipstick.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Na 138 mEq/L
K 6.1 mEq/L
HCO3 18 mEq/L
BUN 38 mg/dL
Creatinine 2.6 mg/dL (baseline 0.9)
Calcium 7.6 mg/dL
Phosphorus 6.8 mg/dL
CK 28,400 U/L
Uric acid 9.2 mg/dL
UA: heme 3+, protein 1+, 0-2 RBC/hpf, pigmented granular casts present
ECG: peaked T waves, normal QRS duration

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After confirming the diagnosis, what is the single most important initial therapy?
options: Intravenous sodium bicarbonate boluses to alkalinize the blood
Aggressive intravenous isotonic crystalloid resuscitation
Loop diuretic to force diuresis and clear myoglobin
Urgent hemodialysis for the creatinine of 2.6 mg/dL
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of rhabdomyolysis management is early, aggressive isotonic IV fluid (normal saline or lactated Ringer's) to restore renal perfusion, increase urine flow, and dilute/clear nephrotoxic myoglobin and uric acid, which reduces the risk and severity of AKI. Hemodialysis is reserved for refractory hyperkalemia, severe acidosis, or volume overload not responsive to medical therapy, not for a moderate creatinine alone. IV bicarbonate to alkalinize urine is sometimes used but is adjunctive and unproven; it should never precede volume repletion and can worsen hypocalcemia. Loop diuretics do not improve outcomes and may worsen volume depletion and acidify urine, promoting cast formation.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which laboratory abnormality poses the most immediate threat to this patient's life and requires urgent treatment?
options: Hyperuricemia of 9.2 mg/dL
Hyperkalemia of 6.1 mEq/L with peaked T waves
Hyperphosphatemia of 6.8 mg/dL
Hypocalcemia of 7.6 mg/dL
stages.explanation: Hyperkalemia is the most immediately life-threatening complication of rhabdomyolysis because potassium released from injured myocytes can precipitate fatal arrhythmias; ECG changes (peaked T waves) signal cardiac membrane instability and mandate immediate IV calcium for cardioprotection followed by insulin/glucose and other shifting/elimination measures. Hyperphosphatemia and hyperuricemia result from cell lysis but are not acutely lethal and improve with hydration. Hypocalcemia is usually asymptomatic in early rhabdomyolysis (calcium is sequestered in damaged muscle) and should generally NOT be aggressively repleted unless symptomatic or needed for hyperkalemia treatment, because rebound hypercalcemia commonly occurs during recovery.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: During his hospital stay he develops worsening, tense swelling and severe pain in the right calf with pain on passive stretch out of proportion to exam. What is the best next step?
options: Measure compartment pressures
Increase opioid analgesia and continue observation
Order a venous duplex to rule out deep vein thrombosis
Apply ice and elevate the limb above the heart
stages.explanation: Pain out of proportion to exam, a tense swollen compartment, and pain on passive stretch are hallmark signs of acute compartment syndrome, a limb-threatening emergency and a recognized complication of rhabdomyolysis. The next step is to measure compartment pressures and obtain urgent surgical consultation for possible fasciotomy; delays cause irreversible muscle and nerve necrosis. Escalating opioids masks a surgical emergency and delays definitive care. Elevating the limb above heart level can paradoxically reduce arterial perfusion in compartment syndrome and is contraindicated. Venous duplex evaluates DVT, which does not cause pain on passive stretch with a tense compartment and would dangerously delay diagnosis.

cases.diagnosis: Rhabdomyolysis-induced acute kidney injury
pearls: A heme-positive urine dipstick with no RBCs on microscopy is myoglobinuria (or hemoglobinuria) until proven otherwise.
Aggressive early isotonic IV fluid is the single most important intervention to prevent rhabdomyolysis-associated AKI.
Watch for the lysis triad of hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, and hyperuricemia, with early hypocalcemia that later rebounds to hypercalcemia.
Pain out of proportion with a tense compartment signals compartment syndrome, a surgical emergency that can both cause and result from rhabdomyolysis.

cases.title: New Kidney Dysfunction Days After Starting Chemotherapy
cases.chiefComplaint: Decreased urine output, nausea, and muscle cramps three days into chemotherapy.
cases.history: A 15-year-old boy was admitted three days ago and diagnosed with B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia after presenting with fatigue, bruising, and a WBC of 92,000/µL with 80% blasts. He began induction chemotherapy two days ago. Today the nursing staff notes decreased urine output, and he reports nausea, muscle cramps, and tingling around his mouth. He denies chest pain but feels his heart "fluttering." Home medications: none prior to admission. Inpatient: induction chemotherapy and as-needed antiemetics. He received only modest IV hydration and no uric-acid-lowering prophylaxis was documented at presentation. Allergies: none. Social: lives with parents, in 9th grade, no substance use. Family history: noncontributory. He has had no fevers since admission and no diarrhea. Review of systems is positive for lethargy and decreased appetite; he has voided only a small amount in the past several hours despite ongoing maintenance fluids.
cases.exam: Tired-appearing adolescent, mildly uncomfortable. Cardiac exam: tachycardic, regular, no murmur. Lungs clear. Abdomen soft with mild diffuse tenderness; spleen tip palpable, no rebound. Mild scattered ecchymoses on the extremities. Chvostek sign present; mild carpopedal posturing noted when blood pressure cuff inflated. No flank tenderness, no peripheral edema, no rash. Mucous membranes moist. Neurologic exam nonfocal aside from perioral paresthesias reported by the patient.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the timing and presentation, which laboratory panel is most urgent to obtain?
options: Potassium, phosphorus, calcium, uric acid,
Blood cultures and procalcitonin for neutropenic fever
Complete blood count and peripheral smear only
Liver function tests and coagulation studies
stages.explanation: Within days of initiating cytotoxic therapy for a high-burden hematologic malignancy, rapid tumor cell lysis releases intracellular contents, producing tumor lysis syndrome (TLS). The defining metabolic derangements are hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, hypocalcemia (secondary to phosphate binding), and hyperuricemia, often with a rising creatinine from acute kidney injury. Therefore potassium, phosphorus, calcium, uric acid, and creatinine are the most urgent labs and directly establish the diagnosis and guide treatment. A CBC and smear track the leukemia but miss the life-threatening electrolyte picture. Blood cultures are warranted if febrile/neutropenic but he is afebrile and they do not address his cramps, arrhythmia risk, and oliguria. LFTs and coagulation studies do not capture the acute metabolic emergency.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Na 137 mEq/L
K 6.4 mEq/L
HCO3 19 mEq/L
BUN 42 mg/dL
Creatinine 2.1 mg/dL (admission 0.7)
Phosphorus 8.9 mg/dL
Calcium 6.8 mg/dL (ionized low)
Uric acid 12.4 mg/dL
LDH 1,980 U/L
UA: amorphous urate crystals, no casts
ECG: peaked T waves, QRS 92 ms

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which medication is most appropriate to rapidly lower this patient's markedly elevated uric acid?
options: Probenecid as initial therapy
Rasburicase
Allopurinol as initial therapy
Febuxostat as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Rasburicase is a recombinant urate oxidase that enzymatically converts existing uric acid into highly soluble allantoin, producing a rapid, dramatic fall in uric acid. It is the agent of choice for established TLS with high-burden disease and significant hyperuricemia and AKI. Allopurinol and febuxostat are xanthine oxidase inhibitors that only PREVENT new uric acid formation and do not lower the existing pool, so they work too slowly for established severe hyperuricemia. Probenecid is uricosuric, increasing renal uric acid excretion, which is contraindicated here because it promotes urate precipitation in already-injured tubules. Note: rasburicase is contraindicated in G6PD deficiency due to hemolysis risk, so screening is appropriate when feasible.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Regarding his phosphorus of 8.9 mg/dL and calcium of 6.8 mg/dL, what is the most appropriate approach to the hypocalcemia?
options: Begin oral calcium carbonate as the sole intervention as initial therapy
Treat the hyperphosphatemia
Start calcitriol to raise serum calcium as initial therapy
Give a large IV calcium bolus immediately to normalize the calcium
stages.explanation: In TLS, hypocalcemia is driven by hyperphosphatemia; the priority is lowering phosphorus (IV hydration, oral phosphate binders, and dialysis if severe), which allows calcium to correct. Calcium should be given only for symptomatic hypocalcemia (tetany, seizures) or significant ECG changes, and even then cautiously, because administering calcium in the setting of high phosphate promotes calcium-phosphate precipitation and metastatic calcification, including in the kidneys. A large empiric calcium bolus is therefore hazardous. Calcitriol does not address the acute calcium-phosphate product and can raise it further. Oral calcium carbonate alone is inadequate and again risks precipitation if phosphate remains high.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Despite IV fluids, rasburicase, and medical management, he becomes anuric with K 7.2 mEq/L, phosphorus 11 mg/dL, and volume overload with crackles. What is the best next step?
options: Add a second dose of rasburicase and continue medical therapy
Give sodium polystyrene sulfonate and recheck in six hours
Administer high-dose furosemide and restrict fluids
Initiate renal replacement therapy (hemodialysis)
stages.explanation: Anuric AKI with refractory hyperkalemia, severe hyperphosphatemia, and volume overload that no longer responds to medical therapy is a clear indication for renal replacement therapy; hemodialysis efficiently removes potassium, phosphorus, and uric acid and corrects volume status in TLS. A second dose of rasburicase does not address the potassium, phosphate, or volume overload and ignores the dialysis indications. High-dose furosemide is futile in an anuric, volume-overloaded patient and delays definitive care. Sodium polystyrene sulfonate acts too slowly for a potassium of 7.2 with anuria and carries risk of intestinal necrosis; it cannot substitute for dialysis in this emergency.

cases.diagnosis: Tumor lysis syndrome with acute kidney injury
pearls: Suspect tumor lysis syndrome within hours to days of treating a high-burden, rapidly proliferating malignancy (ALL, Burkitt lymphoma).
The metabolic signature is hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, hyperuricemia, and hypocalcemia, often with rising creatinine.
Rasburicase rapidly degrades existing uric acid (avoid in G6PD deficiency); allopurinol only prevents new formation.
Avoid aggressive calcium repletion when phosphate is high, and start dialysis early for refractory hyperkalemia, severe hyperphosphatemia, or volume overload.

cases.title: Rising Creatinine With a New Rash on the Medicine Ward
cases.chiefComplaint: Low-grade fever, an itchy rash, and a creatinine that has been climbing over the past week.
cases.history: A 48-year-old woman admitted for community-acquired pneumonia is now improving on antibiotics but the team notes a rising creatinine over the past week. She reports a new low-grade fever, a diffuse itchy maculopapular rash, and mild flank discomfort. About ten days ago, before admission, she was started on a proton pump inhibitor (omeprazole 40 mg daily) by her primary doctor for reflux. Inpatient medications: ceftriaxone (completing course), the home omeprazole continued, and acetaminophen as needed. No NSAIDs. PMH: gastroesophageal reflux, hypertension on amlodipine. Allergies: none known. Social: nonsmoker, occasional wine, works as a teacher. Family history: unremarkable. She denies dysuria, gross hematuria, or decreased urine output, though urine volume seems somewhat reduced. No joint pains, oral ulcers, or photosensitivity. She has not received IV contrast and has been normotensive and euvolemic on the ward.
cases.exam: Comfortable woman, no acute distress. Skin: diffuse blanching erythematous maculopapular rash over the trunk and proximal extremities, no mucosal involvement, no target lesions, no desquamation. Lungs with improving right basilar crackles. Cardiac exam normal. Abdomen soft, mild bilateral flank discomfort to deep palpation without true costovertebral angle tenderness. No peripheral edema. No joint swelling or tenderness. Neurologic exam normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the rising creatinine, new rash, and low-grade fever, which diagnosis is most likely?
options: Acute interstitial nephritis
Acute tubular necrosis from sepsis
Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis
Pre-renal azotemia from poor intake
stages.explanation: The combination of acute kidney injury, fever, and rash arising after a new drug, classically a PPI, antibiotic, or NSAID, is the hallmark triad of acute interstitial nephritis (AIN), an immune-mediated hypersensitivity reaction in the renal interstitium. The temporal link to omeprazole and the maculopapular rash strongly support AIN. ATN from sepsis is plausible with infection, but she is improving, euvolemic, and ATN does not cause rash or fever. Pre-renal azotemia would show concentrated urine and improve with fluids and produces no rash. Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis presents with hematuria, edema, hypertension, and low complement after a streptococcal infection, not a drug-triggered rash with bland symptoms.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Creatinine 2.4 mg/dL (baseline 0.8)
BUN 30 mg/dL
WBC 9.8 ×10³/µL with 9% eosinophils
Urinalysis: protein trace, leukocyte esterase positive, nitrite negative
Urine microscopy: 15-20 WBC/hpf, WBC casts, no bacteria, eosinophils present on Hansel stain
Urine culture: no growth
Renal ultrasound: normal-sized kidneys, no hydronephrosis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which urinary finding best supports the suspected diagnosis?
options: Sterile pyuria with white blood cell casts
Muddy-brown granular casts
Heavy proteinuria with oval fat bodies
Dysmorphic red blood cells and RBC casts
stages.explanation: AIN classically produces sterile pyuria (white cells in the urine with negative culture), often with white blood cell casts, reflecting interstitial inflammation rather than infection; peripheral and urinary eosinophilia may also be seen, though urine eosinophils are neither sensitive nor specific. Dysmorphic RBCs and RBC casts indicate glomerulonephritis, not interstitial disease. Muddy-brown granular casts are the signature of acute tubular necrosis. Heavy proteinuria with oval fat bodies points to nephrotic-range glomerular disease. The sterile pyuria with WBC casts in this drug-exposed patient is the finding most consistent with AIN.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the single most important initial step in management?
options: Begin empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics for pyelonephritis
Start high-dose corticosteroids immediately
Arrange urgent hemodialysis as initial therapy
Discontinue the offending proton pump inhibitor
stages.explanation: The most important and first intervention in drug-induced AIN is to identify and stop the offending agent, here the PPI; many patients recover renal function with discontinuation alone. Corticosteroids are often considered if there is no improvement after stopping the drug or if injury is severe/prolonged, but they are not the first step and should follow drug withdrawal. Empiric antibiotics are inappropriate because the pyuria is sterile (negative culture, no bacteria) and reflects inflammation, not infection. Hemodialysis is reserved for the usual emergent indications and is not warranted with a creatinine of 2.4 and stable electrolytes and volume status.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: One week after stopping the PPI, her creatinine has only minimally improved to 2.2 mg/dL and a biopsy confirms dense interstitial inflammation. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Start an ACE inhibitor to reduce proteinuria
Resume the PPI to confirm causation
Initiate a course of corticosteroids
Begin chronic intermittent hemodialysis
stages.explanation: When AIN fails to improve after withdrawal of the offending drug and biopsy confirms active interstitial inflammation, a course of corticosteroids (e.g., prednisone) is the appropriate next step to hasten recovery and limit progression to chronic fibrosis, particularly when started relatively early. Rechallenging with the PPI is dangerous and can worsen the immune injury. ACE inhibitors reduce glomerular proteinuria but do not treat the interstitial inflammatory process and could worsen renal perfusion in acute injury. Chronic hemodialysis is unjustified; her function is impaired but stable, with potential for recovery, and there is no dialysis indication.

cases.diagnosis: Drug-induced acute interstitial nephritis
pearls: The classic AIN triad is AKI, fever, and rash after a culprit drug (PPIs, NSAIDs, beta-lactams), though all three appear together in a minority of cases.
Sterile pyuria with white blood cell casts is the characteristic urinary finding; urine eosinophils are neither sensitive nor specific.
Stopping the offending drug is the first and most important treatment and may be sufficient for recovery.
Add corticosteroids when there is no improvement after drug withdrawal or when injury is severe or biopsy-proven and progressive.

cases.title: Fatigue, Bone Pain, and Worsening Kidney Numbers in an Older Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Several weeks of fatigue, bone pain, and constipation, now with abnormal kidney tests on admission.
cases.history: A 69-year-old man is admitted with progressive fatigue, diffuse back and rib pain, constipation, and increased thirst over six weeks. He reports a 5 kg unintentional weight loss and recurrent sinus and chest infections this year. He denies gross hematuria, flank colic, dysuria, or recent NSAID use.
