Merely liberalizing glucose targets ignores the metabolic driver. Continuing metformin in the setting of lactic acidosis, AKI, and shock is hazardous and contraindicated. Severe fluid restriction is inappropriate in a hypotensive, hypovolemic septic patient who needs resuscitation; fluids should be titrated to perfusion with attention to her cardiopulmonary status, not withheld.

cases.diagnosis: Emphysematous pyelonephritis
pearls: Emphysematous pyelonephritis is a necrotizing infection with gas in the renal parenchyma, almost always in poorly controlled diabetics; E. coli and Klebsiella are typical.
CT is the diagnostic study of choice; parenchymal gas distinguishes it from abscess and pyonephrosis.
Preferred initial management is percutaneous drainage plus antibiotics, resuscitation, and glycemic control, reserving nephrectomy for treatment failure.
Thrombocytopenia, AKI, shock, and altered mental status are poor prognostic markers.

cases.title: Burning with Urination in a Healthy Young Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Two days of burning with urination and needing to urinate frequently.
cases.history: A 23-year-old woman presents to clinic with two days of dysuria, urinary frequency, and urgency. She feels she cannot fully empty her bladder and notes mild suprapubic discomfort. There is no fever, chills, flank pain, nausea, or vomiting. She has had one similar episode last year that resolved with antibiotics. She is sexually active with a new partner over the past month and reports increased frequency of intercourse; she uses condoms inconsistently. Her last menstrual period was one week ago and she is not pregnant. She has no vaginal discharge, no genital lesions, and no itching. She has no diabetes, kidney disease, or known urinary tract abnormalities. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social history: nonsmoker, occasional alcohol, graduate student. Family history is noncontributory. She has no recent antibiotic use and no history of recurrent infections beyond the single prior episode.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, comfortable young woman in no distress. Abdomen soft with mild suprapubic tenderness; no rebound or guarding. There is NO costovertebral angle tenderness bilaterally. No palpable masses. Cardiopulmonary exam normal. External genitalia normal without lesions, erythema, or discharge; she declines internal pelvic exam given absence of vaginal symptoms. No inguinal lymphadenopathy. Skin without rash. Neurologic exam normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In a healthy, nonpregnant young woman with classic dysuria, frequency, and urgency without systemic or vaginal symptoms, what is the most appropriate approach?
options: Refer to urology for cystoscopy as the first step today
Require a urine culture before starting any antibiotic
Obtain CT urography before treatment as the first step today
Diagnose uncomplicated cystitis clinically
stages.explanation: In a healthy nonpregnant woman, the classic triad of dysuria, frequency, and urgency without vaginal discharge/irritation has a high probability of uncomplicated cystitis; empiric treatment, often supported by a simple urinalysis/dipstick, is appropriate and routine urine culture is not required. CT urography is unnecessary imaging for straightforward cystitis. Mandatory pretreatment culture is reserved for complicated infections, treatment failure, recurrence, or pregnancy—not first-episode uncomplicated cystitis. Cystoscopy and urology referral are inappropriate for an uncomplicated acute infection and would only be considered for recurrent or atypical disease.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Urine dipstick: leukocyte esterase positive, nitrites positive
Urine microscopy: 25 WBC/hpf, moderate bacteria, no casts
Urine pH: 6.0
Beta-hCG: negative
No blood, no glucose, no protein

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which dipstick combination best supports the diagnosis of bacterial cystitis?
options: Positive bilirubin and urobilinogen
Positive leukocyte esterase
Positive blood and ketones
Positive glucose and protein
stages.explanation: Positive leukocyte esterase (indicating pyuria) plus positive nitrites (indicating nitrate-reducing Enterobacteriaceae such as E. coli) together strongly support bacterial cystitis and increase diagnostic confidence. Glucose and protein relate to diabetes and renal disease, not infection. Blood and ketones are nonspecific—hematuria can occur with cystitis but is not diagnostic, and ketones reflect fasting or diabetes. Bilirubin and urobilinogen point toward hepatobiliary disease. Among urinary nitrate reducers, gram-positive organisms and some others may not produce nitrites, so a negative nitrite does not exclude infection, but a positive nitrite with positive LE is the classic supportive pattern.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which is an appropriate first-line antibiotic for uncomplicated cystitis in this patient?
options: Ciprofloxacin for 14 days as initial therapy
IV ceftriaxone for 7 days as initial therapy
Amoxicillin for 3 days as initial therapy
Nitrofurantoin for 5 days
stages.explanation: Recommended first-line agents for uncomplicated cystitis are nitrofurantoin (5 days), trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (3 days, where local resistance is <20%), or single-dose fosfomycin; nitrofurantoin is excellent for cystitis because it concentrates in urine. Fluoroquinolones like ciprofloxacin are effective but are reserved as alternatives due to resistance concerns and serious adverse effects, and a 14-day course is excessive for cystitis. Amoxicillin alone has high E. coli resistance and poor efficacy. IV ceftriaxone is overtreatment for an uncomplicated lower tract infection in a well patient who can take orals.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: She asks how to reduce future infections, noting they follow intercourse. What is the most appropriate evidence-based advice?
options: Prophylactic antibiotics for life starting now with appropriate follow-up
Increase fluid intake and consider postcoital voiding
Routine cranberry tablets are proven to eliminate recurrence with appropriate follow-up
Avoid all sexual activity permanently with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: For a young woman with intercourse-associated cystitis, reasonable measures include increased fluid intake and behavioral counseling; if recurrences become frequent and clearly tied to intercourse, postcoital (single-dose) antibiotic prophylaxis is an effective, targeted strategy. Lifelong continuous antibiotics are not warranted after a second episode and promote resistance. Cranberry products have weak, inconsistent evidence and should not be presented as proven prevention. Advising permanent abstinence is neither evidence-based nor acceptable counseling; behavioral modification and selective prophylaxis are the appropriate tools.

cases.diagnosis: Uncomplicated cystitis
pearls: Uncomplicated cystitis in a healthy nonpregnant woman can be diagnosed clinically and treated empirically; routine culture is unnecessary.
Positive leukocyte esterase plus nitrites supports infection; nitrites imply nitrate-reducing Enterobacteriaceae like E. coli.
First-line agents: nitrofurantoin (5 d), TMP-SMX (3 d if resistance <20%), or single-dose fosfomycin; reserve fluoroquinolones.
For recurrent intercourse-associated UTIs, consider postcoital prophylaxis and increased hydration rather than lifelong antibiotics.

cases.title: Rising Creatinine in a Man with Cirrhosis
cases.chiefComplaint: Decreased urine output and rising creatinine in a hospitalized man with cirrhosis.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man with alcohol-related cirrhosis (Child-Pugh C) was admitted five days ago for tense ascites and is being treated for the complications of advanced liver disease. He has known portal hypertension, prior variceal bleeding, and recurrent ascites managed with spironolactone 100 mg and furosemide 40 mg daily. During admission he received a large-volume paracentesis without albumin and was started on lactulose for early encephalopathy. The team now notes his urine output has fallen and his creatinine has progressively risen. He denies recent NSAID use; he received one dose of IV contrast a week ago at an outside facility. No recent aminoglycosides. Medications: spironolactone, furosemide (both held yesterday), lactulose, nadolol. Allergies: none. Social: abstinent from alcohol for 3 months, former smoker. Family history noncontributory. He reports mild abdominal fullness but no fever, dysuria, melena, or diarrhea. He has had no episodes of overt GI bleeding this admission.
cases.exam: Cachectic, jaundiced man with scleral icterus. He is mildly somnolent but arousable, with subtle asterixis. Cardiovascular exam normal with no murmurs; warm extremities and bounding pulses. Lungs clear. Abdomen is distended with shifting dullness consistent with ascites; mild diffuse tenderness without rebound; no fluid-wave tenderness suggesting peritonitis. Spider angiomata and palmar erythema present. Lower extremities with 1+ pitting edema. No flank tenderness. Mucous membranes slightly dry. Foley drains scant concentrated urine.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Before diagnosing hepatorenal syndrome, what is the most important first step in evaluating his AKI?
options: Withhold diuretics and nephrotoxins, give a volume challenge with IV albumin, and exclude other causes of AKI
Start empiric antibiotics for spontaneous bacterial peritonitis without diagnostic paracentesis
Immediately start a vasoconstrictor (terlipressin/midodrine-octreotide) as the first step today
Begin hemodialysis as the first step today
stages.explanation: Hepatorenal syndrome is a diagnosis of exclusion. The required first step is to stop diuretics and nephrotoxins and confirm the AKI does not respond to volume expansion—withdrawal of diuretics plus a volume challenge with IV albumin (typically 1 g/kg/day) over 48 hours, while excluding prerenal azotemia, ATN, obstruction, and infection. Starting a vasoconstrictor presumes the diagnosis before exclusion. Hemodialysis is premature without an indication and is reserved for refractory cases or transplant bridging. Empiric antibiotics may be warranted if SBP is present, but a diagnostic paracentesis must be done to confirm SBP rather than treating blindly; SBP itself is a major precipitant of HRS and must be sought.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Serum creatinine: 2.6 mg/dL (admission 0.9 mg/dL)
BUN: 48 mg/dL
Serum sodium: 128 mEq/L
Urine sodium: 8 mEq/L
Fractional excretion of sodium (FENa): 0.2%
Urinalysis: bland; no protein, no blood, no casts; no granular casts
Urine output: 0.3 mL/kg/hr
Renal ultrasound: normal-sized kidneys, no hydronephrosis, no obstruction
Diagnostic paracentesis: 120 PMNs/µL (no SBP); ascitic fluid culture pending
No improvement in creatinine after 2 days of IV albumin and diuretic withdrawal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of findings most supports hepatorenal syndrome over acute tubular necrosis?
options: Very low urine sodium , FENa <0.1-0.2%, bland urine sediment,
Muddy brown granular casts with FENa >2% as the primary diagnosis
Hydronephrosis on ultrasound as the primary diagnosis
Heavy proteinuria with dysmorphic RBCs as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: HRS is a functional, vasomotor renal failure: intense renal vasoconstriction produces avid sodium retention, so urine sodium is very low (<10 mEq/L) and FENa is markedly low (<0.1-0.2%), with a bland sediment and no improvement after volume expansion and removal of diuretics. Muddy brown granular casts with high FENa indicate ATN, in which tubular injury impairs sodium reabsorption. Heavy proteinuria with dysmorphic RBCs suggests glomerulonephritis. Hydronephrosis indicates postrenal obstruction. The hallmark of HRS is the combination of profound sodium avidity, bland urine, and failure to respond to albumin in a patient with advanced liver disease.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After excluding other causes, what is the most appropriate pharmacologic therapy for HRS type 1 (acute HRS-AKI)?
options: Large-volume normal saline boluses repeated daily as initial therapy
A splanchnic vasoconstrictor combined with IV albumin
Low-dose 'renal' dopamine infusion as initial therapy
Aggressive IV furosemide to increase urine output as initial therapy
stages.explanation: HRS results from splanchnic vasodilation and effective arterial underfilling; the mainstay of medical therapy is a vasoconstrictor—terlipressin (preferred where available), norepinephrine, or midodrine plus octreotide—combined with IV albumin to raise effective arterial volume and improve renal perfusion. Furosemide worsens intravascular depletion and does not address the pathophysiology. 'Renal-dose' dopamine has no proven benefit and is not recommended. Repeated saline boluses do not correct the underlying arterial underfilling and risk worsening ascites and hyponatremia; albumin, not crystalloid, is the volume expander of choice. Definitive treatment is liver transplantation.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: His family asks about the definitive treatment and prognosis. What is the most accurate statement?
options: Liver transplantation is the definitive treatment
HRS always resolves spontaneously without intervention with appropriate follow-up
HRS is permanent kidney damage requiring lifelong dialysis regardless of liver status
Chronic immunosuppression cures HRS with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: HRS is a functional renal failure driven by the failing liver and circulatory dysfunction, so liver transplantation is the definitive treatment and frequently reverses the kidney injury because the kidneys themselves are structurally intact. It is therefore not inherently permanent damage requiring lifelong dialysis, though dialysis may bridge severely ill patients to transplant. HRS does not reliably resolve spontaneously; untreated type 1 HRS carries a very poor short-term prognosis. Immunosuppression treats rejection after transplant but does not itself cure HRS. Vasoconstrictors plus albumin are a bridge; transplant is the cure.

cases.diagnosis: Hepatorenal syndrome
pearls: HRS is a diagnosis of exclusion: stop diuretics/nephrotoxins, exclude ATN, obstruction, and infection, and confirm no response to a 48-hour albumin volume challenge.
Hallmark labs: very low urine sodium (<10 mEq/L), FENa <0.1-0.2%, and a bland urine sediment—functional, not structural, injury.
Albumin without replacement after large-volume paracentesis, SBP, and GI bleeding are common precipitants—give albumin with LVP.
Vasoconstrictors (terlipressin/norepinephrine/midodrine-octreotide) plus albumin are a bridge; liver transplant is definitive.

cases.title: Worsening Kidney Function During Diuresis for Heart Failure
cases.chiefComplaint: Rising creatinine while being treated for a heart failure exacerbation.
cases.history: A 74-year-old man was admitted three days ago with acute decompensated heart failure: progressive dyspnea, orthopnea, weight gain of 6 kg, and lower extremity edema. He has ischemic cardiomyopathy with a left ventricular ejection fraction of 25%, prior myocardial infarction, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and stage 3 CKD (baseline creatinine ~1.4 mg/dL). On admission he was placed on an aggressive IV furosemide regimen with a robust diuretic response, losing 5 kg over three days. The team now notes his creatinine has climbed and asks for guidance. Medications: home lisinopril 20 mg daily, carvedilol, atorvastatin, aspirin, and IV furosemide drip in hospital; ACE inhibitor was continued. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker, no alcohol. Family history: coronary disease. He feels improved with less dyspnea and can now lie flat, and his weight is approaching his estimated dry weight. No fever, chest pain, dysuria, or hematuria.
cases.exam: Comfortable elderly man, no longer in respiratory distress. Jugular venous pressure now approximately 7 cm (down from markedly elevated on admission). Lungs with minimal bibasilar crackles, much improved. Cardiac exam: regular rhythm, soft S3, no new murmurs. Abdomen soft, nontender, no hepatomegaly or ascites. Lower extremities with trace edema, down from 3+ on admission. Extremities warm and well perfused with normal capillary refill. No flank tenderness. Mucous membranes moist.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His creatinine rose during effective decongestion of heart failure. What is the most likely explanation for this pattern?
options: Acute tubular necrosis from the furosemide as the first step today
Acute interstitial nephritis from carvedilol as the first step today
Postrenal obstruction as the first step today
Cardiorenal syndrome
stages.explanation: Worsening renal function during treatment of decompensated heart failure—from reduced cardiac output, venous congestion, neurohormonal activation, and the hemodynamic effects of diuresis and RAAS blockade—defines cardiorenal syndrome. Loop diuretics rarely cause ATN at standard doses; a creatinine rise during decongestion is hemodynamic, not necessarily tubular injury, and is often acceptable if the patient is improving. Acute interstitial nephritis would typically show pyuria/eosinophiluria and is not characteristic of carvedilol in this scenario. Postrenal obstruction is excluded by the clinical picture and would be confirmed by ultrasound; nothing suggests obstruction here.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Serum creatinine: 2.0 mg/dL (baseline 1.4, admission 1.5)
BUN: 52 mg/dL (BUN:Cr ratio elevated ~26)
Serum sodium: 137 mEq/L
Serum potassium: 4.6 mEq/L
Urinalysis: bland, no casts, no protein, no blood
Net fluid balance: negative 5 L since admission
Weight: down 5 kg, approaching dry weight
BNP: improving from admission value

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: His BUN:Cr ratio is elevated at ~26 with a bland sediment. Together with his clinical improvement, what does this most likely indicate?
options: Obstructive uropathy as the primary diagnosis
A prerenal/hemodynamic physiology with relative intravascular volume reduction from diuresis, in a patient who is clinically improving
Intrinsic renal injury (ATN) requiring stopping diuresis entirely as the primary diagnosis
Glomerulonephritis as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: An elevated BUN:Cr ratio (>20) with a bland urinary sediment in a decongesting heart failure patient reflects a prerenal/hemodynamic physiology—reduced effective renal perfusion as intravascular volume is removed—rather than intrinsic tubular damage. Because he is symptomatically improving with falling JVP and weight near dry, a modest creatinine rise can be tolerated. ATN would show muddy brown casts and a lower BUN:Cr ratio. Glomerulonephritis would show hematuria/proteinuria/RBC casts. Obstructive uropathy would be evident on imaging. The key is recognizing a hemodynamic creatinine rise in the context of successful decongestion.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is now near euvolemia (JVP 7 cm, trace edema, dry weight) but creatinine is 2.0. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Start hemodialysis as initial therapy
Give large-volume IV normal saline boluses to drop the creatinine as initial therapy
Reassess volume status
Continue the same aggressive IV furosemide drip unchanged as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Once a patient approaches euvolemia, continued aggressive diuresis risks over-diuresis and worsening prerenal azotemia; the correct step is to reassess volume status and slow or reduce diuretic dosing while monitoring renal function and electrolytes. Continuing the unchanged drip in a near-euvolemic patient will worsen the prerenal picture. Reflexively giving saline boluses to a heart failure patient can re-precipitate congestion and pulmonary edema and is the wrong instinct. Hemodialysis is not indicated—there is no refractory volume overload, severe electrolyte disturbance, or uremia; the creatinine rise is hemodynamic and expected to stabilize.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Regarding his ACE inhibitor (lisinopril) during this admission, which statement is most accurate?
options: It must be permanently discontinued because it caused irreversible kidney damage with appropriate follow-up
A modest, stable creatinine rise with normal potassium is often acceptable, as ACE inhibitors provide long-term mortality benefit in HFrEF
ACE inhibitors have no effect on renal hemodynamics with appropriate follow-up
It should be replaced immediately with an NSAID for symptom control with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: ACE inhibitors reduce mortality in heart failure with reduced ejection fraction and cause a predictable, usually modest rise in creatinine by dilating the efferent arteriole and lowering intraglomerular pressure; a stable rise (commonly up to ~30%) with acceptable potassium does not mandate discontinuation and the drug should be continued with monitoring. Permanent discontinuation is not warranted for an expected hemodynamic effect. ACE inhibitors clearly do affect renal hemodynamics (efferent arteriolar dilation). Substituting an NSAID would be harmful—NSAIDs worsen renal perfusion, blunt diuretics, and exacerbate heart failure.

cases.diagnosis: Cardiorenal syndrome
pearls: Cardiorenal syndrome is worsening renal function from the interplay of cardiac dysfunction, venous congestion, and decongestive/RAAS therapy.
A modest creatinine rise with an elevated BUN:Cr ratio and bland sediment during decongestion is usually hemodynamic—acceptable if the patient is improving.
Near euvolemia, slow diuresis rather than reflexively giving fluids; saline can re-precipitate congestion.
ACE inhibitors/ARBs cause a predictable creatinine bump but confer mortality benefit in HFrEF—continue with monitoring unless the rise is excessive or hyperkalemia develops.

cases.title: A Toddler with Bloody Diarrhea and Pallor
cases.chiefComplaint: Decreased urination and pallor following a week of bloody diarrhea.
cases.history: A 4-year-old previously healthy girl is admitted after one week of abdominal pain and watery diarrhea that became grossly bloody on day three. She attended a petting zoo and ate an undercooked hamburger at a family barbecue about ten days ago; two other children at the event had diarrheal illness. Over the past two days her parents note pallor, irritability, decreased oral intake, and markedly reduced urine output—only one wet diaper today. There is no recent antibiotic use; she did not receive antidiarrheals. She is fully immunized. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: attends preschool. Family history is noncontributory. On review of systems she has had low-grade fevers and now appears puffy around the eyes. No rash, no joint complaints, no prior bleeding tendency. Her stool culture from the outside clinic is pending. Parents report the diarrhea is now improving but she looks 'more sick' overall.
cases.exam: Pale, tired-appearing child, mildly irritable but consolable. Conjunctival pallor and mild periorbital edema. Scattered petechiae on the lower legs. Cardiovascular: tachycardic, regular, soft flow murmur (consistent with anemia). Abdomen soft, mildly diffusely tender, no rebound, no hepatosplenomegaly, normal bowel sounds. No focal neurologic deficits; she is interactive. Mild pitting edema of the lower extremities. Mucous membranes moist. Skin without purpura beyond the petechiae noted. Capillary refill 2 seconds.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given a young child with preceding bloody diarrhea now developing pallor, petechiae, and oliguria, which diagnostic triad should you evaluate for?
options: Eosinophilia, rash, and fever as the first step today
Hypertension, proteinuria, and edema only in this clinical context
Leukocytosis, hypoglycemia, and seizures in this clinical context
Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia,
stages.explanation: The clinical scenario—bloody diarrhea followed by pallor (anemia), petechiae (thrombocytopenia), and oliguria (AKI)—is classic for hemolytic uremic syndrome, defined by the triad of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and acute kidney injury. Leukocytosis with hypoglycemia and seizures is not the defining triad of HUS, though neurologic involvement can occur in severe cases. Hypertension, proteinuria, and edema describe a nephritic/nephrotic pattern but omit the hematologic features central to HUS. Eosinophilia, rash, and fever suggest a drug reaction (DRESS) or interstitial nephritis, not post-diarrheal HUS.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hemoglobin: 7.2 g/dL
Platelets: 38 ×10³/µL
Peripheral smear: numerous schistocytes, helmet cells
LDH: 1850 U/L (markedly elevated)
Haptoglobin: undetectable
Reticulocyte count: elevated
Total bilirubin: 2.4 mg/dL (indirect predominant)
Serum creatinine: 1.8 mg/dL (markedly elevated for age)
BUN: 60 mg/dL
Coombs (direct antiglobulin) test: negative
PT/PTT: normal; fibrinogen normal
Stool: Shiga toxin-producing E. coli O157:H7 confirmed

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which set of findings confirms microangiopathic hemolytic anemia and distinguishes this from disseminated intravascular coagulation?
options: A positive direct Coombs test as the primary diagnosis
Schistocytes with elevated LDH
Normal LDH with high haptoglobin as the primary diagnosis
Prolonged PT/PTT with low fibrinogen and elevated D-dimer
stages.explanation: MAHA is confirmed by schistocytes on smear, elevated LDH, low/undetectable haptoglobin, elevated indirect bilirubin, and a negative direct Coombs (mechanical, non-immune hemolysis). Crucially, in HUS the coagulation studies (PT, PTT, fibrinogen) are normal, which distinguishes it from DIC, where consumptive coagulopathy prolongs PT/PTT, lowers fibrinogen, and raises D-dimer. A positive Coombs test would indicate immune-mediated (autoimmune) hemolysis, not the mechanical hemolysis of a thrombotic microangiopathy. Normal LDH with high haptoglobin would argue against active hemolysis altogether. Normal coagulation with active MAHA is the key discriminator from DIC.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of management for typical (Shiga toxin-mediated) HUS in this child?
options: High-dose corticosteroids as initial therapy
Meticulous supportive care
Urgent plasma exchange as first-line therapy as initial therapy
Empiric antibiotics and anti-motility agents to treat the E. coli as monotherapy
stages.explanation: Typical HUS is managed primarily with supportive care: careful fluid and electrolyte management, treatment of hypertension, judicious red cell transfusion for symptomatic anemia, and renal replacement therapy (dialysis) when indicated for severe AKI, fluid overload, or refractory electrolyte disturbance. Antibiotics and anti-motility agents are AVOIDED in STEC infection because they may increase toxin release and the risk of HUS. Plasma exchange is the treatment for TTP, not first-line for typical pediatric STEC-HUS. Corticosteroids have no established role in typical HUS. Most children recover with supportive care alone.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Her parents ask about prognosis. What is the most accurate statement about typical HUS?
options: It is uniformly fatal without bone marrow transplant with appropriate follow-up
It always progresses to lifelong dialysis dependence with appropriate follow-up
Recurrence is the rule, unlike atypical HUS with appropriate follow-up
Most children recover renal function with supportive care, though a minority have long-term renal sequelae
stages.explanation: The prognosis for typical (STEC) HUS is generally favorable: the majority of children recover renal function with supportive care, although a minority develop long-term sequelae such as hypertension, chronic kidney disease, or proteinuria and require follow-up. It is not uniformly fatal, and bone marrow transplant has no role. It does not always lead to lifelong dialysis—most regain function. Recurrence is characteristic of atypical (complement-mediated) HUS, not typical STEC-HUS, which usually does not recur once the inciting infection resolves.

cases.diagnosis: Typical hemolytic uremic syndrome
pearls: Typical HUS follows Shiga toxin-producing E. coli (often O157:H7) with the triad of MAHA, thrombocytopenia, and AKI after bloody diarrhea.
MAHA shows schistocytes, high LDH, low haptoglobin, indirect hyperbilirubinemia, and a negative Coombs; normal PT/PTT/fibrinogen distinguishes it from DIC.
Avoid antibiotics and anti-motility agents in STEC infection—they may increase toxin release and HUS risk.
Management is supportive (fluids, transfusion, dialysis as needed); most children recover renal function.

cases.title: Anemia and Kidney Failure Without Diarrhea
cases.chiefComplaint: Fatigue, dark urine, and rapidly worsening kidney function without preceding diarrhea.
cases.history: A 31-year-old woman is admitted to the ICU with a one-week history of fatigue, bruising, dark urine, and decreasing urine output. She denies any diarrheal illness, recent travel, or undercooked food exposure. She had an uncomplicated delivery two months ago, which she identifies as a recent stressor. There is no recent medication change and no new drugs (no quinine, no chemotherapy, no calcineurin inhibitors). She has a family history notable for a brother who required a kidney transplant in his twenties for an unclear cause and a maternal aunt with 'kidney problems.' Medications: prenatal vitamins only. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, no alcohol, no illicit drugs. On review of systems she reports no fevers, no neurologic symptoms such as confusion or focal weakness, and no joint pains or rashes. Blood pressure has been elevated. She has no prior history of thrombocytopenia or anemia. The hematology and nephrology teams are consulted for a thrombotic microangiopathy.
cases.exam: Pale, tired-appearing woman, alert and fully oriented with no focal neurologic deficits. Scattered bruises on the arms and legs; no purpura. Cardiovascular: regular rhythm, no murmur. Lungs clear. Abdomen soft, nontender, no organomegaly. No costovertebral angle tenderness. Lower extremities with trace edema. Mucous membranes moist. Skin without rash. Fundoscopic exam shows mild hypertensive changes. Neurologic exam is entirely normal, with intact strength, sensation, and mentation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: She has MAHA, thrombocytopenia, and AKI but NO diarrhea and a suggestive family history. Which initial workup best narrows the cause of this thrombotic microangiopathy?
options: ADAMTS13 activity, complement studies,
Echocardiogram as the first step today
Stool studies for Shiga toxin only as the first step today
Bone marrow biopsy as the first step today
stages.explanation: A TMA without diarrhea requires distinguishing among TTP (severe ADAMTS13 deficiency), complement-mediated (atypical) HUS, and secondary TMAs; the key initial workup is ADAMTS13 activity, complement studies/genetics, and screening for secondary triggers such as pregnancy, drugs, and autoimmune disease. Stool Shiga toxin testing is appropriate when diarrhea-associated HUS is suspected, but the absence of diarrhea makes typical HUS unlikely. A bone marrow biopsy is not indicated for a TMA with an obvious peripheral mechanism. An echocardiogram does not address the diagnostic question. The ADAMTS13 result, in particular, is pivotal because severe deficiency defines TTP and changes management.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hemoglobin: 8.0 g/dL
Platelets: 55 ×10³/µL
Peripheral smear: schistocytes present
LDH: 1100 U/L (elevated)
Haptoglobin: low
Direct Coombs: negative
Serum creatinine: 4.2 mg/dL (severe AKI)
PT/PTT/fibrinogen: normal
ADAMTS13 activity: 65% (normal; NOT severely deficient)
Stool Shiga toxin: negative
Complement: low C3, elevated soluble C5b-9; genetic testing reveals a complement factor H mutation

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given prominent AKI, ADAMTS13 activity of 65%, negative Shiga toxin, and a complement factor H mutation, what is the diagnosis?
options: Disseminated intravascular coagulation
Atypical hemolytic uremic syndrome
Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura
Typical Shiga toxin-mediated HUS
stages.explanation: Atypical HUS is a complement-mediated TMA caused by dysregulation of the alternative complement pathway (e.g., factor H mutations), presenting with MAHA, thrombocytopenia, and prominent AKI without diarrhea; the complement factor H mutation and elevated soluble C5b-9 confirm complement activation. TTP is excluded because ADAMTS13 activity is not severely deficient (<10% is required) and renal failure tends to be less prominent than neurologic disease. Typical HUS is excluded by negative Shiga toxin and the absence of diarrhea. DIC is excluded by normal PT/PTT and fibrinogen. The family history and postpartum trigger further support a complement-mediated process.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive, disease-specific therapy for atypical (complement-mediated) HUS?
options: Plasma exchange as lifelong monotherapy
Eculizumab
High-dose corticosteroids alone
Splenectomy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Eculizumab, a monoclonal antibody that blocks terminal complement at C5, is the definitive, disease-specific therapy for atypical HUS; it halts the complement-mediated endothelial injury and improves both hematologic and renal outcomes. Plasma exchange was historically used and may be started empirically while ADAMTS13 results are pending (because TTP must not be missed), but it is not the definitive therapy for aHUS and is not adequate lifelong monotherapy. Corticosteroids alone do not address complement dysregulation. Splenectomy has no role in aHUS. Patients on eculizumab require meningococcal vaccination and prophylaxis because of increased Neisseria infection risk.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Before starting the chosen complement-directed therapy, which preventive measure is essential?
options: Vaccinate against Neisseria meningitidis , since terminal complement blockade increases meningococcal risk
Administer prophylactic platelet transfusions as the leading concern
Start anticoagulation with warfarin as the leading concern
Begin prophylactic antifungal therapy as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Terminal complement inhibition with eculizumab markedly increases susceptibility to Neisseria meningitidis, so meningococcal vaccination (and antibiotic prophylaxis when therapy must begin before vaccine protection develops) is essential before or at initiation. Antifungal prophylaxis is not routinely indicated. Empiric anticoagulation with warfarin is not part of aHUS management and adds bleeding risk in a thrombocytopenic patient. Prophylactic platelet transfusions are generally avoided in TMAs unless there is active bleeding or a planned invasive procedure, as they may fuel ongoing microthrombosis; meningococcal protection is the critical, specific safeguard with this drug.

cases.diagnosis: Atypical hemolytic uremic syndrome
pearls: Atypical HUS is a complement-mediated TMA (alternative pathway dysregulation, e.g., factor H mutation) with MAHA, thrombocytopenia, and prominent AKI—typically WITHOUT diarrhea.
ADAMTS13 activity is NOT severely reduced (helps exclude TTP); pregnancy can trigger aHUS, and family history is common.
Eculizumab (terminal C5 inhibitor) is the definitive therapy; plasma exchange may bridge while excluding TTP.
Vaccinate against (and provide prophylaxis for) Neisseria meningitidis before complement blockade.

cases.title: Confusion, Bruising, and Fever in a Middle-Aged Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Fluctuating confusion, easy bruising, and fever over several days.
cases.history: A 43-year-old woman is admitted to the ICU after presenting with several days of headache, intermittent confusion, transient slurred speech, and easy bruising. Her husband reports she has seemed 'not herself,' with episodes of disorientation that wax and wane. She has noted dark urine and feels feverish. She has no diarrhea and no recent infectious exposures. She has a history of hypothyroidism on levothyroxine and was recently started on no new medications other than an over-the-counter supplement. She has no known autoimmune disease. Medications: levothyroxine. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, social alcohol only, works as an accountant. Family history is noncontributory. On review of systems she reports fatigue and one brief episode of left-hand weakness that resolved. There is no history of prior bleeding disorder, no melena, and no hematuria beyond the dark urine. She is admitted for evaluation of thrombocytopenia with neurologic changes and possible thrombotic microangiopathy.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, intermittently confused woman; oriented to person and place but not date, with fluctuating attention. Scattered petechiae over the shins and several ecchymoses on the arms. Mild scleral icterus. Cardiovascular: tachycardic, regular, no murmur. Lungs clear. Abdomen soft, nontender, no organomegaly. Neurologic: no fixed focal deficit at present, but a transient subtle right-sided drift was observed; speech is intermittently slowed. No neck stiffness. Skin without purpura beyond petechiae/ecchymoses. Mucous membranes show a small area of gingival bleeding.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: She has thrombocytopenia, MAHA features, fever, neurologic signs, and mild renal involvement. Which classic pentad raises concern for thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura?
options: Fever, anemia , thrombocytopenia, neurologic symptoms,
Fever, leukocytosis, hyperkalemia, seizures, and rash in this clinical context
Jaundice, ascites, encephalopathy, coagulopathy, and varices
Hypertension, edema, proteinuria, hematuria, and oliguria
stages.explanation: The classic (though now rarely complete) pentad of TTP is fever, microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, neurologic abnormalities, and renal dysfunction; her fluctuating confusion, transient focal signs, petechiae, fever, and dark urine fit this picture, with neurologic involvement typically more prominent than in HUS. The second option lists nonspecific features without MAHA. The third describes a nephritic syndrome. The fourth describes decompensated cirrhosis. Importantly, the modern threshold to treat TTP is the dyad of MAHA plus thrombocytopenia without another cause—one need not wait for the full pentad.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hemoglobin: 7.8 g/dL
Platelets: 12 ×10³/µL (severe thrombocytopenia)
Peripheral smear: abundant schistocytes
LDH: 1400 U/L (elevated)
Haptoglobin: undetectable
Indirect bilirubin: elevated
Direct Coombs: negative
Serum creatinine: 1.5 mg/dL (mildly elevated)
PT/PTT/fibrinogen: normal
ADAMTS13 activity: <5% (severely deficient) with inhibitor present
Stool Shiga toxin: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which laboratory result is most specific in confirming TTP and distinguishing it from atypical HUS?
options: Mildly elevated creatinine as the primary diagnosis
Elevated LDH as the primary diagnosis
Negative direct Coombs test in this clinical context
Severe ADAMTS13 deficiency , here with an inhibitor
stages.explanation: Severe ADAMTS13 deficiency (activity <10%), typically due to an autoantibody inhibitor in acquired TTP, is the specific finding that confirms TTP and distinguishes it from atypical HUS, where ADAMTS13 is normal or only modestly reduced. ADAMTS13 normally cleaves ultralarge von Willebrand factor multimers; its severe deficiency leads to platelet-rich microthrombi. Elevated LDH reflects hemolysis/tissue injury but is nonspecific. A mildly elevated creatinine is compatible with TTP (renal involvement is usually less severe than in HUS) but is not diagnostic. A negative Coombs test confirms non-immune hemolysis common to all TMAs and does not separate TTP from aHUS.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the immediate, life-saving first-line treatment for acquired TTP?
options: Urgent plasma exchange , typically with corticosteroids
Eculizumab as initial therapy
Observation with serial platelet counts as initial therapy
Platelet transfusion to correct the thrombocytopenia
stages.explanation: Urgent plasma exchange (plasmapheresis) is the immediate, life-saving therapy for TTP: it removes the ADAMTS13 inhibitor and replaces functional ADAMTS13, and it should be started emergently—often before confirmatory ADAMTS13 results return—usually combined with corticosteroids; rituximab and caplacizumab are important adjuncts. Platelet transfusion is generally CONTRAINDICATED in TTP except for life-threatening hemorrhage, because it can fuel further microthrombosis. Eculizumab treats atypical HUS, not TTP. Observation is dangerous given the high mortality of untreated TTP; the disease is a hematologic emergency requiring prompt plasma exchange.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Her platelet count is 12 ×10³/µL with petechiae and gingival bleeding. The covering team proposes a prophylactic platelet transfusion. What is the most appropriate response?
options: Avoid prophylactic platelet transfusion unless there is life-threatening bleeding, as it may worsen microthrombosis
Transfuse platelets and start aspirin as the leading concern
Delay plasma exchange until platelets normalize as the leading concern
Give platelets routinely because the count is very low as the leading concern
stages.explanation: In TTP, prophylactic platelet transfusions are generally avoided because adding platelets to a prothrombotic, ADAMTS13-deficient state can precipitate further microvascular thrombosis and clinical deterioration; they are reserved for life-threatening hemorrhage or before urgent invasive procedures. Minor mucocutaneous bleeding with a low count does not by itself justify transfusion. Starting aspirin in a severely thrombocytopenic patient adds bleeding risk and is not the priority. Plasma exchange must not be delayed waiting for platelet recovery—prompt plasmapheresis is the treatment that actually reverses the disease and raises the platelet count.

cases.diagnosis: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura
pearls: TTP classic pentad: fever, MAHA, thrombocytopenia, neurologic signs, and renal dysfunction—but treat on the dyad of MAHA + thrombocytopenia without another cause.
Severe ADAMTS13 deficiency (<10%), often with an inhibitor, confirms TTP and distinguishes it from atypical HUS (normal/near-normal ADAMTS13).
Urgent plasma exchange plus corticosteroids is first-line and life-saving; rituximab and caplacizumab are key adjuncts.
Avoid platelet transfusions in TTP except for life-threatening bleeding—they can worsen microthrombosis.

cases.title: Severe Headache and Rising Creatinine in the ICU
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe headache, blurry vision, and decreasing urine output over two days.
cases.history: A 49-year-old woman with diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis diagnosed 8 months ago is admitted to the ICU with a 2-day history of pounding frontal headache, blurry vision, and dyspnea. Her skin thickening has progressed rapidly over the past 3 months, now involving the trunk and proximal arms, and she has new puffy hands and palpitations. She denies chest pain. Home medications are nifedipine 30 mg daily for Raynaud phenomenon and omeprazole 20 mg daily; she was started on prednisone 40 mg daily 6 weeks ago for worsening arthralgias and tendon friction rubs. No NSAIDs. Allergies: none. She does not smoke or drink. Family history is unremarkable. On review of systems she reports frothy urine and mild ankle swelling. She has had no prior hypertension; her clinic blood pressures were normal 2 months ago. She has had no fevers, hemoptysis, or recent contrast exposure. Outpatient labs 6 weeks ago showed a creatinine of 0.8 mg/dL.
cases.exam: Anxious woman in mild distress. Fundoscopy shows flame hemorrhages and cotton-wool spots without papilledema appreciated bedside. Skin is diffusely thickened and taut over the face, trunk, and proximal extremities with sclerodactyly; tendon friction rubs palpable at the wrists. Cardiopulmonary exam reveals an S4 gallop and bibasilar crackles. Abdomen is soft and non-tender without bruits. There is trace bilateral pedal edema. Neurologic exam is non-focal; she is alert and oriented. No malar rash, no synovitis, no oral ulcers, and no palpable purpura.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given her diffuse scleroderma, recent corticosteroid exposure, and new severe hypertension with AKI, which is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Lupus nephritis flare
Scleroderma renal crisis
Pheochromocytoma crisis
Hypertensive emergency from essential hypertension
stages.explanation: Scleroderma renal crisis (SRC) is the unifying diagnosis: abrupt malignant hypertension and rapidly rising creatinine in a patient with early diffuse cutaneous disease, rapidly progressive skin thickening, and recent high-dose corticosteroids (a known precipitant). Flame hemorrhages reflect a hypertensive emergency, and tendon friction rubs are an additional SRC risk marker. Essential hypertension does not explain the acute fulminant course with AKI in a previously normotensive young woman. Lupus nephritis is wrong because she lacks malar rash, oral ulcers, synovitis, or serologic clues, and her phenotype is clearly scleroderma. Pheochromocytoma typically causes paroxysmal episodic symptoms with sweating and tachycardia rather than progressive skin disease and steroid-triggered renal failure.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Na 138 mEq/L
K 4.6 mEq/L
HCO3 20 mEq/L
BUN 48 mg/dL
Creatinine 2.9 mg/dL (baseline 0.8)
Hemoglobin 8.9 g/dL
Platelets 92 ×10³/µL
LDH 410 U/L
Haptoglobin <10 mg/dL
Peripheral smear: schistocytes present
Urinalysis: 1+ protein, few granular casts, no dysmorphic RBCs
Renin (plasma): markedly elevated
ANA 1:640 nucleolar; anti-RNA polymerase III positive
Chest X-ray: mild pulmonary vascular congestion

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The smear, LDH, haptoglobin, and platelet findings indicate which associated process?
options: Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia
Immune thrombocytopenic purpura
Disseminated intravascular coagulation
Vitamin B12 deficiency
stages.explanation: Schistocytes, elevated LDH, undetectable haptoglobin, anemia, and thrombocytopenia define a microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA), which accompanies scleroderma renal crisis from endothelial injury and intrarenal thrombotic microangiopathy. DIC is wrong because coagulation parameters (PT/PTT/fibrinogen) are not deranged here and the picture is driven by mechanical RBC shearing, not consumptive coagulopathy. ITP causes isolated thrombocytopenia without hemolysis, schistocytes, or LDH elevation. B12 deficiency can cause intramedullary hemolysis with high LDH but produces macrocytosis, hypersegmented neutrophils, and pancytopenia over months, not acute schistocytic hemolysis with renal failure and severe hypertension.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most important immediate pharmacologic intervention?
options: Initiate plasma exchange as initial therapy
Intravenous methylprednisolone pulse as initial therapy
Intravenous nitroprusside infusion as initial therapy
Short-acting oral or IV ACE inhibitor , titrated aggressively
stages.explanation: ACE inhibitors—classically captopril titrated rapidly—are the cornerstone of SRC therapy and dramatically improve survival by counteracting the renin-angiotensin overactivation driving the crisis; they are continued even if creatinine rises further because renal recovery often follows over weeks. Nitroprusside controls pressure but does not address the renin-mediated mechanism and risks cyanide toxicity in renal failure; it is at best adjunctive. High-dose corticosteroids are contraindicated—they precipitate SRC and worsen outcomes. Plasma exchange is the treatment for TTP, not SRC; although the smear shows MAHA, the mechanism here is angiotensin-driven thrombotic microangiopathy, and ACEi, not PLEX, is the answer.
stages.answer_text: Short-acting oral or IV ACE inhibitor (captopril), titrated aggressively

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: After ACE inhibitor initiation her creatinine rises to 4.5 mg/dL with worsening oliguria and pulmonary edema. What is the appropriate next step?
options: Add high-dose corticosteroids for the thrombotic microangiopathy
Continue the ACE inhibitor
Discontinue the ACE inhibitor immediately as the leading concern
Switch to an angiotensin receptor blocker as the leading concern
stages.explanation: In SRC, an early creatinine rise is expected and is NOT a reason to stop the ACE inhibitor; it should be continued—often at full dose—because long-term renal recovery and survival depend on it, and many patients who require temporary dialysis later recover enough renal function to come off it. Renal replacement therapy bridges patients through severe AKI, fluid overload, and uremia. Stopping the ACEi worsens outcomes. ARBs are not equivalent first-line agents in SRC and should not replace a tolerated ACEi. Corticosteroids are contraindicated as they precipitate and worsen SRC, so adding them is harmful.
stages.answer_text: Continue the ACE inhibitor and initiate renal replacement therapy as needed

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Which serologic finding in this patient most strongly predicted her risk of developing scleroderma renal crisis?
options: Anti-RNA polymerase III antibody
Anti-Scl-70 (topoisomerase I) antibody
Anti-Ro/SSA antibody in this setting
Anti-centromere antibody
stages.explanation: Anti-RNA polymerase III antibody is strongly associated with diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis, rapidly progressive skin involvement, and the highest risk of scleroderma renal crisis—exactly this patient's course. Anti-centromere antibody is linked to LIMITED cutaneous disease and pulmonary arterial hypertension, with LOW renal crisis risk. Anti-Scl-70 (topoisomerase I) is associated with diffuse disease and interstitial lung disease but is a weaker predictor of renal crisis than RNA polymerase III. Anti-Ro/SSA is characteristic of Sjögren syndrome and lupus, not a marker of scleroderma renal crisis risk.

cases.diagnosis: Scleroderma renal crisis with microangiopathic hemolytic anemia in diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis
pearls: Scleroderma renal crisis presents as abrupt malignant hypertension plus rapidly rising creatinine in early diffuse disease; recent high-dose corticosteroids are a key precipitant.
ACE inhibitors (captopril) are first-line and improve survival—continue them even as creatinine rises, and dialyze through severe AKI since renal recovery is common.
Anti-RNA polymerase III antibody carries the highest risk of renal crisis; anti-centromere (limited disease) carries low risk.
A coexisting MAHA (schistocytes, low haptoglobin, high LDH, thrombocytopenia) reflects thrombotic microangiopathy and does not require plasma exchange in SRC.

cases.title: New Swelling and High Blood Pressure at 32 Weeks
cases.chiefComplaint: Facial and leg swelling with headache at 32 weeks of pregnancy.
cases.history: A 29-year-old woman, G1P0 at 32 weeks gestation, is admitted for evaluation of new lower-extremity and facial swelling, mild headache, and blood pressure readings of 158/102 mmHg in clinic. Her pregnancy had been uncomplicated, with normal blood pressures and no proteinuria at her 20-week visit. Over the past week she has noticed rapid weight gain, puffy eyelids in the morning, and 'foamy' urine. She denies right-upper-quadrant pain, visual scotomata, or seizure activity, though she reports occasional mild epigastric discomfort. She takes only a prenatal vitamin and has no drug allergies. She has no personal or family history of chronic hypertension, diabetes, or kidney disease, but her mother had 'toxemia' during pregnancy. She does not smoke, drink, or use drugs. Fetal movements are normal. She has had no fevers, dysuria, or flank pain. Her pre-pregnancy weight was normal and she has had no prior pregnancies or miscarriages.
cases.exam: Well-appearing gravid woman. Periorbital and facial edema noted. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal with clear lungs. Abdomen is gravid, fundal height consistent with dates, non-tender; no right-upper-quadrant tenderness. There is 2+ pitting edema to the mid-shins bilaterally and mild hand swelling. Deep tendon reflexes are brisk (3+) at the patellae without clonus. Neurologic exam is otherwise non-focal; no visual field deficit. Fetal heart tones are present at 142 bpm. No costovertebral angle tenderness and no rash.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most important initial test to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Antinuclear antibody panel as the first step today
24-hour urine protein or urine protein-to-creatinine ratio
Serum uric acid level as the first step today
Renal ultrasound with Doppler as the first step today
stages.explanation: Preeclampsia is defined by new-onset hypertension after 20 weeks plus proteinuria (or end-organ dysfunction); quantifying proteinuria with a urine protein-to-creatinine ratio or 24-hour collection is the key confirmatory step. Renal ultrasound is not diagnostic of preeclampsia and would be normal. Serum uric acid is often elevated in preeclampsia and can support the diagnosis but is nonspecific and not part of the diagnostic criteria. An ANA panel evaluates for lupus, which is not suggested by this presentation of new gestational hypertension with edema and foamy urine after a normal 20-week visit.
stages.answer_text: 24-hour urine protein or urine protein-to-creatinine ratio

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Urine protein-to-creatinine ratio 1.8 mg/mg
Urinalysis: 3+ protein, no blood, no casts
Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (baseline 0.6)
Uric acid 7.4 mg/dL
AST 28 U/L
ALT 24 U/L
Platelets 188 ×10³/µL
Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL
LDH 230 U/L
Spot 24-hour estimate: ~2.0 g protein/day

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the clinical picture and labs, what is the diagnosis?
options: Chronic hypertension
Gestational hypertension without proteinuria
Preeclampsia with renal involvement
HELLP syndrome in this clinical context
stages.explanation: New-onset hypertension after 20 weeks with significant proteinuria (protein-to-creatinine 1.8, ~2 g/day) and a rising creatinine establishes preeclampsia with renal involvement. Gestational hypertension is defined by hypertension WITHOUT proteinuria or end-organ involvement, which is excluded here by the heavy proteinuria. Chronic hypertension would predate pregnancy or appear before 20 weeks; her 20-week visit was normal. HELLP syndrome requires Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, and Low Platelets—her transaminases, LDH, and platelet count are normal, so HELLP is not present, though she must be monitored for progression.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her BP is now 165/108 mmHg. Besides antihypertensive therapy, which medication should be started for seizure prophylaxis?
options: Magnesium sulfate
Lorazepam as initial therapy
Levetiracetam as initial therapy
Phenytoin as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Magnesium sulfate is the agent of choice for seizure prophylaxis in preeclampsia with severe features and is more effective than antiepileptics at preventing eclamptic seizures. Her severe-range blood pressure (≥160/110) and renal involvement warrant magnesium plus antihypertensive control with labetalol, hydralazine, or oral nifedipine. Phenytoin is inferior to magnesium for preventing and treating eclamptic seizures and is not recommended. Lorazepam is used to abort an active seizure but is not used for prophylaxis. Levetiracetam is a general antiepileptic without an established role in eclampsia prophylaxis.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: She is 32 weeks with severe-range blood pressures and worsening proteinuria but a reassuring fetal status. What is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Immediate cesarean delivery with appropriate follow-up
Begin an ACE inhibitor and outpatient follow-up with appropriate follow-up
Discharge home with weekly blood pressure checks with appropriate follow-up
Admit for close monitoring, BP control, magnesium,
stages.explanation: Delivery is the only definitive cure for preeclampsia, but at 32 weeks with reassuring fetal status, expectant management with inpatient monitoring, blood pressure control, magnesium, and antenatal corticosteroids to accelerate fetal lung maturity—aiming to reach about 34 weeks—balances maternal and fetal risk. Immediate cesarean is reserved for uncontrollable hypertension, eclampsia, HELLP, or non-reassuring fetal status, none present here. Discharge home is unsafe with severe-range pressures and end-organ involvement. ACE inhibitors are contraindicated in pregnancy because of fetal renal and developmental toxicity.
stages.answer_text: Admit for close monitoring, BP control, magnesium, and antenatal corticosteroids with planned delivery at 34 weeks unless she deteriorates

cases.diagnosis: Preeclampsia with severe features and renal involvement at 32 weeks gestation
pearls: Preeclampsia = new hypertension after 20 weeks plus proteinuria OR end-organ dysfunction (e.g., rising creatinine, thrombocytopenia, transaminitis).
Magnesium sulfate—not antiepileptics—is first-line for eclampsia seizure prophylaxis and treatment.
Delivery is the only cure; at <34 weeks with stable mother and fetus, give corticosteroids and manage expectantly while controlling BP.
ACE inhibitors and ARBs are contraindicated in pregnancy due to fetal toxicity; use labetalol, hydralazine, or nifedipine.

cases.title: Confusion, Excessive Thirst, and Frequent Urination
cases.chiefComplaint: Two weeks of confusion, constipation, excessive thirst, and frequent urination.
cases.history: A 66-year-old woman with a 45-pack-year smoking history is admitted with two weeks of progressive confusion, profound fatigue, constipation, and polyuria with polydipsia. Her family reports she has lost 9 kg over three months, has a chronic cough, and was told at a recent visit she had a 'spot on her lung.' She has had no hemoptysis lately but had one episode last month. Medications are hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily for hypertension and a calcium-vitamin D supplement for osteoporosis. She has no drug allergies. She drinks socially and lives alone. Family history includes a brother with prostate cancer. On review of systems she notes bone aches and nausea but no fevers, headache, or focal weakness. She has not seen a clinician about the lung finding and has had no recent imaging. Her appetite is poor. She denies diarrhea, melena, and prior kidney stones. She has had no recent immobilization or new medications other than those listed.
cases.exam: Lethargic but arousable, oriented to person and place only. Mucous membranes are dry and skin turgor is reduced. There is mild right supraclavicular fullness; lung exam reveals decreased breath sounds and dullness at the right base. Cardiac exam is normal. Abdomen is soft with hypoactive bowel sounds and mild diffuse tenderness without rebound. There is no focal neurologic deficit, no nuchal rigidity, and no asterixis. No lymphadenopathy elsewhere, no clubbing noted, and no peripheral edema. Deep tendon reflexes are diminished symmetrically.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which laboratory abnormality best explains her constellation of confusion, polyuria, polydipsia, and constipation?
options: Hyponatremia in this clinical context
Hypoglycemia in this clinical context
Hypercalcemia
Hyperkalemia in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Hypercalcemia produces the classic 'stones, bones, groans, thrones, and psychiatric overtones'—here confusion, bone pain, abdominal/GI symptoms with constipation, and polyuria with polydipsia from nephrogenic diabetes insipidus. In a heavy smoker with weight loss and a lung mass, hypercalcemia of malignancy is highly likely. Hyponatremia can cause confusion but not polyuria with constipation and bone pain. Hypoglycemia causes acute neuroglycopenia but not the chronic polyuria/constipation picture. Hyperkalemia primarily causes weakness and cardiac conduction abnormalities, not this neuro-GI-renal constellation.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Calcium 14.6 mg/dL (corrected)
Albumin 3.6 g/dL
Ionized calcium elevated
Phosphorus 2.2 mg/dL
Creatinine 2.1 mg/dL (baseline 0.9)
BUN 36 mg/dL
Intact PTH <5 pg/mL (low)
PTHrP markedly elevated
25-OH vitamin D normal; 1,25-OH vitamin D low-normal
TSH normal
CXR: 5-cm right hilar mass with right pleural effusion

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The suppressed PTH with markedly elevated PTHrP and a lung mass point to which mechanism of hypercalcemia?
options: Humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy from PTHrP
Milk-alkali syndrome as the primary diagnosis
Primary hyperparathyroidism
Vitamin D intoxication in this clinical context
stages.explanation: An appropriately suppressed PTH (the parathyroids correctly shut down in the face of high calcium) together with markedly elevated PTHrP and a lung mass indicates humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy—classically from squamous cell carcinoma of the lung secreting PTHrP. Primary hyperparathyroidism would show an inappropriately normal or elevated PTH, not a suppressed one. Vitamin D intoxication would elevate 25-OH vitamin D and is excluded by the normal level. Milk-alkali syndrome arises from large calcium plus alkali intake and would not produce a high PTHrP or this oncologic phenotype.
stages.answer_text: Humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy from PTHrP

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most important first step in managing her hypercalcemia and AKI?
options: Oral phosphate replacement as initial therapy
Hemodialysis as initial therapy
Loop diuretic (furosemide) as monotherapy
Aggressive isotonic saline volume resuscitation
stages.explanation: Volume expansion with isotonic saline is the immediate priority—these patients are profoundly volume-depleted from calcium-induced nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, and saline restores renal perfusion and promotes calciuresis. Loop diuretics are NOT first-line and should not be used as monotherapy or routinely; they are reserved for managing volume overload after rehydration and can worsen dehydration if given upfront. Oral phosphate does little acutely and risks metastatic calcification. Hemodialysis is reserved for severe hypercalcemia with renal failure or symptoms refractory to medical therapy, not the initial maneuver here.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After saline resuscitation her calcium remains 13.8 mg/dL. Which agent provides the most durable reduction in calcium for this malignancy-related hypercalcemia?
options: Calcium gluconate in this setting
An IV bisphosphonate
Thiazide diuretic in this setting
Oral calcitriol in this setting
stages.explanation: IV bisphosphonates such as zoledronic acid inhibit osteoclast-mediated bone resorption and provide durable calcium lowering over days, making them the mainstay for malignancy-associated hypercalcemia after fluids; calcitonin can be added for rapid but short-lived effect. Calcitriol would RAISE calcium by increasing intestinal absorption—exactly the wrong direction. Thiazide diuretics decrease urinary calcium excretion and worsen hypercalcemia, so they are contraindicated (her home HCTZ should be stopped). Calcium gluconate is used to TREAT hypocalcemia and would dangerously worsen her condition.

cases.diagnosis: Humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy (PTHrP-secreting squamous cell lung carcinoma) with AKI
pearls: Hypercalcemia of malignancy with suppressed PTH and elevated PTHrP is classic for squamous cell carcinoma (lung, head/neck).
Symptoms: stones, bones, abdominal groans, psychiatric overtones, plus polyuria/polydipsia from nephrogenic DI.
Treat with aggressive IV saline first; add IV bisphosphonate (and calcitonin for rapid effect) for durable control.
Stop thiazides and calcium/vitamin D supplements; avoid upfront loop diuretics, which worsen volume depletion.

cases.title: Itching and Bone Pain in a Dialysis Patient
cases.chiefComplaint: Persistent itching, bone aches, and abnormal labs in a hemodialysis patient.
cases.history: A 58-year-old man with end-stage renal disease from diabetic nephropathy, on thrice-weekly hemodialysis for two years, presents to nephrology clinic with worsening generalized pruritus, diffuse bone and joint aches, and proximal muscle weakness. He admits he frequently forgets to take his binders with meals and 'eats whatever he wants,' including processed foods and dairy. His medications are calcium acetate 667 mg with meals, cinacalcet 30 mg daily, cholecalciferol, insulin glargine, and aspirin. He has no drug allergies. He is a former smoker, does not drink, and lives with his wife. Family history includes diabetes. He denies fractures but reports a recent fall. On review of systems he notes red, gritty eyes intermittently and skin nodules. He has been adherent with dialysis sessions but admits poor dietary phosphate restriction. He has had no fevers and his appetite is good. He has had no recent hospitalizations and his last access infection was over a year ago.
cases.exam: Well-nourished man with excoriations across the back and forearms. Conjunctival injection is present (band keratopathy/red eye). There is a functioning left arm arteriovenous fistula with a palpable thrill and audible bruit. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. Abdomen is soft and non-tender. Musculoskeletal exam reveals proximal muscle weakness in the hip flexors and mild bony tenderness over the ribs and pelvis; no acute deformity. There are a few firm subcutaneous nodules over the shoulders. No peripheral edema and no asterixis. Neurologic exam is otherwise non-focal.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Calcium 9.8 mg/dL
Phosphorus 7.8 mg/dL
Calcium-phosphate product ~76 mg²/dL²
Intact PTH 720 pg/mL
25-OH vitamin D 22 ng/mL
Bicarbonate 22 mEq/L
Alkaline phosphatase 210 U/L
Hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL
Kt/V adequate
Hand X-ray: subperiosteal bone resorption of the phalanges
Shoulder X-ray: soft-tissue calcified nodule

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: His elevated PTH, high phosphorus, and subperiosteal resorption are most consistent with which form of renal osteodystrophy?
options: Osteoporosis as the primary diagnosis
Osteomalacia as the primary diagnosis
Adynamic bone disease as the primary diagnosis
Secondary hyperparathyroidism with high-turnover bone disease
stages.explanation: High PTH (720), hyperphosphatemia, elevated alkaline phosphatase, and subperiosteal resorption on hand films define high-turnover bone disease from secondary hyperparathyroidism (osteitis fibrosa cystica). Phosphate retention, low calcitriol, and hypocalcemia in CKD drive PTH up. Adynamic bone disease is LOW-turnover with LOW PTH and is excluded by his markedly elevated PTH. Osteomalacia (defective mineralization) is associated with low/normal turnover and aluminum or severe vitamin D deficiency, not this florid hyperparathyroid picture. Osteoporosis is reduced bone density without the biochemical and radiographic hyperparathyroid signature seen here.
stages.answer_text: Secondary hyperparathyroidism with high-turnover bone disease (osteitis fibrosa cystica)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given his phosphorus of 7.8 mg/dL with a calcium of 9.8 mg/dL and soft-tissue calcification, which phosphate binder is most appropriate to add or switch to?
options: Aluminum hydroxide as long-term therapy as monotherapy
Increase calcium acetate substantially as initial therapy
Stop all binders and intensify dietary restriction alone
A non-calcium-based binder such as sevelamer or lanthanum
stages.explanation: With an elevated calcium-phosphate product and evidence of vascular/soft-tissue calcification, a non-calcium-based binder (sevelamer or lanthanum) is preferred to control phosphate without adding calcium load that promotes calcification. Substantially increasing calcium acetate raises calcium intake and the calcium-phosphate product, worsening calcification risk. Aluminum hydroxide effectively binds phosphate but causes aluminum toxicity (osteomalacia, encephalopathy) and is only acceptable short-term. Dietary restriction alone is insufficient given his marked hyperphosphatemia, though counseling and binder adherence with meals are essential adjuncts.
stages.answer_text: A non-calcium-based binder such as sevelamer or lanthanum

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: His PTH remains markedly elevated despite phosphate control. Which additional therapy directly lowers PTH while helping avoid hypercalcemia?
options: Loop diuretic as the immediate next step
Teriparatide as the immediate next step
High-dose calcium carbonate in this setting
The calcimimetic cinacalcet, titrated upward
stages.explanation: Cinacalcet is a calcimimetic that increases the sensitivity of the parathyroid calcium-sensing receptor, lowering PTH while tending to lower (not raise) serum calcium—ideal when calcium and the calcium-phosphate product are already high. Up-titrating his existing cinacalcet is appropriate; active vitamin D analogs also lower PTH but can raise calcium and phosphate. High-dose calcium carbonate increases calcium load and calcification risk and does not target the parathyroid set point. Teriparatide is a PTH analog used in osteoporosis and would worsen hyperparathyroidism. Loop diuretics have no role in lowering PTH.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which intervention is most important for long-term control of his hyperphosphatemia and bone disease?
options: Switching to peritoneal dialysis with appropriate follow-up
Immediate parathyroidectomy with appropriate follow-up
Discontinuing dialysis between visits based on current risk
Counseling on dietary phosphate restriction
stages.explanation: The single most impactful, lowest-risk step is improving adherence: taking phosphate binders with every meal and restricting dietary phosphate (processed foods, dairy, colas). His labs reflect nonadherence, so reinforcing these behaviors is foundational before escalating. Switching to peritoneal dialysis does not inherently solve phosphate control and is not indicated by his symptoms. Parathyroidectomy is reserved for severe, refractory secondary/tertiary hyperparathyroidism after medical therapy fails—premature here. Reducing or skipping dialysis would worsen phosphate retention and uremia and is clearly harmful.
stages.answer_text: Counseling on dietary phosphate restriction and binder adherence with every meal

cases.diagnosis: Hyperphosphatemia with secondary hyperparathyroidism and high-turnover renal osteodystrophy in a dialysis-dependent patient
pearls: CKD drives phosphate retention → low calcitriol and hypocalcemia → secondary hyperparathyroidism (high-turnover osteitis fibrosa cystica with subperiosteal resorption).
Use non-calcium binders (sevelamer, lanthanum) when calcium or the calcium-phosphate product is high to limit vascular calcification.
Cinacalcet lowers PTH and serum calcium; active vitamin D lowers PTH but can raise calcium/phosphate.
Binder adherence with every meal plus dietary phosphate restriction is the foundation; reserve parathyroidectomy for refractory disease.

cases.title: Weakness and Falling Phosphorus After Hospital Admission
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive weakness and confusion days after starting nutrition for malnutrition.
cases.history: A 38-year-old man with a long history of alcohol use disorder is admitted after being found malnourished and disheveled; he reports he has eaten almost nothing for three weeks. He is cachectic with temporal wasting. On hospital day 1 he was started on a high-calorie enteral feeding regimen plus IV dextrose, along with thiamine and a multivitamin. Over the next 48-72 hours he develops worsening generalized weakness, muscle aches, paresthesias, and mild confusion. His only medications before admission were occasional ibuprofen; he has no drug allergies. He drinks roughly a pint of liquor daily and smokes. He lives alone and is unemployed. Family history is noncontributory. On review of systems he reports palpitations and difficulty breathing with exertion that began after feeding started. He denies chest pain, fever, and diarrhea. He has had no seizures. His baseline labs on admission, before feeding, were near-normal aside from low-normal electrolytes and a mildly elevated MCV.
cases.exam: Cachectic man appearing weak and mildly confused. He has diffuse proximal muscle weakness and reduced grip strength. Cardiopulmonary exam shows tachycardia with a regular rhythm and shallow, slightly labored breathing; lungs are clear. Abdomen is scaphoid and non-tender without organomegaly. Neurologic exam reveals symmetric weakness, intact sensation aside from distal paresthesias, and no asterixis; cranial nerves are intact and there is no nystagmus or ophthalmoplegia. There is no peripheral edema. Reflexes are diminished symmetrically.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the timing relative to refeeding, which electrolyte derangement is the most likely and most dangerous cause of his deterioration?
options: Hyperkalemia in this clinical context
Hypophosphatemia
Hypercalcemia in this clinical context
Hypernatremia in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Refeeding syndrome classically causes profound hypophosphatemia: as carbohydrate intake spikes insulin, phosphate (along with potassium and magnesium) shifts intracellularly and is consumed in ATP synthesis, dropping serum levels and causing weakness, rhabdomyolysis, respiratory failure, arrhythmias, and confusion. Hypernatremia does not fit a refeeding mechanism or his presentation. Hypercalcemia is not a feature of refeeding and would cause different symptoms. Hyperkalemia is the opposite of the expected hypoKALEMIA in refeeding; potassium also shifts intracellularly. Phosphate depletion is the hallmark and the most life-threatening here.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Phosphorus 1.0 mg/dL
Potassium 3.0 mEq/L
Magnesium 1.2 mg/dL
Calcium 8.6 mg/dL
Glucose 142 mg/dL
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
CK 1,150 U/L
Thiamine: repleted on admission
ECG: sinus tachycardia, normal QT
ABG: mild respiratory compromise

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the underlying pathophysiology driving his hypophosphatemia?
options: Decreased intestinal phosphate absorption from binders in this clinical context
Hyperparathyroidism causing phosphaturia as the primary diagnosis
Insulin-driven intracellular shift of phosphate after carbohydrate refeeding
Renal phosphate wasting from a tubular defect as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: After prolonged starvation, total-body phosphate is depleted despite near-normal serum levels; reintroducing carbohydrates surges insulin, which drives phosphate, potassium, and magnesium into cells and accelerates ATP/2,3-DPG synthesis, abruptly dropping serum phosphate—the core mechanism of refeeding syndrome. A primary renal tubular wasting defect is not the cause; his kidneys are normal. He is not on phosphate binders, so reduced absorption is not the mechanism. Hyperparathyroidism causes phosphaturia but does not explain the tight temporal link to carbohydrate refeeding or the concurrent hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia.
stages.answer_text: Insulin-driven intracellular shift of phosphate after carbohydrate refeeding

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: With phosphorus 1.0 mg/dL, symptoms, and a CK of 1,150, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Continue advancing feeds at full rate and recheck in 24 hours as monotherapy
Give IV phosphate repletion, also correct potassium
Switch entirely to parenteral nutrition at higher calories as initial therapy
Administer IV calcium gluconate only as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Severe symptomatic hypophosphatemia requires IV phosphate repletion, with simultaneous correction of the accompanying hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia, and temporarily REDUCING caloric/carbohydrate delivery to slow the intracellular shifts—then advancing feeds cautiously. Continuing full-rate feeds worsens the shift and can be fatal. Escalating to higher-calorie parenteral nutrition would intensify refeeding and is exactly wrong. IV calcium alone does not address the phosphate, potassium, or magnesium deficits and ignores the primary problem; magnesium repletion is also needed because hypomagnesemia perpetuates refractory hypokalemia and hypocalcemia.
stages.answer_text: Give IV phosphate repletion, also correct potassium and magnesium, and reduce caloric delivery

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: How could this complication have been prevented during his initial refeeding?
options: Giving high-dose dextrose boluses to restore energy quickly with appropriate follow-up
Starting feeds at full caloric goal immediately with appropriate follow-up
Starting with low caloric intake, advancing slowly,
Withholding all nutrition for the first week with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Prevention of refeeding syndrome involves identifying at-risk patients (chronic alcohol use, prolonged starvation, cachexia), starting nutrition at low caloric levels and advancing gradually over days, giving thiamine before carbohydrates, and closely monitoring and pre-emptively repleting phosphate, potassium, and magnesium. Starting at full goal immediately precipitates the dangerous shifts seen here. Withholding all nutrition for a week causes further harm and is not the strategy—cautious, monitored feeding is. High-dose dextrose boluses maximize the insulin surge and would worsen the intracellular electrolyte shifts.
stages.answer_text: Starting with low caloric intake, advancing slowly, and proactively monitoring and repleting phosphate, potassium, and magnesium

cases.diagnosis: Refeeding syndrome with profound hypophosphatemia in a malnourished patient with alcohol use disorder
pearls: Refeeding syndrome: carbohydrate load → insulin surge → intracellular shift of phosphate, potassium, and magnesium, with phosphate depletion the hallmark danger.
Severe hypophosphatemia causes weakness, rhabdomyolysis, respiratory failure, arrhythmias, and altered mentation.
Treat with IV phosphate plus correction of K and Mg, and slow the feeds; give thiamine before carbohydrates.
Prevent by starting low-calorie feeding, advancing slowly, and proactively monitoring/repleting electrolytes in high-risk patients.

cases.title: Cramps and Tingling That Won't Resolve
cases.chiefComplaint: Muscle cramps, tingling, and fatigue with potassium that won't stay corrected.
cases.history: A 55-year-old woman is admitted with several weeks of muscle cramps, perioral and finger tingling, palpitations, and fatigue. She has gastroesophageal reflux disease treated with omeprazole 40 mg daily for the past four years, hypertension on lisinopril, and osteopenia. She also takes occasional loperamide for loose stools. She has no drug allergies. On the ward, her hypokalemia (K 2.9) was repleted with potassium chloride, but repeat levels remained persistently low, and she also has a low calcium that has not responded to calcium supplementation. She does not drink alcohol, does not smoke, and works as an accountant. Family history is unremarkable. On review of systems she reports no vomiting or significant diarrhea currently, no diuretic use, and no licorice ingestion. She denies polyuria and recent illness.
