Her symptoms have worsened over the last month. She has had no surgeries and her diet is unremarkable. She has had intermittent loose stools but no large-volume diarrhea.
cases.exam: Alert, mildly anxious woman. A Chvostek sign is present (facial twitch on tapping the facial nerve) and a Trousseau sign is elicited with blood pressure cuff inflation. There is mild carpopedal spasm. Cardiopulmonary exam reveals occasional ectopic beats but is otherwise normal; lungs clear. Abdomen is soft and non-tender with normal bowel sounds. Neurologic exam shows intact strength with hyperreflexia and distal paresthesias; no focal deficit. There is no goiter and no neck surgical scar. No edema. Skin and mucous membranes are normal.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Potassium 2.9 mEq/L (refractory to KCl)
Magnesium 0.9 mg/dL
Calcium 7.2 mg/dL
Ionized calcium low
Phosphorus 3.4 mg/dL
Intact PTH 12 pg/mL (inappropriately low-normal)
Bicarbonate 26 mEq/L
Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL
Urine magnesium: low (renal conservation appropriate)
ECG: prolonged QT interval, occasional PVCs

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the unifying cause of her refractory hypokalemia and hypocalcemia?
options: Primary hypoparathyroidism
Hypomagnesemia
Bartter syndrome
Vitamin D deficiency
stages.explanation: Severe hypomagnesemia (0.9 mg/dL) is the unifying cause: low magnesium impairs PTH secretion AND induces end-organ PTH resistance (causing hypocalcemia with an inappropriately low/normal PTH), and it promotes renal potassium wasting via ROMK channels, making hypokalemia refractory until magnesium is corrected. Primary hypoparathyroidism would not explain the hypokalemia or the magnesium-driven picture. Vitamin D deficiency causes hypocalcemia but with elevated (secondary) PTH and does not cause refractory hypokalemia. Bartter syndrome causes hypokalemia and metabolic alkalosis from a tubular defect but would not typically present this way with profound symptomatic hypomagnesemia and hypocalcemia linked to chronic PPI use.

stages.label: ETIOLOGY
stages.question: Which of her medications is the most likely cause of the magnesium derangement?
options: Calcium carbonate in this setting
Acetaminophen in this setting
Lisinopril in this setting
Long-term proton pump inhibitor
stages.explanation: Long-term PPI use (here omeprazole for four years) is a well-described cause of hypomagnesemia, thought to result from impaired intestinal magnesium absorption via TRPM6/7 channels; the low urinary magnesium here confirms appropriate renal conservation, pointing to a GI/absorptive rather than renal-wasting cause. Lisinopril does not cause hypomagnesemia (ACE inhibitors tend to retain potassium, if anything). Calcium carbonate does not cause magnesium wasting. Acetaminophen has no effect on magnesium. Recognizing PPI-associated hypomagnesemia is high-yield because it can be profound and refractory until the PPI is reduced or stopped.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is symptomatic with a prolonged QT, K 2.9, Mg 0.9, and Ca 7.2. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Replete magnesium first, then continue potassium
Give IV calcium gluconate alone as initial therapy
Start a thiazide diuretic as initial therapy
Continue aggressive potassium repletion alone as monotherapy
stages.explanation: Magnesium must be repleted first—and typically intravenously when symptomatic with QT prolongation—because the hypokalemia and hypocalcemia will not correct until magnesium is restored; magnesium repletion removes the renal potassium leak and allows PTH/calcium normalization. Continuing potassium alone fails because urinary potassium wasting persists with low magnesium. IV calcium alone may transiently help symptoms but will not durably correct the calcium while magnesium remains low. A thiazide diuretic would worsen potassium loss and is contraindicated here. Magnesium-first is the key principle for refractory hypokalemia/hypocalcemia.
stages.answer_text: Replete magnesium (IV) first, then continue potassium and calcium repletion

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: To prevent recurrence after discharge, what is the most appropriate long-term recommendation?
options: Continue the same-dose PPI indefinitely with oral magnesium based on current risk
Start a potassium-sparing diuretic permanently with appropriate follow-up
Discontinue or reduce the PPI and monitor magnesium
Begin chronic IV magnesium infusions at home with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Because the PPI is the root cause, the durable fix is to discontinue or minimize it—using the lowest effective dose, switching to an H2 blocker like famotidine, or addressing reflux with lifestyle measures—while monitoring magnesium; oral magnesium alone often fails if the PPI continues, since absorption remains impaired. Continuing the same PPI dose perpetuates the problem. A potassium-sparing diuretic does not address the magnesium absorptive defect and adds risk. Chronic home IV magnesium is impractical and unnecessary if the offending drug is stopped; reserve it only for refractory cases where the PPI truly cannot be discontinued.
stages.answer_text: Discontinue or reduce the PPI (use lowest effective dose or switch to an H2 blocker) and monitor magnesium

cases.diagnosis: PPI-associated hypomagnesemia causing refractory hypokalemia and hypocalcemia
pearls: Hypomagnesemia causes refractory hypokalemia (renal K wasting via ROMK) and hypocalcemia (impaired PTH secretion and resistance) with an inappropriately low/normal PTH.
Always check and replete magnesium first when potassium or calcium won't correct.
Long-term PPI use is a key cause of hypomagnesemia; low urine magnesium points to a GI/absorptive source.
Definitive management is reducing or stopping the PPI; oral magnesium alone often fails while the PPI continues.

cases.title: Unable to Urinate with Lower Abdominal Fullness
cases.chiefComplaint: Inability to pass urine for 18 hours with lower abdominal discomfort.
cases.history: A 76-year-old man presents to the emergency department unable to urinate for the past 18 hours, with progressive lower abdominal fullness and discomfort. For the past two years he has had worsening urinary hesitancy, weak stream, nocturia three to four times nightly, and a sensation of incomplete emptying. Three days ago he started an over-the-counter cold remedy containing pseudoephedrine and diphenhydramine for congestion. His medications include amlodipine for hypertension; no chronic anticholinergics otherwise. He has no drug allergies. He drinks occasionally, is a former smoker, and lives with his wife. Family history includes a father with prostate problems. On review of systems he denies fever, dysuria, hematuria, flank pain, and prior catheterization. He has not had any back pain or saddle anesthesia and his bowel function is normal. He has had no weight loss. He notes his symptoms acutely worsened after starting the cold medication. He has never had a prior episode of complete urinary retention.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable man shifting on the stretcher. Abdomen reveals a distended, dull, tender suprapubic mass (palpable bladder) extending toward the umbilicus; no rebound or guarding elsewhere. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. Digital rectal exam shows a symmetrically enlarged, smooth, firm, non-tender prostate without nodules; normal sphincter tone and no saddle anesthesia. Genitalia are normal without phimosis or meatal stenosis. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness. Lower-extremity strength and sensation are intact, and reflexes are normal. No peripheral edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?
options: Emergent dialysis as the first step today
CT abdomen and pelvis before any intervention
Start an alpha-blocker and discharge as the first step today
Immediate bladder decompression with urethral catheterization
stages.explanation: Acute urinary retention with a palpable, painful bladder demands immediate bladder decompression via urethral (Foley) catheterization—this relieves pain, drains the obstruction, and allows measurement of the residual volume; it is both diagnostic and therapeutic. Obtaining a CT first delays relief of a readily treatable obstruction and is unnecessary when the bedside diagnosis is clear. Starting an alpha-blocker and discharging without draining the bladder leaves the obstruction unrelieved and risks ongoing renal injury. Emergent dialysis is not indicated for post-renal AKI that will resolve with simple catheter decompression unless there is a life-threatening metabolic emergency.
stages.answer_text: Immediate bladder decompression with urethral catheterization

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Catheter placed: 1,400 mL of urine drained immediately
Creatinine 2.6 mg/dL (baseline 1.0)
BUN 42 mg/dL
Potassium 4.8 mEq/L
Urinalysis: bland, no infection
Renal ultrasound: bilateral moderate hydronephrosis, distended bladder
PSA: mildly elevated
Post-void residual prior to catheter: markedly elevated

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The elevated creatinine with bilateral hydronephrosis and a distended bladder represents which category of acute kidney injury?
options: Intrinsic (acute tubular necrosis)
Post-renal (obstructive) AKI
Glomerulonephritis
Prerenal azotemia
stages.explanation: Bilateral hydronephrosis with a markedly distended bladder and an elevated creatinine that follows bladder outlet obstruction is classic post-renal (obstructive) AKI; relief of the obstruction typically reverses the renal injury. Prerenal azotemia results from hypoperfusion and would not produce hydronephrosis or a distended obstructed bladder. Acute tubular necrosis is intrinsic injury (e.g., ischemia/toxins) with muddy-brown casts, not mechanical hydronephrosis. Glomerulonephritis produces an active urine sediment (dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts, proteinuria) and does not cause bilateral hydronephrosis—his urinalysis is bland.

stages.label: ETIOLOGY
stages.question: What most likely precipitated his acute retention on top of chronic obstructive symptoms?
options: A urinary tract infection in this setting
The pseudoephedrine/diphenhydramine cold remedy
Dehydration as the immediate next step
Amlodipine as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Over-the-counter cold remedies combining a sympathomimetic (pseudoephedrine, an alpha-agonist that increases bladder neck/prostatic smooth-muscle tone) with an anticholinergic antihistamine (diphenhydramine, which impairs detrusor contraction) frequently precipitate acute urinary retention in men with underlying BPH—exactly his timeline. Amlodipine, a calcium channel blocker, is not a typical precipitant of retention. Dehydration would reduce urine output but not cause an obstructed, overdistended bladder with hydronephrosis. A UTI can precipitate retention, but his urinalysis is bland without evidence of infection, making the medication the clear trigger.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After decompression and creatinine improvement, what is the most appropriate medical therapy to facilitate a future voiding trial and reduce recurrence?
options: Long-term indwelling catheter without further therapy
An alpha-blocker such as tamsulosin
A loop diuretic as initial therapy
An anticholinergic such as oxybutynin
stages.explanation: An alpha-1 blocker such as tamsulosin relaxes prostatic and bladder-neck smooth muscle, improves urine flow, and increases the success of a subsequent voiding trial after catheter removal in BPH-related retention; a 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor (finasteride) can be added for larger glands over time. Anticholinergics like oxybutynin reduce detrusor contractility and would worsen retention—contraindicated here. A loop diuretic does not relieve outlet obstruction. A long-term indwelling catheter without addressing the obstruction is not the goal; medical therapy plus a voiding trial, and avoidance of offending drugs, is the standard approach.

cases.diagnosis: Acute urinary retention with post-renal (obstructive) AKI from benign prostatic hyperplasia, precipitated by a sympathomimetic/anticholinergic cold remedy
pearls: Acute retention with a palpable, painful bladder requires immediate catheter decompression—diagnostic and therapeutic.
Bilateral hydronephrosis with a distended bladder and elevated creatinine = post-renal (obstructive) AKI, usually reversible after relief.
OTC cold remedies (sympathomimetic + anticholinergic) commonly precipitate retention in men with BPH.
Alpha-blockers (tamsulosin) improve flow and voiding-trial success; avoid anticholinergics, which worsen retention.

cases.title: Massive Urine Output After a Catheter
cases.chiefComplaint: Very large urine output and lightheadedness after relief of urinary obstruction.
cases.history: A 78-year-old man was admitted for acute urinary retention from longstanding benign prostatic hyperplasia; a urethral catheter was placed and drained 1.6 L immediately, with an initial creatinine of 3.4 mg/dL. Over the following 24 hours his urine output surged to 5-6 L per day. He now reports lightheadedness when sitting up, dry mouth, fatigue, and muscle cramps. His medications are amlodipine and a newly started alpha-blocker; he has no drug allergies. He drinks rarely, is a former smoker, and lives with his daughter. Family history is noncontributory. On review of systems he denies fever, chest pain, and dyspnea. He has had no diarrhea or vomiting. Nursing notes document hourly urine outputs of 250-350 mL. He has been drinking water but cannot keep up with his urinary losses. He has no history of diabetes mellitus or diabetes insipidus. His mentation is intact but he feels weak. He has had no recent diuretic exposure.
cases.exam: Tired-appearing elderly man. Mucous membranes are dry and skin turgor is reduced; there is orthostatic drop in pressure with reflex tachycardia. Cardiopulmonary exam reveals tachycardia, clear lungs, and no edema. Abdomen is soft and non-tender; the bladder is now decompressed and the catheter is draining clear, copious urine. Neurologic exam is non-focal with intact mentation; mild diffuse weakness is noted. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness. Jugular venous pressure is low. Extremities are warm but show signs of volume depletion.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most likely explanation for his massive urine output following catheter placement?
options: New-onset central diabetes insipidus
Osmotic diuresis from hyperglycemia
Overzealous IV fluid administration
Post-obstructive diuresis
stages.explanation: Post-obstructive diuresis is the expected polyuria after relief of bilateral (or bladder outlet) obstruction: a combination of appropriate excretion of retained urea, sodium, and water, plus a transient tubular concentrating defect and natriuresis. It can be massive and cause volume depletion with electrolyte losses, as seen here. Central diabetes insipidus is not triggered by relieving urinary obstruction and lacks the antecedent retention story. Osmotic diuresis from hyperglycemia would require markedly elevated glucose, and he is not diabetic. Overzealous IV fluids are not described—his polyuria is driven by the post-obstructive state, not iatrogenic fluid loading.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Sodium 148 mEq/L
Potassium 3.1 mEq/L
Magnesium 1.5 mg/dL
Bicarbonate 20 mEq/L
Creatinine 2.0 mg/dL (improving from 3.4)
BUN 38 mg/dL
Urine output: 5.5 L over 24 h
Urine sodium elevated; urine osmolality low
Glucose 96 mg/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given orthostatic hypotension, polyuria, and hypokalemia, what is the most appropriate management of his diuresis?
options: Administer desmopressin (DDAVP) as initial therapy
Restrict all fluids to suppress urine output as initial therapy
Start a thiazide diuretic as initial therapy
Provide judicious IV fluid replacement with electrolyte repletion
stages.explanation: Management of symptomatic post-obstructive diuresis is judicious volume replacement—typically replacing roughly half to two-thirds of urine losses with isotonic or hypotonic fluid based on serum sodium—plus repletion of potassium and magnesium, while monitoring weights, orthostatics, and electrolytes. Aggressive total fluid restriction risks worsening hypovolemia and AKI, but you also avoid matching output 1:1, which can perpetuate the diuresis. Desmopressin treats central DI, not post-obstructive diuresis. A thiazide diuretic would worsen polyuria, hypokalemia, and volume depletion and is contraindicated here.
stages.answer_text: Provide judicious IV fluid replacement (e.g., replacing a portion of urine output) with electrolyte repletion and close monitoring

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication is the greatest immediate concern if his losses are not adequately addressed?
options: Volume overload and pulmonary edema
Hyperkalemia and cardiac arrest
Hypertensive emergency as the leading concern
Hypovolemic shock with electrolyte derangements
stages.explanation: With 5-6 L/day of urinary losses, orthostatic hypotension, tachycardia, dry mucous membranes, and falling potassium, the dominant danger is hypovolemic shock accompanied by hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, and a rising sodium from free-water loss. Volume overload/pulmonary edema is the opposite problem and would only occur from inappropriate over-replacement, not under-treatment. Hypertensive emergency is not expected; he is hypotensive. Hyperkalemia is incorrect—he is actively losing potassium in the urine and is hypoKALEMIC, so the risk is the reverse. Recognizing the hypovolemic, hypokalemic trajectory drives appropriate fluid and electrolyte management.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: How should the diuresis and fluid replacement be monitored to know when to taper support?
options: Rely solely on the patient's thirst to guide intake with appropriate follow-up
Discontinue monitoring once the catheter is in place with appropriate follow-up
Serial monitoring of urine output, body weight, orthostatic vitals,
Check a single basic metabolic panel at discharge only with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Post-obstructive diuresis is usually self-limited over hours to a few days as tubular function and solute balance normalize, so management requires serial tracking of hourly urine output, daily weights, orthostatic vitals, and frequent electrolyte panels, tapering IV support as urine output declines and the patient stabilizes. Discontinuing monitoring after catheter placement misses dangerous ongoing losses. A single metabolic panel at discharge is inadequate during active diuresis. Relying on thirst alone is unsafe in an elderly patient with impaired thirst response and large obligate losses, risking under-replacement and shock.
stages.answer_text: Serial monitoring of urine output, body weight, orthostatic vitals, and electrolytes, tapering fluids as the diuresis self-limits

cases.diagnosis: Post-obstructive diuresis with hypovolemia and electrolyte losses after relief of urinary obstruction
pearls: Post-obstructive diuresis follows relief of obstruction: physiologic excretion of retained solute/water plus a transient tubular concentrating defect and natriuresis.
It can cause hypovolemia, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, and hypernatremia from free-water loss.
Replace a portion (not all) of urine losses with appropriate fluid and replete electrolytes; avoid matching output 1:1, which perpetuates diuresis.
Monitor urine output, weight, orthostatics, and electrolytes; the diuresis is usually self-limited.

cases.title: Blood in the Urine and a Lump in the Side
cases.chiefComplaint: Painless blood in the urine and a fullness in the left flank.
cases.history: A 63-year-old man presents to clinic with three episodes of painless gross hematuria over the past month and a sensation of fullness in the left flank. He has also noticed dull left-sided back discomfort and unintentional 5 kg weight loss, plus low-grade fevers and night sweats. He has a 30-pack-year smoking history and works in construction. His medications are lisinopril for hypertension; no anticoagulants. He has no drug allergies. He drinks beer on weekends. Family history is unremarkable for kidney disease. On review of systems he denies dysuria, urinary frequency, and recent trauma. He notes mild fatigue but no flank colic. He has had no prior stones or infections. He reports his face sometimes feels flushed. He has not had any chest pain or shortness of breath. His blood pressure has been harder to control recently. He has had no prior urologic evaluation and no recent instrumentation. His appetite is mildly reduced.
cases.exam: Well-appearing man with mild facial plethora. Abdominal exam reveals a palpable, firm, non-tender mass in the left flank/upper quadrant that moves with respiration; no overlying bruit. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. There is a new left-sided varicocele that does not decompress when supine. No costovertebral angle tenderness to percussion. There is no lower-extremity edema and no lymphadenopathy. Genital exam confirms the non-reducing left varicocele. Neurologic exam is non-focal. No skin lesions or jaundice are noted.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature is the most concerning red flag mandating urgent malignancy workup?
options: A reducible varicocele
Painless gross hematuria
Well-controlled blood pressure
Weekend alcohol use
stages.explanation: Painless gross hematuria in an older adult, especially a smoker, is a classic red flag for urologic malignancy (renal or urothelial) and mandates prompt imaging and cystoscopy. Combined here with a palpable flank mass and a new non-reducing left varicocele, renal cell carcinoma is strongly suspected. Weekend alcohol use is not a specific malignancy risk in this context. His blood pressure is actually elevated and harder to control, not well-controlled, and a reducing varicocele would be benign—but his varicocele does NOT decompress when supine, which is the worrisome finding suggesting venous obstruction by tumor. The painless hematuria is the key alarm symptom.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 18.2 g/dL (elevated)
Hematocrit 54%
Erythropoietin: elevated
Calcium 10.8 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL
Urinalysis: gross blood, no infection
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast: 8-cm enhancing left renal mass with areas of necrosis; tumor thrombus in the left renal vein
No distant metastases identified

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the enhancing renal mass, classic triad, and the polycythemia, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Transitional cell carcinoma of the renal pelvis
Simple renal cyst
Angiomyolipoma in this clinical context
Renal cell carcinoma
stages.explanation: An older smoker with the classic triad (flank pain, hematuria, palpable mass), a large solid enhancing renal mass with necrosis and renal vein tumor thrombus, plus paraneoplastic polycythemia from ectopic erythropoietin and hypercalcemia, is textbook renal cell carcinoma. Transitional cell carcinoma arises from urothelium (renal pelvis, ureter, bladder) and typically appears as a filling defect, not a large solid enhancing parenchymal mass with EPO-driven polycythemia. Angiomyolipoma contains macroscopic fat on imaging and is usually benign, without paraneoplastic syndromes. A simple cyst is non-enhancing, thin-walled, and fluid-density—not a necrotic enhancing solid mass.

stages.label: ETIOLOGY
stages.question: The patient's elevated hemoglobin and hematocrit are best explained by which paraneoplastic mechanism?
options: Tumor production of renin in this setting
Ectopic erythropoietin production by the tumor
Tumor production of PTHrP in this setting
Tumor production of ACTH in this setting
stages.explanation: Renal cell carcinoma can secrete erythropoietin ectopically, driving secondary polycythemia—exactly matching his elevated hemoglobin/hematocrit and high EPO level. PTHrP secretion can occur in RCC and explains his mild hypercalcemia, but it does not cause polycythemia. Ectopic ACTH (causing Cushing syndrome) is more characteristic of small cell lung cancer and would not produce erythrocytosis. Renin secretion by RCC can contribute to hypertension but does not cause polycythemia. Recognizing EPO-mediated polycythemia as an RCC paraneoplastic syndrome is high-yield.
stages.answer_text: Ectopic erythropoietin production by the tumor

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this localized 8-cm renal mass with renal vein tumor thrombus and no distant metastases, what is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
options: External beam radiation therapy alone
Active surveillance as initial therapy
Surgical resection
Systemic chemotherapy alone
stages.explanation: Surgical resection—radical nephrectomy, including management of the renal vein tumor thrombus—is the definitive treatment for localized renal cell carcinoma and offers the best chance of cure for an 8-cm mass without distant metastases. RCC is notoriously resistant to conventional cytotoxic chemotherapy, so chemotherapy alone is not standard. RCC is also relatively radioresistant, so radiation alone is not curative for the primary tumor. Active surveillance is reserved for small (<2 cm) incidental renal masses in poor surgical candidates, not for a large symptomatic tumor with venous extension. Targeted/immunotherapy is reserved for advanced/metastatic disease.

cases.diagnosis: Renal cell carcinoma with paraneoplastic polycythemia (ectopic erythropoietin) and renal vein tumor thrombus
pearls: Classic RCC triad—flank pain, hematuria, palpable mass—is present in a minority; painless hematuria in a smoker warrants urgent workup.
A new left varicocele that does not decompress when supine suggests renal vein/IVC obstruction by tumor.
RCC paraneoplastic syndromes: polycythemia (ectopic EPO), hypercalcemia (PTHrP), hypertension (renin), Stauffer syndrome (nonmetastatic hepatic dysfunction).
Radical nephrectomy is definitive for localized disease; RCC is resistant to conventional chemotherapy and radiation.

cases.title: Painless Bleeding with Every Void
cases.chiefComplaint: Painless gross blood in the urine for two weeks.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man presents to clinic with two weeks of intermittent painless gross hematuria, including a few clots. He has no flank pain, dysuria, fever, or weight loss. He has a 50-pack-year smoking history and worked for decades in a textile/dye factory with documented aniline dye exposure. His medications are metformin and atorvastatin; he takes no anticoagulants. He has no drug allergies. He drinks occasionally and is married. Family history is unremarkable. On review of systems he denies urinary frequency, urgency, or obstructive symptoms, and reports no recent trauma or instrumentation. He has not had prior kidney stones or infections. He notes the hematuria occurs throughout the urinary stream rather than only at the beginning or end. He has had no prior urologic evaluation. His appetite and energy are normal. He has had no recent vigorous exercise. He has had no prior episodes of visible blood in the urine.
cases.exam: Comfortable, well-appearing man. Abdominal exam is soft and non-tender with no palpable masses and no flank fullness. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. Digital rectal exam reveals a mildly enlarged, smooth prostate without nodules and normal sphincter tone. Genitalia are normal. There is no lower-extremity edema and no inguinal lymphadenopathy. Neurologic exam is non-focal. No suprapubic tenderness or palpable bladder is appreciated. Skin and mucous membranes appear normal without pallor.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic workup for painless gross hematuria in this high-risk patient?
options: Cystoscopy plus CT urography
Renal ultrasound alone
Repeat urinalysis in three months
Empiric antibiotics for presumed UTI
stages.explanation: Painless gross hematuria in an older smoker with occupational aniline dye exposure carries a high risk of urothelial (bladder) malignancy and requires full evaluation with cystoscopy (to directly visualize the bladder) and CT urography (to image the upper tracts and ureters); urine cytology is a useful adjunct. Empiric antibiotics are inappropriate without evidence of infection and dangerously delay cancer diagnosis. Renal ultrasound alone is insufficient—it can miss small bladder and upper-tract urothelial tumors. Deferring with a repeat urinalysis in three months delays diagnosis of a potentially curable cancer and is not acceptable for gross hematuria with red flags.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Urinalysis: gross blood, no nitrites, no leukocyte esterase
Urine cytology: atypical/malignant urothelial cells
Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL
Hemoglobin 13.8 g/dL
CT urography: irregular bladder wall mass at the trigone; upper tracts normal
Cystoscopy: 3-cm papillary, sessile mass on the lateral bladder wall
No hydronephrosis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The bladder mass with positive cytology and his risk factors are most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Adenocarcinoma of the bladder in this clinical context
Urothelial carcinoma of the bladder
Squamous cell carcinoma of the bladder
Benign prostatic hyperplasia causing hematuria
stages.explanation: Urothelial (transitional cell) carcinoma is by far the most common bladder cancer in the developed world and is strongly linked to cigarette smoking and aromatic amine/aniline dye exposure—precisely this patient's profile—presenting as painless hematuria with a papillary bladder mass and malignant urothelial cells on cytology. Squamous cell carcinoma of the bladder is associated with chronic irritation such as Schistosoma haematobium infection or long-term catheters, not aniline dye. Adenocarcinoma is rare and linked to urachal remnants or bladder exstrophy. BPH can cause hematuria but does not produce a malignant bladder mass or positive cytology.
stages.answer_text: Urothelial (transitional cell) carcinoma of the bladder

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the appropriate next step to diagnose, stage, and initially treat this bladder mass?
options: Transurethral resection of the bladder tumor with examination of the muscularis propria
Systemic chemotherapy before any biopsy as initial therapy
Intravesical BCG without tissue diagnosis as initial therapy
Partial cystectomy immediately as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Transurethral resection of the bladder tumor (TURBT) is the key next step: it provides tissue for histologic diagnosis and—critically—samples the muscularis propria to determine whether the tumor invades the detrusor muscle, which dictates staging and subsequent treatment (intravesical therapy for non-muscle-invasive disease vs. radical cystectomy or chemoradiation for muscle-invasive). Partial cystectomy is not the standard initial procedure for most urothelial tumors. Giving systemic chemotherapy before any tissue diagnosis is inappropriate—you must confirm and stage first. Intravesical BCG is used after TURBT for high-risk non-muscle-invasive disease, but only once tissue diagnosis and staging are established.
stages.answer_text: Transurethral resection of the bladder tumor (TURBT) with examination of the muscularis propria

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: TURBT shows high-grade carcinoma confined to the lamina propria without muscle invasion (non-muscle-invasive). What is the most appropriate adjuvant therapy?
options: Observation alone with appropriate follow-up
Systemic cisplatin-based chemotherapy based on current risk
Intravesical BCG immunotherapy with surveillance cystoscopy
Radical cystectomy with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: For high-grade non-muscle-invasive urothelial carcinoma, intravesical BCG immunotherapy after complete TURBT reduces recurrence and progression and is standard, combined with surveillance cystoscopy because these tumors frequently recur. Radical cystectomy is reserved for muscle-invasive disease or BCG-refractory high-risk tumors, not first-line for this stage. Systemic cisplatin-based chemotherapy is used (often neoadjuvantly) for muscle-invasive or metastatic disease, not for non-muscle-invasive tumors. Observation alone is inadequate for high-grade disease given the substantial recurrence and progression risk; close surveillance plus intravesical therapy is required.
stages.answer_text: Intravesical BCG immunotherapy with surveillance cystoscopy

cases.diagnosis: Non-muscle-invasive high-grade urothelial (transitional cell) carcinoma of the bladder
pearls: Painless gross hematuria in an older smoker (or aniline dye/aromatic amine exposure) requires cystoscopy plus CT urography—never empiric antibiotics.
Urothelial carcinoma is the most common bladder cancer; squamous cell is linked to Schistosoma/chronic irritation, adenocarcinoma to urachal remnants.
TURBT must sample muscularis propria to determine muscle invasion, which drives staging and treatment.
Non-muscle-invasive high-grade disease: TURBT + intravesical BCG + surveillance; muscle-invasive disease: radical cystectomy or chemoradiation.

cases.title: A Belly Lump Found at Bath Time
cases.chiefComplaint: Her mother noticed a firm swelling on the left side of the child's abdomen while bathing her.
cases.history: A previously healthy 3-year-old girl is brought to clinic after her mother felt a firm, smooth bulge in the left upper abdomen during a bath two days ago. The child has been eating, playing, and acting normally with no fever, vomiting, abdominal pain, or change in bowel or bladder habits. There has been no weight loss or night sweats. Urine has been normal in color and the parents deny any gross blood. Birth and developmental history are unremarkable; she was full-term with a normal newborn screen. Immunizations are up to date. She takes no medications and has no allergies. She attends daycare three days a week and lives with both parents and an older brother. Family history is notable for a maternal aunt with breast cancer; there is no family history of childhood kidney tumors or known cancer-predisposition syndromes. On review of systems, the parents report no aniridia, no limb asymmetry, and no genitourinary abnormalities noted at prior well-child visits.
cases.exam: The child is well-appearing, interactive, and in no distress. Abdomen is soft and nontender with a large, firm, smooth, nontender mass palpable in the left flank that does not cross the midline. There is no hepatosplenomegaly and no other palpable masses. No abdominal bruit is heard. There is no aniridia; irides are normal bilaterally. There is no hemihypertrophy or limb-length discrepancy. External genitalia are normal for age. No lymphadenopathy is appreciated. Skin shows no café-au-lait macules or hemangiomas. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first imaging study to evaluate this flank mass?
options: Plain abdominal radiograph
CT abdomen with IV contrast
Whole-body MRI
Abdominal ultrasound with Doppler
stages.explanation: Abdominal ultrasound with Doppler is the correct first step: it is radiation-free, readily available, and ideal in young children to localize the mass to the kidney, characterize it as solid vs cystic, and screen for tumor thrombus in the renal vein/IVC. CT with contrast is an important second step for staging and chest evaluation but exposes the child to radiation and is not the initial test. Whole-body MRI is not a standard first-line study and often requires sedation. A plain radiograph cannot characterize a solid organ mass and would not reliably localize it to the kidney, so it is inadequate here.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Renal ultrasound: 9.2 cm solid, heterogeneous mass arising from the lower pole of the left kidney with a 'claw sign' of compressed normal parenchyma
No cystic/septated features to suggest a simple cyst
Left renal vein patent; no tumor thrombus identified
Contralateral right kidney normal
CT chest/abdomen with contrast: confirms intrarenal left mass; no pulmonary nodules; no lymphadenopathy; liver normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given a solid intrarenal mass with a claw sign in a 3-year-old, which is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Neuroblastoma in this clinical context
Mesoblastic nephroma
Wilms tumor
Renal cell carcinoma
stages.explanation: Wilms tumor (nephroblastoma) is the most common renal malignancy of childhood, peaking at ages 2-5 years, and classically presents as an asymptomatic, smooth abdominal mass that does not cross the midline, with an intrarenal 'claw sign.' Neuroblastoma is the chief mimic but arises from adrenal/sympathetic tissue, is extrarenal, characteristically crosses the midline and encases vessels, and often presents with systemic symptoms and elevated urine catecholamines. Mesoblastic nephroma is a renal tumor but occurs in infants under 6 months, not a 3-year-old. Renal cell carcinoma is rare in young children and is far more common in adolescents and adults.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this child with a unilateral, resectable renal mass and a normal contralateral kidney, what is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Upfront whole-abdomen radiation therapy as initial therapy
Percutaneous core needle biopsy of the mass before any therapy
Observation with repeat ultrasound in 3 months as monotherapy
Radical nephrectomy with surgical staging
stages.explanation: In the North American (COG) approach, a unilateral, resectable Wilms tumor with a normal contralateral kidney is managed with upfront radical nephrectomy plus surgical staging and lymph node sampling, followed by risk-adapted chemotherapy (and radiation if indicated). Percutaneous biopsy is generally avoided because it upstages the tumor by violating the capsule and risks seeding. Upfront radiation is not used as the initial intervention; radiation is reserved for higher-stage/unfavorable-histology disease after surgery. Observation is inappropriate for a 9 cm malignant mass and risks progression and tumor rupture, which would spill tumor and upstage the disease. (Note that the European/SIOP protocol instead gives neoadjuvant chemotherapy before nephrectomy, but in North America upfront surgery with intraoperative staging guides therapy and avoids pre-treatment biopsy.)

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Before surgery, which examination finding would most specifically raise concern for an associated syndrome (e.g., WAGR) in this patient?
options: A grade I systolic ejection murmur
Mild tachycardia
Aniridia
A single café-au-lait macule
stages.explanation: Aniridia is part of WAGR syndrome (Wilms tumor, Aniridia, Genitourinary anomalies, intellectual disability/Range of developmental delay), caused by an 11p13 deletion involving WT1 and PAX6. Identifying aniridia should prompt genetic evaluation and heightened surveillance, since these children have markedly increased Wilms risk. A single café-au-lait macule is a common, nonspecific finding (neurofibromatosis requires ≥6) and is not part of WAGR. Mild tachycardia is expected in an anxious toddler and is nonspecific. A benign flow murmur is a common incidental finding with no association to Wilms predisposition syndromes. Other syndromic clues to look for on exam include hemihypertrophy and macroglossia (Beckwith-Wiedemann) and ambiguous genitalia with early nephrotic syndrome (Denys-Drash, also a WT1 disorder); recognizing any of these warrants genetic referral and surveillance imaging of the remaining kidney.

cases.diagnosis: Wilms tumor (nephroblastoma) of the left kidney
pearls: Wilms tumor presents as a smooth, asymptomatic abdominal mass that does NOT cross the midline in a child aged 2-5; neuroblastoma crosses the midline and is often symptomatic.
Ultrasound is the first imaging step; the intrarenal 'claw sign' distinguishes a renal-origin tumor from an extrarenal adrenal neuroblastoma.
Avoid percutaneous biopsy of a resectable unilateral Wilms tumor in North America—upfront nephrectomy with staging is preferred to avoid upstaging from capsular violation.
Screen for syndromic associations: WAGR (aniridia, GU anomalies), Beckwith-Wiedemann (hemihypertrophy, macroglossia), and Denys-Drash.

cases.title: Years of Headaches, Now Foamy Urine and Fatigue
cases.chiefComplaint: She presents with months of fatigue and was found to have abnormal kidney labs on routine testing.
cases.history: A 60-year-old woman presents to nephrology clinic after her primary doctor found a rising creatinine on routine bloodwork. She reports years of fatigue and intermittent flank discomfort. For more than 30 years she treated chronic tension and migraine headaches plus low-back pain with daily over-the-counter combination analgesics; in her 30s and 40s these contained phenacetin, and more recently she takes ibuprofen 600 mg two to three times daily plus acetaminophen. She estimates she rarely goes a day without an analgesic. She has hypertension treated with amlodipine 10 mg daily. She reports occasional episodes of dark or 'tea-colored' urine and one prior episode of acute flank pain that resolved spontaneously. No diabetes. She has mild dyspepsia. Allergies: none. Social: 20-pack-year smoking history, quit 5 years ago; minimal alcohol; works as a retired textile worker. Family history: hypertension; no polycystic kidney disease or hereditary nephritis. Review of systems is notable for nocturia and mild lower urinary tract symptoms but no fevers, rash, or joint swelling. She denies recent contrast exposure.
cases.exam: She appears well but pale. There is mild conjunctival pallor. Cardiac exam reveals a regular rhythm without murmurs; no S3 or pericardial rub. Lungs are clear. Abdomen is soft and nontender with no palpable masses, organomegaly, or bruit. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness today. Extremities show trace pedal edema bilaterally without rash, livedo, or joint effusions. Neurologic exam is nonfocal. No skin nodules or signs of vasculitis are seen.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which initial workup best characterizes the pattern of her kidney disease?
options: 24-hour urine catecholamines and plasma metanephrines as the first step today
CT angiography of the renal arteries as the first step today
Serum creatinine, urinalysis with microscopy, urine protein-to-creatinine ratio,
Immediate kidney biopsy as the first step today
stages.explanation: The correct first step is noninvasive characterization: serum creatinine to quantify GFR, urinalysis with microscopy to look for sterile pyuria/WBC casts and hematuria, a urine protein-to-creatinine ratio to gauge proteinuria, and renal ultrasound to assess size, echogenicity, and structural changes such as papillary necrosis or scarring. Kidney biopsy is invasive and rarely needed when the clinical pattern (chronic interstitial disease from analgesics) is clear; it is not the initial step. Catecholamine testing evaluates pheochromocytoma, which is not suggested here. CT angiography evaluates renovascular stenosis and would not explain her tubulointerstitial picture as a first test.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Creatinine 2.3 mg/dL (baseline ~0.9 two years ago); eGFR 24 mL/min/1.73 m²
BUN 38 mg/dL
Urinalysis: specific gravity 1.009; pH 6.0; protein 1+; no glucose; 5-10 WBC/hpf; 3-5 RBC/hpf; no nitrites; no bacteria
Urine protein-to-creatinine ratio 0.6 g/g
No dysmorphic RBCs or RBC casts; occasional renal tubular epithelial cells
Urine culture: no growth
Hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL; MCV 88 fL
Renal ultrasound: small kidneys (right 8.6 cm, left 8.9 cm) with increased cortical echogenicity, irregular bumpy contours, and papillary calcifications

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains chronic kidney disease with sterile pyuria, modest proteinuria, small scarred kidneys, and papillary calcifications in this patient?
options: ANCA-associated glomerulonephritis
Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease
Diabetic nephropathy as the primary diagnosis
Analgesic nephropathy with papillary necrosis
stages.explanation: Analgesic nephropathy is a chronic tubulointerstitial disease classically from decades of combination analgesic (historically phenacetin) and NSAID use. It produces sterile pyuria, modest sub-nephrotic proteinuria, bland sediment, anemia, small bumpy-contour kidneys, and papillary necrosis with calcifications—exactly this picture. Diabetic nephropathy is unlikely without diabetes and typically causes large kidneys and heavier proteinuria. ANCA glomerulonephritis would show an active sediment with dysmorphic RBCs/RBC casts and a rapidly progressive course, absent here. ADPKD causes enlarged kidneys with numerous macroscopic cysts on ultrasound, not small scarred kidneys. The combination of a decades-long combination-analgesic history, a bland-to-mild urinalysis, anemia out of proportion, and bilaterally small kidneys with papillary calcifications makes analgesic nephropathy the unifying diagnosis here.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: She has episodes of dark urine and prior acute flank pain. Which imaging finding would most specifically confirm renal papillary necrosis?
options: A single simple cortical cyst as the immediate next step
Bilateral hydronephrosis on ultrasound as the immediate next step
A 'ring shadow' or sloughed papilla within a calyx on CT urography
Cortical thinning alone as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Papillary necrosis is best confirmed by CT urography (or prior IV urography), which can show a sloughed papilla, the classic 'ring shadow' as contrast surrounds a detached papilla, clubbed calyces, and intraluminal filling defects; a sloughed papilla can also obstruct a ureter and cause her colicky flank pain and hematuria. Bilateral hydronephrosis indicates obstruction but is nonspecific and not diagnostic of papillary necrosis. Cortical thinning reflects chronic scarring generally and does not specifically demonstrate papillary disease. A single simple cyst is an incidental, unrelated finding. Beyond analgesics, the classic mnemonic for papillary necrosis is POSTCARDS (Pyelonephritis, Obstruction, Sickle cell, TB, Cirrhosis/chronic alcoholism, Analgesics, Renal vein thrombosis, Diabetes, Systemic vasculitis)—useful when the history is less obvious than hers.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the single most important management intervention to slow progression of her kidney disease?
options: Permanently discontinue all NSAIDs
Start high-dose corticosteroids as initial therapy
Initiate hemodialysis now as initial therapy
Begin empiric ciprofloxacin for presumed pyelonephritis
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of management is permanent cessation of the offending analgesics—NSAIDs and combination/phenacetin-containing products—since ongoing exposure drives progressive interstitial injury and papillary necrosis; alternative pain strategies and blood pressure/proteinuria control (e.g., an ACE inhibitor or ARB) should follow. Corticosteroids have no established role in analgesic nephropathy, which is a chronic fibrotic process, not an acute inflammatory one. Her pyuria is sterile with a negative culture, so antibiotics are not indicated. With an eGFR of 24, she is not yet dialysis-dependent; dialysis is premature and not the key step to slow progression.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Patients with analgesic nephropathy are at increased long-term risk for which malignancy, warranting surveillance of new hematuria?
options: Renal cell carcinoma of the cortex only
Adrenal cortical carcinoma as the leading concern
Urothelial carcinoma of the renal pelvis
Wilms tumor as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Long-standing analgesic (especially phenacetin) nephropathy carries an increased risk of urothelial (transitional cell) carcinoma of the upper tract—renal pelvis and ureter—and bladder, so new or worsening hematuria should prompt urine cytology and upper-tract imaging/cystoscopy. Renal cell carcinoma risk is somewhat increased in CKD generally but the classic, specific association with analgesic abuse is urothelial cancer. Wilms tumor is a pediatric renal malignancy unrelated to analgesic use. Adrenal cortical carcinoma has no association with analgesic nephropathy. Because the upper-tract urothelium is exposed to concentrated metabolites for decades, surveillance should include urine cytology and cross-sectional/cystoscopic evaluation when she develops new or persistent hematuria, even after she stops the offending drugs.

cases.diagnosis: Analgesic nephropathy (chronic tubulointerstitial nephritis with papillary necrosis)
pearls: Suspect analgesic nephropathy with chronic interstitial disease, sterile pyuria, bland sediment, anemia, and small bumpy kidneys after decades of NSAID/phenacetin use.
Papillary necrosis can cause hematuria and colicky flank pain from a sloughed papilla; CT urography may show a 'ring shadow' or sloughed papilla in a calyx.
The key treatment is permanent discontinuation of all NSAIDs/combination analgesics plus BP and proteinuria control with an ACEi/ARB.
There is an increased lifetime risk of upper-tract urothelial (transitional cell) carcinoma—evaluate new hematuria with cytology and imaging.

cases.title: Increasing Thirst and Buckets of Urine on a Mood Stabilizer
cases.chiefComplaint: He reports years of excessive thirst and frequent urination with a recently noted rise in creatinine.
cases.history: A 50-year-old man with bipolar I disorder, well controlled for 18 years, presents to clinic for follow-up after labs showed a creatinine increase. He describes years of intense thirst and waking three to four times nightly to urinate; he carries a water bottle everywhere and estimates drinking 5-6 liters daily. He has been on lithium carbonate (currently 900 mg/day) since his early 30s, with levels generally in the therapeutic range; he has had two episodes of acute toxicity over the years during illnesses. Other medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily for hypertension and a recently added thiazide his PCP started for 'mild swelling.' No NSAID use. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, rare alcohol, works as an accountant. Family history: father with hypertension; no kidney disease. Review of systems: no flank pain, no gross hematuria, no edema today; he notes mild tremor and stable mood. He has not had a recent lithium level checked since the thiazide was started.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented, with mild bilateral fine hand tremor. Mucous membranes are slightly dry. Cardiovascular exam is regular without murmurs; JVP is not elevated. Lungs are clear. Abdomen is soft, nontender, without masses or bruit; no costovertebral angle tenderness. No peripheral edema is present today. Skin shows no rash. Neurologic exam shows the noted tremor but no ataxia, hyperreflexia, or confusion. Thyroid is normal to palpation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His thirst and high-volume urination most likely reflect which tubular defect, and what is the best initial test to characterize it?
options: Central diabetes insipidus—start desmopressin empirically as the first step today
Primary polydipsia—restrict all fluids immediately at home as the first step today
Osmotic diuresis from hyperglycemia—check fasting glucose and HbA1c only
Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus
stages.explanation: Chronic lithium therapy classically causes nephrogenic diabetes insipidus by downregulating aquaporin-2 channels in the collecting duct, producing polyuria and compensatory polydipsia. The best initial characterization is paired serum and urine osmolality with serum sodium: you expect inappropriately dilute urine despite a normal-to-high serum osmolality/sodium. Checking only glucose/HbA1c addresses osmotic diuresis, which his history does not suggest. Telling him to abruptly restrict fluids at home is unsafe and could precipitate hypernatremia. Empiric desmopressin assumes central DI; in nephrogenic DI the kidney is resistant to ADH, so desmopressin will not concentrate the urine.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Serum sodium 144 mEq/L; potassium 4.0 mEq/L
Serum osmolality 296 mOsm/kg
Urine osmolality 180 mOsm/kg
Creatinine 1.8 mg/dL (was 1.3 two years ago); eGFR 44 mL/min/1.73 m²
Urinalysis: specific gravity 1.005; trace protein; no glucose; bland sediment
Lithium level 1.4 mEq/L (drawn today)
Desmopressin (DDAVP) trial: urine osmolality rises only to 205 mOsm/kg
Renal ultrasound/MRI: normal-sized kidneys with numerous tiny (1-3 mm) cortical and medullary microcysts bilaterally

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding most specifically points to chronic lithium nephropathy rather than another cause of CKD?
options: A bland urinalysis as the primary diagnosis
Numerous small renal microcysts on imaging with nephrogenic DI
Trace proteinuria as the primary diagnosis
Mildly elevated blood pressure as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Numerous small renal microcysts (cortical and medullary), combined with nephrogenic DI and chronic tubulointerstitial disease, is the characteristic imaging/clinical signature of chronic lithium nephropathy. Mild hypertension is extremely common and nonspecific. A bland urinalysis is consistent with tubulointerstitial disease but is seen in many conditions and does not point specifically to lithium. Trace proteinuria is likewise nonspecific. The constellation—chronic lithium exposure, an ADH-resistant urinary concentrating defect (urine osmolality that fails to rise appreciably after DDAVP), and numerous small cortical/medullary microcysts—is what distinguishes lithium nephropathy from other CKD etiologies; the microcysts can persist even after lithium is discontinued and are a useful retrospective clue.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His lithium level rose after a thiazide was added. What is the most appropriate immediate medication adjustment?
options: Stop the thiazide and recheck the lithium level, coordinating with psychiatry
Add an NSAID to control his blood pressure as initial therapy
Double the lisinopril dose as initial therapy
Continue the thiazide and increase the lithium dose as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Thiazides cause volume contraction that enhances proximal sodium and lithium reabsorption, raising lithium levels and toxicity risk—exactly what happened here. The thiazide should be stopped and the lithium level rechecked, with psychiatry involved before any dose changes. Increasing the lithium dose while on an interacting diuretic would worsen toxicity. NSAIDs also reduce renal lithium clearance and are nephrotoxic, so they are contraindicated. Doubling lisinopril does not address the thiazide-lithium interaction and ACE inhibitors themselves can modestly raise lithium levels; the targeted fix is to remove the thiazide. Because his level (1.4 mEq/L) is at the upper limit, he should also be screened for symptoms of toxicity—worsening tremor, ataxia, or confusion—and counseled to maintain hydration, since volume depletion from any cause will again concentrate his lithium.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After discussing risks, what is the most appropriate long-term strategy for his lithium-related kidney disease?
options: Coordinate with psychiatry to weigh continuing the lowest effective lithium dose vs. switching agents, monitor renal function/levels,
Begin amiloride and continue lithium without any renal monitoring as the immediate next step
Abruptly stop lithium today regardless of psychiatric stability as the immediate next step
Refer directly for kidney transplantation as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Management requires a shared decision with psychiatry: in someone stable for nearly two decades, abruptly stopping lithium risks severe mood relapse, so the team weighs continuing the lowest effective dose versus switching to an alternative mood stabilizer, with regular monitoring of renal function and lithium levels and attention to hydration. Abruptly stopping lithium without a plan can precipitate dangerous relapse. Amiloride can blunt lithium-induced nephrogenic DI by blocking ENaC-mediated lithium entry, but it must be paired with ongoing renal monitoring—omitting monitoring is inappropriate. Transplant referral is premature at an eGFR of 44.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which adjunct can specifically reduce lithium-induced nephrogenic diabetes insipidus by limiting lithium entry into collecting duct cells?
options: Amiloride
Furosemide as the leading concern
Acetazolamide in this presentation
Spironolactone
stages.explanation: Amiloride blocks the epithelial sodium channel (ENaC) in the principal cells of the collecting duct, the same channel through which lithium enters these cells; by reducing intracellular lithium accumulation it can attenuate lithium-induced nephrogenic DI. Furosemide acts at the thick ascending limb and does not specifically block lithium uptake; loop diuretics can also worsen volume depletion. Acetazolamide is a proximal carbonic anhydrase inhibitor and is not a targeted therapy for lithium NDI. Spironolactone antagonizes the mineralocorticoid receptor rather than directly blocking ENaC-mediated lithium entry and is not the preferred agent for this purpose.

cases.diagnosis: Chronic lithium-induced nephropathy with nephrogenic diabetes insipidus and renal microcysts
pearls: Chronic lithium therapy causes nephrogenic DI (aquaporin-2 downregulation) and chronic tubulointerstitial disease; renal microcysts on imaging are a characteristic clue.
Thiazides and NSAIDs reduce renal lithium clearance and can precipitate toxicity—review interacting drugs whenever a lithium level rises.
Manage with psychiatry: weigh lowest effective dose vs. switching agents; abrupt cessation risks mood relapse.
Amiloride blocks ENaC-mediated lithium entry into collecting duct cells and can mitigate lithium-induced nephrogenic DI.

cases.title: Rising Creatinine During a Long Antibiotic Course
cases.chiefComplaint: His kidney function is worsening on hospital day 9 while being treated for a serious infection.
cases.history: A 67-year-old man is hospitalized for a complicated intra-abdominal infection after diverticular perforation, managed with source control and broad-spectrum antibiotics. Because of a resistant gram-negative organism, he has received IV gentamicin dosed for synergy/coverage for the past 8 days in addition to a beta-lactam. His past history includes type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and prior tobacco use. Home medications: metformin (held in hospital), lisinopril (held), aspirin 81 mg. Allergies: penicillin causes rash (tolerating current beta-lactam). On hospital day 9 the team notes his creatinine has risen progressively. He has continued to make good urine—roughly 1.6 liters over the last 24 hours—and denies dysuria or flank pain. He has had no hypotensive episodes and no contrast administration. Social: lives with spouse, retired mechanic. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems: mild nausea, no rash, no new edema. Nursing notes good oral intake and no diarrhea.
cases.exam: He is comfortable and afebrile. Cardiovascular exam is regular without murmurs; JVP is normal and capillary refill is brisk. Lungs are clear. The abdomen is soft with a healing surgical incision, mild expected tenderness, no rebound or guarding, and active bowel sounds. There is no flank tenderness. Skin is warm and well-perfused with no rash. Mucous membranes are moist; he appears euvolemic. Neurologic exam is nonfocal without tetany or carpopedal spasm.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given a rising creatinine in an antibiotic-treated patient who is making good urine, which finding would best support aminoglycoside-induced acute tubular necrosis?
options: Eosinophiluria with a maculopapular rash and fever in this clinical context
Muddy-brown granular casts with renal tubular epithelial cells
RBC casts and dysmorphic red cells as the first step today
Numerous WBC casts with positive urine culture as the first step today
stages.explanation: Aminoglycoside nephrotoxicity is a proximal-tubule injury producing non-oliguric ATN; the urine shows muddy-brown granular casts and renal tubular epithelial cells while urine output is preserved—matching this patient. Eosinophiluria with rash and fever suggests acute interstitial nephritis, a different mechanism. RBC casts and dysmorphic RBCs indicate glomerulonephritis, which aminoglycosides do not cause. Numerous WBC casts with a positive culture point to pyelonephritis/infection, not direct tubular toxicity; he has no urinary symptoms and the time course fits drug-induced ATN. The key clue is that the creatinine rose after roughly a week of therapy in a hemodynamically stable, well-perfused patient with preserved urine output—classic for the cumulative proximal-tubular toxicity of aminoglycosides rather than an abrupt ischemic or obstructive insult.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Creatinine 2.6 mg/dL (admission 1.0); BUN 34 mg/dL
Urine output 1.6 L/24 h (non-oliguric)
Urinalysis: specific gravity 1.010; trace protein; no blood; no nitrites; renal tubular epithelial cells and muddy-brown granular casts present
FENa 2.8%
Magnesium 1.1 mg/dL (low)
Potassium 3.3 mEq/L; calcium 8.6 mg/dL
Gentamicin trough 3.2 µg/mL (elevated)
Renal ultrasound: normal-sized kidneys, no hydronephrosis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which electrolyte abnormality is most characteristically caused by aminoglycoside tubular injury and explains part of his lab panel?
options: Hyperphosphatemia from proximal tubule sparing in this clinical context
Hypermagnesemia as the primary diagnosis
Renal magnesium wasting causing hypomagnesemia
Hyperkalemia from tubular potassium retention in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Aminoglycosides injure the proximal tubule and the thick ascending limb, causing renal magnesium wasting and hypomagnesemia; the low magnesium can in turn drive refractory hypokalemia and hypocalcemia—consistent with his Mg 1.1, K 3.3, and low-normal calcium. Hypermagnesemia is the opposite of the expected derangement. Aminoglycoside ATN does not cause potassium retention/hyperkalemia; if anything it promotes kaliuresis. Hyperphosphatemia from 'proximal sparing' is not characteristic; the proximal tubule is the primary site of toxicity, not spared. A practical point: his hypokalemia will be refractory to potassium replacement until the magnesium is corrected, because hypomagnesemia removes inhibition of the ROMK channel and drives ongoing renal potassium loss—so magnesium must be repleted first.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate management step?
options: Start high-dose corticosteroids for interstitial nephritis as initial therapy
Discontinue gentamicin , maintain euvolemia, repair magnesium
Begin urgent hemodialysis as initial therapy
Continue gentamicin at the same dose and add furosemide as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The key intervention is to stop the offending aminoglycoside and choose a culture-directed, non-nephrotoxic alternative, while maintaining euvolemia, replacing magnesium (which is also needed to correct the hypokalemia), and avoiding additional nephrotoxins and contrast. Continuing gentamicin with an elevated trough perpetuates injury, and loop diuretics do not treat ATN and can worsen magnesium/potassium losses. Corticosteroids treat allergic interstitial nephritis, which is not the mechanism here (no rash/eosinophiluria; casts indicate ATN). He is non-oliguric, hemodynamically stable, and without dialysis indications (no refractory hyperkalemia, acidosis, volume overload, or uremia), so urgent dialysis is not warranted. Dose adjustment of his other renally cleared medications and a daily review for additional nephrotoxins round out appropriate supportive care.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which counseling point about his renal recovery and future antibiotic dosing is most accurate?
options: He should never receive intravenous fluids again with appropriate follow-up
Magnesium does not need to be replaced because it will normalize on its own immediately with appropriate follow-up
Non-oliguric aminoglycoside ATN typically recovers over days to weeks after the drug is stopped
Aminoglycoside ATN is usually irreversible and progresses to dialysis with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Non-oliguric aminoglycoside ATN generally has a favorable prognosis and recovers over days to weeks once the drug is discontinued and supportive care is provided; if aminoglycosides are ever needed again, use weight-based (often extended-interval) dosing with drug-level monitoring and renal-function adjustment to minimize toxicity. The toxicity is usually reversible, not invariably progressive. Avoiding all IV fluids is wrong—euvolemia is part of supportive care. The magnesium wasting can persist for weeks and frequently requires active repletion; it should not be assumed to self-correct, especially since it perpetuates his hypokalemia. He should leave with documentation of the aminoglycoside exposure and the resulting ATN so future prescribers avoid unnecessary nephrotoxins and, if an aminoglycoside is truly required, use extended-interval dosing with level-guided adjustment.

cases.diagnosis: Aminoglycoside (gentamicin) nephrotoxicity—non-oliguric acute tubular necrosis with hypomagnesemia
pearls: Aminoglycoside nephrotoxicity is classically non-oliguric ATN appearing after 5-10 days of therapy, with muddy-brown granular casts and an elevated FENa.
Proximal-tubule and thick-ascending-limb injury causes renal magnesium wasting; resulting hypomagnesemia drives refractory hypokalemia and hypocalcemia.
Management: stop the drug, choose a non-nephrotoxic alternative, maintain euvolemia, replete magnesium/potassium, and avoid other nephrotoxins.
Reduce risk with weight-based extended-interval dosing, trough/level monitoring, and renal dose adjustment; non-oliguric ATN usually recovers in days to weeks.

cases.title: Confusion and a Sudden Drop in Urine After Starting IV Treatment
cases.chiefComplaint: His creatinine spiked the day after receiving intravenous antiviral therapy.
cases.history: A 45-year-old man with poorly controlled HIV (recent CD4 ~120) is admitted with disseminated herpes zoster and presumed varicella-zoster meningoencephalitis. He was started on high-dose IV acyclovir (10 mg/kg every 8 hours) dosed for his weight, and the first several doses were infused rapidly over 30 minutes. Hydration orders were minimal, and he had poor oral intake from nausea. He has no prior kidney disease. Past history: HIV on intermittently adherent antiretroviral therapy, prior latent TB treated. Medications on admission: tenofovir-based ART (recently restarted), trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole prophylaxis, and now IV acyclovir. Allergies: none. Social: former IV drug use, now in recovery; nonsmoker. Family history: noncontributory. On hospital day 2 the team is called because his urine output has dropped and morning labs show a sharply higher creatinine. He reports mild flank discomfort and continued nausea but no gross hematuria. He has not received iodinated contrast.
cases.exam: He is uncomfortable and mildly somnolent but arousable, consistent with his CNS infection. Mucous membranes are dry and skin turgor is reduced. Cardiovascular exam is regular and tachycardic without murmurs; JVP is low. Lungs are clear. Abdomen is soft with mild bilateral flank discomfort to deep palpation; no rebound. There is a healing dermatomal vesicular rash on the trunk. No peripheral edema. Neurologic exam shows mild confusion attributable to his encephalitis without new focal deficits.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most likely mechanism of his acute kidney injury, and what is the best first diagnostic step?
options: Crystal-induced AKI from acyclovir
Prerenal azotemia alone—no urine studies needed, just give fluids as the first step today
Acute interstitial nephritis from sulfamethoxazole—start steroids empirically in this clinical context
Obstructive uropathy—place a urethral catheter and obtain a CT stone protocol first
stages.explanation: Rapidly infused, high-dose IV acyclovir in a volume-depleted patient precipitates as needle-shaped crystals within the tubular lumen, causing crystal-induced (intratubular obstructive) AKI. The best first step is urinalysis with microscopy to look for the characteristic birefringent needle-shaped crystals, alongside an assessment of volume status. Pure prerenal azotemia is plausible given his dehydration but does not explain the abrupt rise tied to acyclovir dosing, and skipping urine studies misses the crystals. Empiric steroids for AIN are premature without supporting evidence. Obstructive uropathy from a stone is much less likely than drug crystalluria given the temporal link to acyclovir.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Creatinine 3.1 mg/dL (admission 0.9); BUN 40 mg/dL
Urine output: 0.4 mL/kg/h over the last 8 hours
Urinalysis: specific gravity 1.020; pH 6.0; trace protein; no nitrites
Urine microscopy: numerous needle-shaped, birefringent crystals; few renal tubular epithelial cells; no RBC casts
Urine eosinophils: not detected
FENa 0.9%
Renal ultrasound: normal-sized kidneys, no hydronephrosis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which urinary finding most specifically confirms acyclovir crystal nephropathy?
options: Hexagonal cystine crystals as the primary diagnosis
Muddy-brown granular casts as the primary diagnosis
Dysmorphic RBCs and RBC casts as the primary diagnosis
Needle-shaped birefringent crystals on urine microscopy
stages.explanation: Acyclovir crystal nephropathy is identified by needle-shaped, birefringent crystals on urine microscopy in the right clinical context (recent high-dose IV acyclovir, volume depletion). Muddy-brown granular casts indicate ischemic/nephrotoxic ATN, not crystal disease. Dysmorphic RBCs and RBC casts indicate glomerulonephritis, which acyclovir does not cause. Hexagonal crystals are pathognomonic of cystinuria, an inherited stone disease unrelated to acyclovir. The needle-shaped crystals, plus the temporal link to rapid high-dose acyclovir dosing and volume depletion, clinch the diagnosis. Other drugs that cause crystalline AKI worth recognizing include sulfadiazine, indinavir, methotrexate, and triamterene, while ethylene glycol produces calcium oxalate crystals—each tied to its own clinical context.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Continue rapid acyclovir infusions and restrict fluids to avoid edema as initial therapy
Switch to oral acyclovir at the same total dose immediately as initial therapy
Hold/reduce acyclovir, give aggressive isotonic IV fluids to establish high urine flow,
Start emergent hemodialysis as first-line therapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Crystal nephropathy is treated by stopping or reducing the acyclovir and giving aggressive isotonic intravenous fluids to establish high urine flow, which dissolves and flushes the intratubular crystals; future doses should be infused slowly (over at least 1-2 hours) with renal-function-based dose adjustment. Continuing rapid infusions while restricting fluids worsens crystal precipitation. Hemodialysis is reserved for severe AKI with standard indications, not first-line for crystal nephropathy that usually responds to hydration. Simply switching to oral acyclovir at the same dose does not address the ongoing AKI and his severe CNS infection requires effective, safely dosed therapy.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: His AKI is improving with hydration, but he still needs treatment for VZV encephalitis. What is the best way to continue antiviral therapy?
options: Give a single large bolus of acyclovir daily to simplify dosing as the immediate next step
Resume acyclovir with renal dose adjustment, slow infusion over 1-2 hours,
Switch to high-dose oral valacyclovir for the encephalitis as the immediate next step
Stop all antiviral therapy until creatinine is completely normal as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: VZV encephalitis is life-threatening and requires effective IV therapy, so acyclovir should be resumed with renal dose adjustment, infused slowly over 1-2 hours, alongside continued IV hydration and close monitoring of creatinine and urine output to prevent recurrent crystallization. Stopping all therapy risks fatal progression of CNS infection. A large daily bolus would raise peak tubular concentrations and promote crystal precipitation—the opposite of what is needed. Oral valacyclovir does not achieve adequate CNS levels for encephalitis and is inappropriate for this indication. Renal dosing in acyclovir therapy is based on the calculated creatinine clearance, and the infusion should run over at least an hour; pairing this with pre- and intra-infusion isotonic fluids keeps urine flow brisk and prevents recurrent crystallization.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which preventive measure most directly reduces the risk of recurrent acyclovir crystal nephropathy?
options: Concentrating the urine by restricting fluids overnight
Ensuring adequate hydration
Adding an NSAID to reduce flank pain as the leading concern
Alkalinizing the urine with high-dose vitamin C as the leading concern
stages.explanation: The most direct prevention is maintaining adequate hydration to keep urine dilute and flow high, infusing acyclovir slowly, and dosing by weight and renal function so peak tubular drug concentrations stay below the precipitation threshold. Vitamin C acidifies urine and can promote oxalate formation; it does not prevent acyclovir crystals (acyclovir solubility is not improved by acidification). NSAIDs are nephrotoxic and would worsen renal perfusion by reducing prostaglandin-mediated afferent arteriolar vasodilation. Restricting fluids concentrates the urine and promotes crystal formation—exactly the wrong approach. In short, the three levers that prevent recurrence are volume status, infusion rate, and dose: keep him well hydrated, infuse slowly, and dose to his current renal function with ongoing creatinine monitoring.

cases.diagnosis: Acyclovir crystal-induced acute kidney injury (intratubular crystal nephropathy)
pearls: Rapid high-dose IV acyclovir in a volume-depleted patient precipitates needle-shaped, birefringent crystals causing intratubular obstructive AKI.
Diagnose with urine microscopy showing needle-shaped crystals; the FENa can be low early because of the obstructive/prerenal component.
Treat by holding/reducing the drug and giving aggressive isotonic fluids to establish high urine flow; AKI is usually reversible.
Prevent recurrence with adequate hydration, slow infusion over 1-2 hours, and weight- and renal-function-based dosing.

cases.title: Found Drunk and Breathing Hard with a Confusing Acidosis
cases.chiefComplaint: He was brought in confused and intoxicated-appearing with rapid breathing.
cases.history: A 36-year-old man is brought to the ED by his roommate, found confused, slurring speech, and 'acting drunk' over the past several hours, but the roommate insists he had no access to alcohol today. The patient is too altered to give a reliable history. The roommate reports the patient has struggled with depression and was recently distraught after a breakup; an empty container of automobile antifreeze was found near him in the garage. There is no reported seizure, but he has become progressively more lethargic with deep, rapid breathing. Past history: depression, prior alcohol use disorder. Medications: unknown adherence to an SSRI. Allergies: none known. Social: lives with one roommate, history of binge drinking. Family history: noncontributory. The roommate denies that the patient ingested any other medications or rubbing alcohol, and there is no fever or trauma reported. Time of ingestion is uncertain but likely within the last several hours.
cases.exam: He is lethargic but arousable to voice, with slurred speech and nystagmus; he appears intoxicated though no alcohol odor is detected on his breath. Respirations are deep and rapid (Kussmaul). Cardiovascular exam is tachycardic and regular without murmurs. Lungs are clear. Abdomen is soft and nontender without organomegaly. There is no focal neurologic deficit, but he is ataxic when stimulated. There is mild flank discomfort to palpation. No tetany is noted, though he reports tingling. Fundoscopic exam is unremarkable, without papilledema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which initial set of studies will most quickly characterize this toxic ingestion?
options: CT head only as the first step today
Blood alcohol level only as the first step today
Arterial blood gas, basic metabolic panel ,
Urine drug screen only as the first step today
stages.explanation: A suspected toxic alcohol ingestion is rapidly characterized by an ABG and basic metabolic panel to compute the anion gap, a measured serum osmolality to calculate the osmolar gap, and a lactate level. Early ethylene glycol can produce a high osmolar gap before the anion gap rises; as it is metabolized to glycolic and oxalic acids the high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis develops. A CT head evaluates structural causes of altered mentation but won't reveal the metabolic derangement. A blood alcohol level alone misses toxic alcohols. A urine drug screen does not detect ethylene glycol and would not explain the acidosis.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
ABG: pH 7.18; pCO2 22 mmHg; HCO3 8 mEq/L
Sodium 140 mEq/L; chloride 102 mEq/L; bicarbonate 8 mEq/L; potassium 4.2 mEq/L
Anion gap = 30 (elevated)
Glucose 96 mg/dL; lactate 2.0 mmol/L (only mildly elevated)
Measured serum osmolality 330 mOsm/kg; calculated osmolality 295 mOsm/kg → osmolar gap 35
Ethanol level: undetectable
Serum creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (rising); calcium 7.6 mg/dL (low)
Urinalysis: pH 5.5; envelope-shaped (and needle-shaped) crystals; calcium oxalate crystals seen; urine fluoresces under Wood lamp

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis with a large osmolar gap, hypocalcemia, and calcium oxalate crystalluria is most consistent with which poisoning?
options: Methanol poisoning
Salicylate toxicity
Ethylene glycol poisoning
Diabetic ketoacidosis
stages.explanation: Ethylene glycol poisoning produces a high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis with an elevated osmolar gap; its metabolite oxalic acid chelates calcium, causing hypocalcemia and calcium oxalate crystalluria (envelope-shaped and needle-shaped crystals) with AKI. Methanol also raises both gaps but classically causes visual disturbance/optic injury and lacks oxalate crystals/hypocalcemia. DKA causes a high anion gap from ketones with hyperglycemia and only a modest osmolar gap—his glucose is normal. Salicylate toxicity causes a mixed respiratory alkalosis with high-anion-gap acidosis and tinnitus but not oxalate crystals or a large osmolar gap.
